data.text:
INT: Welcome to sunny Australia. This is Melbourne, April 28, Monday, interview with Mark Verstandig in English, interviewer, Richard Reisend. 

Good morning. Could you please tell us your name? 

MV: Good morning. My name is Mark Verstandig. 

INT: And how do you spell your surname? 

MV: V for Victor, E for Elizabeth, R for Robert, S for Sam, T for Tom, A for Andrew, N for Nellie, D for David, I for Ivan, G for George. You are lucky it's not longer. 

INT: Thank you very much. And the name of your mother? 

MV: My mother's name was Gela, again, Verstandig. My father's name was Verstandig, and my mother's name was Verstandig. They were cousins. Their parents-- their fathers were brothers. The brother-- the father of my mother was Isaac Verstandig-- Yitzhak Verstandig. And the father of my father, my-- my grandfather, was David Verstandig. So they were Verstandigs from both sides. 

INT: And your father's first name? 

MV: My father's first name was Lajzer, Lajzer Verstandig. 

INT: And do you remember or know the names of your other grandparents? 

MV: Yes. It's-- my grandmother on the maternal side was Lana. And my grandmother on the paternal-- on the paternal side was [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: And the maiden name of your paternal grandmother? 

MV: I think the maiden names of my paternal mother was Gross. But I wouldn't be-- I think it was Gross. 

INT: And your maternal grandmother? 

MV: I don't remember. 

INT: Thank you. And did you have any brothers and sisters? 

MV: Yes, I have-- I had one brother, Solomon. And I had five sisters. The oldest is [NON-ENGLISH]. She was called the name [NON-ENGLISH] after my grandmother. And the-- she was the oldest child. Then there was my brother Solomon. He was a twin-- he had-- they were twins, but the other did not survive. Then there was my sister Golda. And later sister Sucha or Sarah, Rachel, or Rucha, Berta or [NON-ENGLISH] And we were, all together, seven children. 

As a matter of fact, there were more children. I looked up once the book in the city council. It was-- I think this was the book which kept by a special official who kept all kinds of old-- he kept all kinds of names and people born and died and so on, for-- for the Jewish population. And I could see there, there were some other children, at least two. And-- but they did not survive. They died in childhood or you were-- and they were a few days old when they died. 

INT: Of the surviving children in childhood, for historical purposes, could you give us the approximate birth dates, including your own, going from oldest to youngest. 

MV: Yes, I think my older sister was born about 1901. My brother was born in 1903. Golda was born a couple of years later, which would have been '05, 1905. Sucha, 1906, Rucha, 1908, and Berta or [NON-ENGLISH] was 1910. And I was in 1912. 

INT: Thank you. Which town did you grow up in? 

MV: I grew up in a town called Mielec. 

INT: Could you tell us where it was, which part of the country? 

MV: That was in Galicia. Officially it was the western part of Galicia. But it was really the central part of Galicia because it was halfway between Cracow and Lwow or Lemberg, how it was known in the time of the-- in the time of the Austrian-- of the Austrian Empire, Austro-Hungarian Empire. so Mielec was just in the middle. And consequently, we could officially belong to the-- to Cracow, to-- it would-- it would-- it would be officially belongs to the western part of Galicia, whose capital was Cracow. But really it was in the middle. Therefore, we could say it was the central part of Galicia. 

INT: What sort of town was it? What was its main activity or industry, if you like? 

MV: My time, there was no industry. It was a village, about 10,000 people, about 5,000 Jews and about 5,000 Gentiles, Poles-- the Gentiles-- Christians. And mostly there was no industry in that-- at that time of my childhood. There were a few, a very few artisans, tailors, shoemakers, and a few others like cabinetmakers, but no industry as such. They were only people around who were making-- who were making-- who-- who-- who works for the-- for-- for other-- for other people in town, let's say a custom tailor or a cabinetmaker, who was not making for any export or for any-- or for any shops, only somebody ordered a-- a cupboard or somebody ordered a bedroom and so on and so on. 

It's a local industry. There was no industry in town. There were-- people had shops, the shopkeepers. And around the town were a lot of villages. I would say with the villages around the town, it would have been about 18,000 people around or-- or even more. 

And once a week they used to come to town, where they used to buy from the shops whatever they needed. There were some ironmongers. They brought iron for their purposes in the farms. And from our hands, and from people selling cigarettes, they-- or tobacco. They bought tobacco. They bought everything in town once a week. So once a week, the town was full. The marketplace, which was the central square, was full of carts and horses. 

And the farmers, the small farmers, they call them in Poland-- in Poland they call themselves peasants, the Party of Peasants. It's not a derogatory expression, but this is how they called themselves. Here we would say they were small farmers. 

So they bought all their needs from the shops all around the town. Most of the shops, I would say about 80%, 90% of the shops were in Jewish hands. 

INT: Which day of the week was this? 

MV: That was on Thursday. It was the market day. 

INT: And what was the main activity or livelihood of the Gentile population? 

MV: The Gentile population is really is-- the Gentile population, first of all, supplied public servants, the city council and then all other kinds of offices. There were the-- there were the-- the Polish-Christian population were occupying all those-- all those posts. 

Besides this, there was a kind of an industry, of an-- of-- namely, there were people or Poles who used to buy pigs around the town, around the villages. The pigs were slaughtered, and they exported bacon to England. So that was really-- that was really the only industry there. We called them [NON-ENGLISH]. They were-- they were buying pigs, killing them, processing them, and selling, and exporting to-- and exporting to England. 

By the way, there were other people, Jews, who were exporting feathers from from geese, chickens. There were-- there were quite a number of people employed. It's called flicking the feathers and cleaning the feathers and exporting them to France. There was a well-known family called [NON-ENGLISH], and they were quite well off. And they were exporters of this kind of-- of products. 

INT: Were there other small farms, except for pig farms, right outside the town? 

MV: There were farms. It was not only pig farm. There were no special pig farms in Poland. In Poland, farms were mixed farms, small-- small holdings, very small holdings. No, you mainly found the small farmers, very small holdings, and they had everything and nothing. 

They had a cow, two cows at the most. And they had the pigsty, where they used-- where they used to have-- where they used to breed pigs. But besides this-- besides this, there was no other industry, no other industry around the town or around the villages. 

INT: What was the main transport in Mielec? In other words, was there a railway station close by? 

MV: If there was a railway station? Yes, there was a railway station. There was a railway station about two kilometers away from-- about two kilometers away from the main square. And this railway station was built just before the First World War. And the story goes that on-- on Sabbath afternoon, Jews used to gather in the-- the main square and talk politics, talk whatever they like to. And they used to go the two kilometers to see how the railway is proceeding. 

Now there was one Jew, who was an invalid, a disabled man, and he could not walk the two kilometers. So-- and one day, one Saturday, he asked them, tell me. You are going every Saturday to this railway. What is a railway? He said, don't you know what's a railway? It's a big cart. 

You board the cart in the evening, after the evening prayers. And in the morning, before the morning players, you are in Vienna. So he said, but what's the hurry? That was the atmosphere in this small town. What is the hurry to go to Vienna in eight hours or in 10 hours? 

INT: And where does this railway station communicate to? Was it-- 

MV: This railway station took to-- went from here. And one side was to the east. It went right to Przemysl and to Lwow. But to the west, you have-- it-- it-- it-- it took us to the-- to a town called [NON-ENGLISH]. And there you had to catch another train to Cracow because there were other trains going direct Cracow-- Cracow and Lwow without Mielec. 

INT: Were there any such things as buses before the war? 

MV: Yeah, there were buses too. But not-- not-- not when I was a child, but when I was adolescent, there were buses. And the buses were taking people mostly from Mielec to Tarnow. Tarnow was a larger town. And people used to travel once a day. The bus took people to Tarnow and-- and back to Mielec. It's about 40, 45 kilometers, 40, 45 kilometers. There was a bus. 

INT: And around the town itself, in its-- on area. 

MV: Around that town there were no taxis. There were horse carts. And mainly they took people to the railway station. Just before the war, a couple of years before the war, the first taxi was introduced. And the son of one of these-- of-- of-- of this-- of the owners of such horse carts-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- he bought a taxi. And he was a first taxi driver. And a that time, he was also a mechanic-- a taxi driver and a mechanic. 

There were a few cars, a few motor cars in town. One belonged to a Jew, who had a flour mill. But the few others were-- belonged-- belonged to Christians. There was one, a count, Count Bolski, who had a-- who-- who had a taxi-- who had a car, a motor car. 

There was a fellow who was deaf and dumb and spoke French and German fluently through the-- through the throat. He didn't need-- he did not need a-- he did not need a translator when he-- when-- when he was [INAUDIBLE]. He was my client. 

INT: Going back to the time of your early childhood, let's say the early '20s, when you just began your schooling, can we just have a-- a bit of a picture of the standard of life in Mielec? So, were there any telephones? 

MV: Yeah, there were a few telephones. My father had a telephone. Yes, and-- 

INT: But were they common for the people? 

MV: Yeah, there were-- there were big-- they were big fixtures on the wall. And you had to-- you had-- you had to contact the local post office and ask the local post office to connect you with another number in town or with another number in another town. It [INAUDIBLE] another, even in town. 

In town it didn't take long, and you could get a-- you could get a connection. But to get-- to get a connection to Cracow, it took quite a bit of time. 

INT: What about electricity? 

MV: In my time, when I was a child, there was no electricity. But later on, it could-- there could have been about, maybe, when I was 10 years old. I think so. An ex-- a-- a-- a Pole, a Christian Pole came back from the United States. His name was Romanovsky. And he brought from the United States a generator. And he got the license to supply electricity through town. A lot of people he employed, and they went out and, like, put these wires and-- not like here though. The wire-- like here, but around the houses. 

And I was waiting for this moment. I remember, I was waiting for this moment when the electricity will-- will start-- will start working because they had these big lamps in the marketplace. And our house was in the market-- in the main square. And I thought maybe electricity that the-- that the light from lamp will be so big, so-- it will-- so bright. Let's say that we will be able to read a book inside from the light which is going to come from-- from the light in-- in the-- in-- in the square. 

And so my imagination about-- about the strengths, about the brightness of the electricity-- of the electricity. That was when electricity was introduced in town. Some years later, some years later, there was like a state electricity commission. And they bought out his license. And-- but in the villages, there was no electricity, only in town, only in the town Mielec was electricity. But in the villages, there was no electricity. 

INT: What about-- 

MV: Well, there were kerosene lamps for the night-- candles and kerosene lamps. 

INT: And gas? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: Was there any gas at all? Was there any gas? 

MV: No, no, no, no, no gas at all. No gas at all. 

INT: And-- 

MV: The gas was in big towns, but not in Mielec, no. No, not in Mielec, not in the villages. I'm not sure if they sells gas even now in the villages. I'm not sure about. 

INT: And what about radios? 

MV: Radios? Yes, there were radios. There were radios. We had a radio. But not when I was a child. That was later. They're [INAUDIBLE] 

INT: And going back to that time when you were a-- a young boy starting school, what was your impression about the relationships between the Gentiles and the Jewish people? 

MV: I had otherwise impression that the relationship was good. Although, my first memories after, when I was six-year-old and-- and Poland was established in November, there was a pogrom. And the small-- the farmers, the peasants came to town and looted. 

Nobody-- we could have-- you could have been beaten up. But there was no killing, nothing. I-- my impression was that that was the work of agitators, that the people were good people. A Jew could walk from one-- from one end of the-- of-- of the region to the other. And there were such ones, peddlers. They would bring a few chuks to sell them in town. And they were dressed like you see here, the Jews in-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. 

No one would do any-- would do any harm to them. That relationship was good. Whenever there was something, it was done by agitators. And the people were not intelligent people. They were-- they were easy-- easy stuff to be incited. 

And that was that. I mean, there was-- the relation was very good. 

INT: What was your father's profession? Or how did he make his income? 

MV: My father, with-- with his two brothers, they were entrepreneurs before the end of the century. They used to work in Germany. And they used to contract from so-called Junkers. You say the Junkers in-- they were this-- the feudal lords in Germany. They were called Junkers, the rich landowners. They used to contract from them harvesting their-- the-- their land, their crops. 

And for that purpose, they organized labor in Galicia and took them to Germany. They paid the laborers. And after the harvest, they brought them back. So it was a migrating labor force, which they used in order to-- in order to harvest the land owned-- the-- the-- the land-- the estates in Germany. And that is-- that was done by my-- by my father and his two brothers. Later, by the end of the century beginning of the 20th century, they went to Sweden. And they went to Sweden, and over there in a town called Bromolla. 

They contacted the production of porcelain, of cutlery, no, crockery, of crockery. Again, they brought, this time they brought the workers from Poland. They had to build for them barracks and houses in Bromolla because they were really people not for a harvesting, but there were permanent workers. They had to build for them houses. They had to bring their families with them because they were staying there. But they were contracted and [INAUDIBLE] they were contacting the production. 

As a matter of fact, Bromolla, the city council of Bromolla issued-- published a-- published a book about in the same Bromolla. And there you could see a picture of the two brothers. I've got-- I've got this picture here. If you'll remind me later, I will show it to you. I'll find it. 

So you see, the two brothers since they called them the two-- the three brethren, the three brethren Verstandig, who-- who built up is industry in-- in the town of Bromolla. Now, later, But my father wasn't-- my father was in-- in-- in-- in Sweden only occasionally. The two brothers stayed in Sweden longer, for almost all the time. And my father built a house in Mielec. He came from Sokolov to Mielec, and there he built the house, a beautiful house in the main square. 

It was the only house in town with running water. We had in the courtyard-- in the courtyard, there was a-- a well. And it was a three-story. So on top, behind the roof-- behind the roof-- underneath the roof, was a large water tank. As water was pumped from this well to this tank-- and from the tank it was distributed to the-- to-- to the first-- to the first floor and to the-- and to the ground floor. So it was a two-- the only house in Mielic with running water and with WC, with-- with toilets, with modern toilets. 

INT: What year was that? 

MV: Beg your pardon. 

INT: What year did your father build that house? 

MV: No, father built the house in 1903, because I-- I know exactly because there was a little flag on top of the roof, which said 1-3-- 1903. So that was at that time. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: Tape two of Mark Verstandig-- please continue your story from the time your father built the house in Mielec. 

MV: Yes, maybe a year later or so, he bought-- he bought a large estate called Sadkowa Gora, 17 kilometers out of-- from Mielec. As a matter of fact, a broker took him over there, [NON-ENGLISH], and he came back and told my wife-- told his wife, I just bought a real-- I just bought an estate-- [NON-ENGLISH]. 

It was interesting that this estate, when it came to transfer the-- came to transfer the title, it came out that there was, on the title, a clause that this estate should never be sold to a Jew. So my father was worried. He went to this-- to the solicitor, a certain Dr. Eisenberg-- was a young man, a solicitor from Vienna. He was a Galiciana, but he studied in Vienna. 

And I mention his name because he-- in-- I mean, later I'll be talking about him, too, during the war and before the war. So this Dr. Eisenberg said, you shouldn't worry. I will fix it. This clause is contra bonos mores. It's against good behavior. And under-- under the OZN Constitution, it is invalid. It must have been-- it must have come at a time when Jews, after the emancipation, were allowed to buy and lend. 

And then, an antisemite was the owner, put it into-- made the clause that this should never be sold to a Jew. But it is not valid anymore according to the OZN Constitution, and it will be transferred. And this is what happened-- it was transferred to my father. 

You will see later, my-- my daughter brought you-- brought these pictures of the manor, of the house, of the manor, which she visited-- which she visited when she was in Poland a few years ago. 

And so he became a landowner. He knew farming-- because his father was a farmer, so he knew farming, and he liked farming. So really, farming was his profession. And besides this, he made money in Sweden. 

And this was going on until the First World War. After-- after the First World War, he was still farming, he was farming, and he had other businesses, just the same. So he had a wholesale business selling spirits-- 96-proof spirits, in big barrels. And from these big barrels, the wholesale-- wholesale to-- to the wholesale business. They used to sell to shops. Shops used to come to buy spirits, to buy vodka. 

That was-- for that purpose, one must have had a license. How he got his license, I don't know. But he had. It was a license-- it was a licensed business, a profitable one, because it was a monopoly. And then he had the monopoly selling tobacco. I don't know how he got this license. 

And in this house, which he built, on the ground floor were two shops. One shop was a-- a retail tobacco shop. And the other was a clothing shop. The clothing shop was in the hands of my aunt, Vocha Ostrow-- Vocha and Abraham Ostrow. 

And the tobacco was being-- tobacco cigarettes were-- were sold by a Polish invalid after the First World War, after the First World War. It was a Polish invalid who had a license. And that was a gold mine. 

The peasants used to come to town, and there used to be not queues, but crowds of people trying to get into this shop. Not-- I'm not telling you joking. There were crowds of people pushing themselves into this shop. It was a very good shop. 

Now, one day, one day he died. And his widow came to my father. And she wanted to sell the license. Anyhow, my father paid her some money and took over this shop-- a retail shop. At that time, he was a rash hakahal. That means the president of the Jewish community. 

The office of the Jewish community was on the first floor of our house, it was a large house. There were some two rooms for his office. And when there was a meeting, the meeting used to be in the dining room, our dining room, the meetings of the councilors, of the Jewish councilors. 

And downstairs, I mean, on the-- on the ground floor was the shop. And he thought that he can run the shop. Now, he didn't have a clue how to run a shop, how to run business, how to run a retail business whatsoever. And after a short time, he had to get-- yeah, besides this, he employed-- he employed an invalid, again. He employed one of these invalids, in order-- in order to validate the transfer of license. With the license, he had to make some agreements with the union of the invalids. 

And so he put in somebody there. Anyhow, after a quite, maybe a-- a couple of-- a couple of years, three years, and so on-- a few years, this gold mine turned into-- into a-- into a pit-- into a pit of losses. He-- he had no idea how to run a shop, and he gave it away. He gave it away to the invalids. 

And that was the business of my father. He was-- he was a landowner, a farmer, and he never-- he never had any trouble with money. But then, about '20-- maybe '24, '25, 1924, 1924-- you see, my brother had to-- he was-- was 20 years-- '24, 21 years old, and he was conscripted. And that was a catastrophe. 

Normally, Jewish sons tried not to get into the army, because the army was antisemitic. The officers, and the-- the sergeants, the corporals, they all were-- very antisemitic. And besides this, there was the dietary laws were-- were not in-- were not in conformity with Jewish law. You see, you couldn't-- you couldn't eat kosher. 

And my father and my mother were orthodox people. And so were many, many-- most of the Jews. So normally, they tried to get out of the army. 

At about the time when the commission deciding on conscription was sitting for a few weeks in town. And all people reaching 21 years had to-- had to undergo an inspection, it they were able to go to the army. 

Now, people used to walk around for nights for three months, for four months, trying to get skinny, look sickly. And before standing with the-- before appearing in the-- and a bit-- a few hours, an hour or so before appearing before the commission, they were coming into-- into this room. They used to drink strong coffee-- a coffee with, let us say, 20 times the strength of a normal coffee. So really, you should force yourself to drink a cup of coffee like that. 

But after you drink a few cups of coffee, your heart was beating like you would have-- like you would have run kilometers. So when they came-- when they came to the committee, when they stood before this doctor, you see, their heart was abnormally beating there. But it helped when the doctor got $200 US-- a bribe. And that was a hell of a lot of money. With $200, you could make a living in Poland at that time. 

And see, because the time of inflation, and $200 used to give you 10% per month-- per month-- interest. So with $200, one could make a living out of interest. But with a large amount of money, only rich people, wealthy people, could afford it. 

And so when the doctor got $200, and the heart of the young-- of the young man that was beating, you see, he was freed from-- he was freed from-- from this-- from military service. But military service was really a catastrophe. They were beating up the Jews. And besides this, as I said told you there, it's not so far, but the-- the officer corps was antisemitic. And the other peasants who came to the army were under the influence of the sergeant, of the corporal, and so on. 

And this was the reason that people tried to get out of it. 

INT: How long did it last? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: How long did military service last? 

MV: Two years. It was two years. Now, if can-- it came the time my brother had to-- had to stand before the commission. He was 21 years, he was born in 1903, it was 1924. It was a dilemma, because my father was sitting, was a member of this commission. Not only my father, but when people used to come, let us say, from a-- from a little village called Borowa, the mayor of Borowa or the Soltis used to sit there to identify the people. Otherwise, somebody else could have come. 

There was no-- people-- people didn't have IDs with photographs, like-- like they've got here. So there always was a representative of the community sitting as a-- as a member of this commission. So my father was a member of the commission when Mielec-- when the youngsters of Mielec had to stand before the commission. 

So my father was in a dilemma. Because if my-- if my brother, if his son would be freed from military service, the people would say that the rash hakahal, the president of the Jewish community, fixed it for his son, where others had to go-- poor people had to go to-- to be conscripted. Therefore, my father decided that the best would be to take my brother to a town called Sokolow, because he was born in Sokolow, '93, just before my father moved from Sokolow to Mielec. 

So it was the best-- it was the best he could have-- he could have invented-- an idea that he could take him to Sokolow. And there, the president of the community, the Jewish community in Sokolow was very friendly. Because my father, after the First World War, rebuilt a synagogue in Sokolow. 

The synagogue in Sokolow was burned down during the war. Then when my elder sister, Grincha, came back from Sweden, she is-- she lived in Sweden during the First World War, because it so happens that she went to Sweden, and didn't go back when the war broke out. So she stayed over there, and attended over there-- how it-- attended high school in Sweden. 

So when she came back, she became very ill, very sick. So when she became very sick, my-- my mother decided that she was-- she was making the decisions-- that my father should go to a miracle maker, to a great rabbi in another town, and give him some money that he should pray to the Lord in heaven that she should be-- that she should get out of this sickness. 

And my father went over there and told the rabbi that his daughter is very sick. And gave him an envelope containing some nice bank notes. And the rabbi looked up to heaven and said to him, don't worry, I've got a message for you. Your daughter, Grina, the daughter of Gela and Lajzer, will be healthy, will become healthy, will get out of it. But you have to rebuild the synagogue in Sokolow. The synagogue was burned down. 

So he built the synagogue. So he knew when he'd come to that-- that the president of the community will be very friendly, and will-- he will be helpful. Besides his $200 was-- were prepared to pay it for the doctor. That was the standard bribe. $200 US were prepared. 

In the meantime, it so happened, unfortunately, that when the date-- the day came for my brother to appear before the commission, the doctor became ill, and another one, a stand-in came along. He didn't get any-- any-- any bribe. So he asked-- and my brother didn't have any-- didn't have any coffees, strong coffees before that. 

So he said, this man is all right for the army. So he got a-- a so-called A, which means he has to go to the army. There was nothing what could have been done until he enrolled-- until he was-- until he enrolled to the army. 

But then an offensive started, and bribes were given right and left. And my father didn't have the money, so he had to borrow the money. And that was a time, as I told you, that if you borrowed $100, you had to pay 10% interest per month. And after-- after a few months, anyhow, the commission decided-- the commission, they took him to a hospital, and the commission decided that he is a cripple, that he is not good for anything. 

So they sent him out. But it cost a few thousand dollars. And that was a hell of a lot of money at that time. And that was the first dent in his finances. 

And then came 1929, the Great Depression. In the United States, shareholders threw themselves down from-- from sky-- from-- from the 100-- from the floor-- from the-- from the exchange, from the-- from the stock exchange, or-- or from their own houses. 

In Poland, there were no great shareholders, but there were landowners. And it affects the landowner to such an extent that they became almost bankrupt. Really, it didn't pay to produce. Put it that way. Cart of potatoes, potatoes-- we used to sell potatoes to distillers to make vodka. 100 kilo of potatoes were worth $2, 2 zlotys-- 2 zlotys, which is $0.40. 100 kilo potatoes. It cost more to produce them. It cost more to deliver them. 

So in the end, it came, it was a very, very bad time. And that was my time. That was my time. There was not a penny in the house, not a penny in the house. Because on top of it, a few years earlier-- I think it was in 1926 or '27, '27-- my father decided to improve the estate-- buy new machinery, erect new buildings, new sheds, new buildings, and so on-- especially machinery, agricultural machinery. 

And he got a loan of 100,000 zlotys from the rural bank. That was approximately according to the valuation, 10% of the value of the estate. There was no problem. It was four and a-- it was 4 and 1/2% because they didn't make-- by the way, they didn't make any differentiation between a Jewish landowner and a non-Jewish landowner. Landowners used to get these privileged, these privileged loans for improvements on 4 and 1/2%. This was a gift. 

And later, it was-- just later means two years later, two years later, the whole crop, everything together, didn't suffice to pay the interest in the bank. 4 and 1/2%, and it was not. 

My father was lucky in this respect that before the First World War, when some Poles used to emigrate to the United States, and they have very small holdings-- let's say, an acre of land, two acres of land-- next to our estate, he used to buy it from them. Because they didn't need it anymore, they went to the United States. 

So these little holdings were not encumbered with-- with the loan, with-- with the loan to-- from the-- the rural bank, were not encumbered to the rural bank. So he used to sell yearly, all those small holdings in order to pay the interest. And even that did not suffice, he had to borrow money. 

There was not a penny in the house. And that was when I-- when I was in the high school already, before matriculation. 

INT: Can we go back now to the beginning of your childhood? You mentioned earlier, your parents were orthodox. Was that the main tradition amongst the Jewish people in that area? 

MV: Yeah. The main tradition-- everybody, almost everybody was an-- was orthodox. There were a few Jews here and there who were not orthodox. For instance, there was a new-- that Eisenberg, the-- the barrister, solicitor and barrister who came from Vienna. And what we knew is, he is not orthodox. He used to say that he is eating matzo with ham. But he was still eating matzo. He didn't go to the synagogue. 

But normally, the-- even the-- the assimilated Jews used-- used to go still to the synagogue on Yom Kippur. And there were no so-called professional [INAUDIBLE]. But the mainstream was orthodox. And my father was orthodox. And my mother was ultra orthodox. So she-- that was an obsession. The kashruth was, for her, an obsession. 

She used to-- you see, we had-- yes, I told you, we had water running. So the girls, the domestics, we had two domestics always, a Polish, a Christian domestic, and a Jewish cook. And they-- my mother forced them to wash their hands so many times, because from [YIDDISH], from-- from-- from meat, from meat products, to milk products, you touch that, you have to wash your hands. They had swole hands-- they had swole hands from the cold water running, because the water was not heated. It was from-- from the cold water. 

And the cutlery and crockery for Pesach, for instance, was packed in another room on the 3rd floor. There was a kitchen, and we called it the Pesach kitchen. Not that we used it for Pesach-- we used it once a year, once a year, when we had this goose fat to prepare, to prepare the fat for Pesach. Though that was being done in Hanukkah, in Hanukkah. And later, when we had these [YIDDISH], these pieces of fat, after the fat was rendered from it. That was really a treat. 

Many people-- people used to come there for that-- children from all around, from-- from-- from the neighborhood. And now, she was very, very, very kosher. In-- in Sadkowa Gora, for instance, in our estate, we had two different kitchens. One kitchen for milk products and one kitchen for-- and one kitchen for meat products. They were not mixed. You can't mix, you see? 

This was-- and I used to tell her, I used to tell her when she asked, when she could see that I am not orthodox when I grew up and I'm not orthodox, I used to tell her, look. I think the Lord in heaven sent down-- the Lord in heaven sent down a quota of orthodoxy to-- per family, and you took all-- you-- you filled out all the-- all the quotas. Nothing was left for me. 

INT: Thank you, we'll continue next tape.

INT: Tape three of Mark Verstandig. We were talking about your family traditions, the nature of Jewish culture in Mielec. Can we continue on from what you were saying last tape? Let's talk now about how did this Jewish tradition express itself in your home. 

MV: As I mentioned my home was a very orthodox home. It was a kosher home. And my mother used to wear a wig. And besides this, it was traditional. 

Let's say-- take a Friday night. We used to go to the synagogue-- mother and father, and-- and mother stayed home. Father and children and my brother and I went to the synagogue. When we came back from the synagogue, we always used to take with us a poor fellow who-- who-- who-- who didn't have where to go. That was a tradition. Again, we called it an orayach. We called him a guest. 

In the synagogues, there were still poor people, especially from-- from other towns. They used to travel from town to town, begging. And in the synagogue, he used to stand next to the door. And when wealthier people finished, they went home after the service, they used to take with them at least one poor fellow with them home to have dinner. 

And besides this, we had permanent people from town. We had the permanent poor men. This-- their-- their-- their children were artisans. I think they were [NON-ENGLISH]. They made-- they were called blacharz. I don't know how to translate it. 

INT: Tinsmith. 

MV: Tinsmith. They were tinsmiths, but they were poor. So their grandfather used to be our permanent guest for every Shabbat and for every yontif, for every-- for-- for every-- for-- for every holiday. He used to come, and he used to sit with us at the-- at the-- at the dinner-- the dinner table. 

Now, we used to come home, was a very, very orthodox and nice-- really a nice atmosphere. You see the candles on the table. The table was covered-- covered table. And there was-- was a candelabra. 

And we used to come home. And my father used to go first, open the door, and my mother and my sisters were waiting. And he used to greet them with Shabbat Shalom, good Shabbos, a hearty one. 

And then we used to say with us-- he used to sing a song [HEBREW]. Peace be with you. We bring you peace. Like a dance, we were around the table. And then the kiddush, a blessing of the wine. And it was really a very nice atmosphere-- a very nice atmosphere. This was a Friday night. 

Saturday morning, it was not as nice, because you went to the synagogue in the morning. And if we didn't tell you, that we are not allowed to eat before we were going to the synagogue, only a snack-- coffee and a biscuit. And you came home 12 o'clock. You're very hungry, Your kiddush was very short. It was the blessing of the wine. It was a very short one. 

And the-- the meals were not as good as Friday night, because on Friday night, the meals were fresh. You are not allowed to cook on Shabbat. But when the pots are on the stove, they can stay on the stove. So they stayed on the stove, and they were hot. On Saturday, everything was cold. The only thing which was-- which was warm enough was the so-called cholent. 

That was a stew, which day before you-- you-- you-- you sent it to the bakers. And the bakers put it into the oven for the night. The oven was still warm or hot from the baking from baking on Friday. And on Saturday, you came to collect. You went to this baker. You sent your servant to the baker. Every pot has-- has a name. And you brought in the so-called cholent. 

And the cholent-- sometimes it was good, and sometimes it wasn't. They used to say that the angel is cooking-- that the angel is cooking the-- the cholent. Sometimes it is good. And sometimes it's not good. 

So I didn't like it on Saturday. But that was the time. That was the way of life. 

And then there was the third meal, the so-called shaleshudes. And the third meal, that means after the evening prayer-- the first evening prayer, afternoon prayer-- Mincha-- you came home, and you had a third meal on Shabbat. That was [NON-ENGLISH]. That was obligatory. You had to do-- had to it. 

Beautiful was the [HEBREW]. That means the-- the meal on the end of Shabbat. We called it to say goodbye to the princess in Shabbat-- [HEBREW]. Yes, and that was a very nice-- it was a very nice meal. And people were coming and singing and spending this until 10 o'clock or 11 o'clock at night. 

So that was-- that was the Sabbath. During the-- during the week, now, everything was as I told. You're kosher, and kosher is not an easy thing to do. You have to-- we have to be very careful. 

And besides this, let's say the-- the education-- my education was a typical-- typical traditional orthodox education. 

INT: Could I just stop you there, if I may? Let's finish off the household, the standard of living. Did your family eat meat every day? 

MV: Did family? 

INT: Did your family eat meat every day? 

MV: My family, yes, almost. Almost, almost every day meat. But the main meal was midday, and not like here in the evening. The main-- it's this up till now, I think, in Poland's the same. But the main meal with meat is midday. And-- and before going to bed, I mean, the-- the so-called supper, the so-called supper is a very poor. It's just a piece of bread and something-- a snack-- a snack and you go to bed. So I think it's better. It's-- I think it's a much better arrangement. 

INT: Would that be typical for most of the Jewish people there, eating meat almost every day? 

MV: I wouldn't say that it would be typical, but if-- but for wealthier people, yes, for not wealthy people, not very much. Some people would-- will-- would eat-- well, they would eat only a piece of bread and with-- with butter and a piece of herring. That would be it. A sardine was a luxury. A sardine was a luxury. But the wealthier people had meat almost every day, unless they didn't want to. 

But that was only for the Jewish people. The other ones, they-- the-- the-- and-- and-- and-- and that was the way of life of the-- of the Christian people-- of the Christian Poles in town. 

But as far as the way of life, the standard of living in the villages is quite different. It was unbelievable. You know, in the morning, the farmhands and small farmers would eat the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. Really, I couldn't tell you how it was made, with a bit of-- a bit of milk and-- and cream-- and cream mixed with potato, with-- with-- with the potato mash. And it was quite-- and-- and a bit of-- a bit of herbs. It was quite tasty. I'm telling you, it was quite tasty. And a piece of bread-- that was the breakfast. 

And that was the lunch, the dinner-- potatoes. And that was the supper. Farmhands, small farmers, had meat twice a year-- for Easter and Christmas. Just I'm telling you. This is not a joke-- twice a year. For Christmas, they had chickens, but the chickens they also sell because they needed money to buy nails, to buy iron for their farms, iron products for their farm. And this was a monopoly of the-- of the large-- of the large industries, which were, again, these industries were in the hands of non-Jews. And the prices were very high. 

So they needed the money, whatever they had. Yes, sometimes for Christmas, for instance, they used to-- they used to kill a pig if they had to, if they have. And see, they-- and the killing a pig was quite a-- was quite a-- a treat. It was a whole ceremony. The neighbors came, and everybody-- everybody was taking part in it. So normally, the people in the villages had meat only twice a year-- once for Christmas, and once for Easter. 

INT: And the Gentiles in the city? 

MV: And the Gentiles in the city had no-- the Gentiles in the city ate the same as the Jews. The only thing's the Jews didn't eat pork, and they did. The Jews didn't eat bacon for breakfast. They did. Also bacon was not a breakfast meal in-- in-- for anybody-- for anybody in Poland. 

In Poland, the breakfast consisted of eggs, coffee, coffee, a piece of bread, or a bagel, or a-- or a roll. And that was the breakfast. But bacon and eggs was not used-- was-- was not-- I mean, it was not accepted by the Gentile-- by the Gentile population in Poland. But they ate meat every day, the same as the-- as the-- as-- as the Jews. The town people ate differently than-- than-- than the people in the villages-- in-- in-- in the small villages. 

INT: What about fruit and vegetables-- where they plentiful? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Were fruit and vegetables plentiful? 

MV: I don't think it was plentiful. But you had to sell this to buy little things what you needed. As I told you, a question of every farmer needs-- needs some iron to-- to-- for-- for-- for the wheels of the cart-- of the carts and for their-- and for their little machinery, whatever they had. But that was in the hands of monopolies, which were, again in the monopolies of the large, Polish Gentile industries. And for that, they had to pay a lot of money. 

INT: Was it quite common for people to eat fruit and vegetables in your town? 

MV: No. Not-- not very common. Not very common-- fruit and-- fruit and vegetables. But we usually-- it was common for the-- for-- for the farmers, for the peasant people. They eat a lot of fruit-- not fruit, but vegetables. Of course, fruit was-- fruit very-- quite expensive. 

INT: Where would you place-- when you were growing up, where would you place your family? Were they in the middle of the social class, or were they quite wealthy? 

MV: They were very wealthy. They were landowners, very wealthy, very wealthy, before-- even before-- when-- when in 19-- I was there in 1936. And that was after-- that was after the depression. the depression was over. The depression was over. 

And then there was a question of the so-called agricultural reform. The government wanted to buy out larger estates and to divide them among the smaller farmers, so that they should become owners of land. They should-- for farmhands or small farmers should enlarge their-- their-- should enlarge their holdings, because the population enlarged. The farmer who had, let us say, five hectares, and he had three sons. So each son later, when he died, each son has only 1 and 1/2 hectares. So they were very poor. 

This had to be-- this had to be-- this had to be solved. This problem had to be solved. So there was at-- at that time a project of Madagascar-- of Madagascar. They-- it was the Pilsudski regime-- wanted the Poles to emigrate to Madagascar because they were-- they didn't have land. The sons of the small farmers didn't have any more land. There was nothing to divide. 

So they want them to go to Madagascar. And to Madagascar it will be-- it will be a two-fold-- a two-fold profit for Poland. First of all it will solve the problem of land in Poland. The land over there is from next to nothing. The government will give them the land. The colonial government will give them the land. There was plenty of land over there. 

And secondly, when a lot of Poles will come to Madagascar, Poland will claim that Madagascar should be a Polish colony. And so Poland will become a colonial power-- a colonial power. But the Polish peasant leadership was against it. They claimed that Madagascar is an island inhabited by cannibals. And what the government want? The government want to send the Poles over there to-- to-- to-- a cursed island. Who knows what kinds of climate over there is, and so on and so on. 

Now, it didn't-- it-- it-- I mean, it-- it didn't get off the ground. As a matter of fact, a few years later-- a few years later, the same plan was elaborated by the Polish government, but about-- again about Madagascar, that we should send to Madagascar the Polish Jews. And how clever, and how mature the Polish ministers were? I'll tell you. 

Take this example. They sent a delegation to Paris to confer with Léon Blum and with Georges Mandel, the minister for colonies, both Jews. They say they want them, they want Léon Blum and Georges Mandel to give Madagascar to Poles so that the-- to Poland, so that Poland should be able to expel the Jews to Madagascar. Oh, surely, Léon Blum and Georges Mandel we wouldn't have a bar of it. 

So that was the question of when the-- but I don't remember exactly what-- what your question was. 

INT: Oh, we're going back to finishing off about living standards in those days. You made mention earlier about the fact that your family's house had running water, which was an exception. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: So what did most people do for running water? 

MV: They didn't have any. There was an industry. People used to travel, used to carry-- used to, you know, to-- to drag a barrel-- used to drag a barrel of water, pulling a barrel of water from the pump, which was outside the town, around the town, and selling buckets of water to households. And I still remember there was one who came back again from the United States-- a poor immigrant. He made enough money in the United States to buy a horse and a cart. And on this cart he had two or three barrels of water. And he was traveling around the houses and selling buckets of water and carrying the buckets of water into the house. This is how people used to have water. 

INT: What about the WC and the washing-- people washing themselves? 

MV: Ah, that was, again, from the water, which they was-- which they were buying. Now, wash-- this I would say, to do the washing was quite a-- was quite a-- an-- an industry. I mean, first of all, there were professional women who were washing-- who were doing the washing. 

There where-- they used to have, and they put in the kitchen large containers. They called them balia. How do-- would you-- how would you translate balia into English? 

INT: Tubs. 

MV: Tubs-- it's not exactly a tub, but you could-- as a matter of fact, people used it sometimes-- sometimes for a tub. So they used to have such tubs-- round one-- round tubs. And they used to be such-- such a piece of timber covered with corrugated iron. And on that corrugated iron, they used to wash. They used to put, let's say, a shirt. There was water with this-- they would put it with a shirt, and used to put some-- some soap on the shirt or whatever is going-- anything what is being washed and used to rub it. You can see it in pictures sometimes. The Indians and so on are doing it here along the river-- along the river. 

So this was the washing clothes once and two-- once, two-- about every fortnight or every month, there was to be this washing time. There were professional women coming to wash whatever thing could be washed. 

But again, you should-- you had to buy the water from the water carriers. They were called water carriers-- vaser treger-- water carriers. 

INT: And your house was also an exception. It had an internal WC. 

MV: An internal-- and we had a bathroom. And internal-- and internal, we had got the-- he had the upstairs two toilets and downstairs two toilets. 

INT: And what did the-- most of the other people do for-- 

MV: And by the way-- by the way, at-- in the beginning-- in the beginning, there was no kanalisation. There was no sewerage. There was no sewerage. So once every month or every two months, there used to be-- there were-- there were such large containers underneath downstairs, because it would run downstairs. And they used to come and clean it up. 

As a matter of fact, you know, the big town Lodz-- I think you are come from Lodz-- a metropolis of 600,000 people, they didn't have a sewerage. They just didn't have a sewerage. So there were professional people going around and-- during the night, and cleaning-- and cleaning the toilets, cleaning out the large containers underneath. 

INT: Were they Gentiles? 

MV: They were Gentiles. They were-- 

INT: And what did you call them? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: What were they called? 

MV: I don't remember. I don't remember how they were called. I don't remember if there were special names for them. [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember that there were special names for them. But later, in Mielec-- no, in Lodz never, but later in Mielec, they made the sewerage system work. I remember, they were digging this canals and so on, and there were-- there was sewerage in Melbourne-- in Mielec. 

INT: And were most people using kerosene light for lighting at home? 

MV: Yeah, for lighting, they were-- it long had been there. And when they had electricity, they didn't use kerosene. But when there was about-- before they had kerosene-- in Sadkowa Gora we still had kerosene. We didn't-- we-- we-- we never had electricity there. 

INT: And what about the traditions of the mikvah for the men and women? 

MV: Oh yes, there was every-- in every little town, there was a bath-- a steam bath, a proper Turkish steam bath. And matter of fact, some Gentiles, who had a sense of really going to a good steam bath, used to come there on Friday, because it worked only on Friday. 

And it worked in such a way-- it was a large hole. At one end of the hole was a stove covered with stones and heated up with hardwood, not with coals, not with coal, but with hardwood, with hardwood. And against it were benches, like in the amphitheater. Higher-- it got up higher, higher, higher. And on top of the-- of the stove, from the roof, hanging from the roof almost, was a barrel of water-- was a barrel of water. 

And from time to time, the men who were serving as the bath keeper used to turn this barrel upside down, and hot water was poured on these hot stones. And a wave of hot steam used to come against this-- against the benches. And immediately water was pumped up through this barrel through to-- to-- to this barrel again. 

I'm telling you, I used to go there since I was maybe five years old. And this is why I-- why I can stand up today to the hot sauna of 90 degrees. I used to take a bucket-- two buckets of ice cold water and a towel submerged in this water and put it on top. And used to walk up to the top bench. 

And there on the top bench, after one minute, you had to pour a whole bucket of cold, icy cold, water on your head. You could stand another minute and a half and do the same with the other bucket. And then you had this towel, which was submerged in this cold water before. You put the towel of cold water on your head, and you negotiated the stairs down. 

While on the second there was this-- the benches were very large, like here at least-- at least 40 centimeters, because otherwise, people could slip, so at least 40 centimeters-- maybe, maybe 50 centimeters. When I was a second-- the higher you came, the hotter it was. But then you came down. On the second bench, I had prepared there my little broom made out of birch twigs. 

And I lie down, and I pressed the hot air against my front. I lied down on my front, on my belly, and I asked my neighbor to give me a few-- a-- a-- a few hits with this-- with this broom on the back. And from there, I jumped into a cold mikvah. Or if it was winter, I used to run out and roll in the snow. And nothing happened to me. And nothing happened to anybody, because it was so hot. You can't imagine how hot it was when you walked up the highest-- when you walked up the highest bench. 

It came from the heat, from this stove heated up-- from the stones heated up with-- with-- with dry wood. 

INT: And they had a separate night for women? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Had a separate night for women? 

MV: It was just-- woman not-- woman didn't go to this. Woman went only-- came only to this large mikvah. There there were washing facilities. There were-- there were tubs. They used to-- could take a tub. And after that, they'd used to go in the mikvah. The mikvah was like a-- like a small-- a small, deep pool, but a very cold one. 

That was so-- that was so-- as a matter of fact, today people wonder-- I am not a young men, yet I can sit in a-- in a-- in a-- in a hot sauna for half an hour in [INAUDIBLE] recreation center. It's about 85 degrees or 90 degrees. And other people come in and run out after five minutes. But I'm used to it, because I started when I was young. 

And there were quite a few Gentiles coming there. But they could come only on Friday afternoon. That was the only day when this steam bath was working. 

INT: According to religious practice, did your mother use the mikvah as the only time she went to wash herself? 

MV: No, we had a-- she had a bathroom. We had a bathroom-- a proper bathroom. So you didn't need to use the mikvah. Besides this, you don't need-- you're not washing yourself in the mikvah. If you want to wash yourself, you take a bath in the tub. After the tub, you went to the mikvah. The mikvah is only for ritual purposes. You go there, and you submerge in the mikvah, but not to wash. You can't take soap to the mikvah. 

You jump in. You see, like-- what's his name-- John the Baptist. You see? He used to take somebody and throw them into the river. You baptize him, but you're not wash him. 

INT: What was the language used at home? 

MV: The language we use at home was either German or Yiddish, because my father spoke perfect German. And this is the reason that I studied the German. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: --Verstandig. A description of a-- a-- what was the role of the woman of the house when you were a young boy? 

MV: --there was-- used to be always the husband's. And the woman had her place in the house to look after the children, after the growing-up children. And after that-- --to the house. Normally-- [AUDIO OUT] 

The reason being, that we-- we-- you-- you didn't have all these gadgets-- [AUDIO OUT] --all the little gadgets today, vacuum cleaners and so on. For instance, you had-- in our house, we had parquets, parquets in floors, parquet floors. 

And I remember the domestic used to have two brushes, each-- on each leg, one brush. And she, at six o'clock in the morning, she used to dance and-- [AUDIO OUT] Quite an exercise, really. But we had to pay for this exercise. But it was quite a-- quite a healthy exercise, dancing on the floor-- on the floor, with the-- with the-- with the brushes. 

And so, the household, at that time, required a lot of more work than today. Therefore, very-- [AUDIO OUT] --wife-- [AUDIO OUT] --jobs, or tried to get some-- [AUDIO OUT] [INAUDIBLE] [AUDIO OUT] --but really they ought to-- [AUDIO OUT] --said they in the morning, it was a mikveh. [AUDIO OUT] --doing prayers. [AUDIO OUT] 

And after that they used to go back to the-- [AUDIO OUT] which was at the same time as synagogue, by the way. [AUDIO OUT] And there were no more morning prayer. The prayers-- morning prayers were finished much earlier. [AUDIO OUT] and studied the-- [AUDIO OUT] page, two pages, the whole day, a page a day. 

Went-- when they went home for dinner, midday, and 2 o'clock, they went back and spent their time studying in the house-- in the study house, studying Talmud. And somebody had to-- [AUDIO OUT] and somebody had to work for a living. 

So it was a woman, who carried on as shopkeepers. Not only that they had to prepare the-- the breakfast and the-- the-- the dinner and the-- and the supper, and they had to clean the house if she didn't have a domestic. 

Not everyone has a domestic. The poor-- poor shopkeepers didn't have domestics, so they had to keep the house clean. Clean the house, and at-- [AUDIO OUT] and at the same time, to look after the shops. The shop-- [AUDIO OUT] actually in-- in front of still the dwelling. 

So they did the-- they-- they did both. But the woman for orthodox people-- and for orthodox people, they were almost the majority in the shtetl-- they were the breadwinners. It's unbelievable. The woman were the breadwinners. And the men were spending their time speaking with God. 

INT: Earlier on, you mentioned that Yiddish was your home language-- 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: --back at home. Was this the usual case for Jewish people? 

MV: No, it was the usual case. That was the usual case. I would say that 90%, 99% of the Jewish population in Poland spoke Yiddish at home. It does not mean that they couldn't speak Polish. There were some who couldn't speak Polish even. There were some couldn't speak Polish. It's unbelievable. 

There were-- there were Jews in Poland for generations, and they couldn't speak Polish. They never-- they've-- they have never met a Pole. They lived in their-- in-- in their circle of Jews, from the home to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the study house, to the synagogue, or to the mikveh and back. And they never-- they-- they never came in contact with-- with-- with Poles. 

And there's-- there's this-- there's this, there's this story which-- which I mentioned. It was the story with [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a Jew, who-- he had a-- a haberdashery store. I remember exactly where it was, even. In the corner of the-- the-- or in the corner of this main square, on the right of the church, there was a haberdashery store. 

His name was Birnbaum. But he was called [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember him. He used to have such a large-- a large black hat and a-- and a beard, a-- a black beard, and-- and this-- and this-- this dress, the kapoteh, the-- this-- how do you call this? This long-- this long blank-- black dress? 

INT: Frock coat. 

MV: He had a frock coat, right all-- almost until the knee. And every day, I could see him going across-- because we lived on the other side, across the main square-- when he went to the mikveh. You see? 

Now, one day his wife went out. So-- and she told him to watch the store. And two-- two Polish woman came in and asked him, [NON-ENGLISH], where's your wife? He didn't understand what they're asking for. 

So he started to take out these boxes with thread, blue threads, grey threads, and so on. He said-- he said, [NON-ENGLISH], showing it. He ask them, if this-- if they want that. 

Until-- and they were laughing their heads off, until-- until his wife came in. And since then, this fellow was called [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] he was called, [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean people didn't know him by any other name, only [NON-ENGLISH]. 

That shows you how isolated the Jews were. As a matter of fact, I read through, once, an interview with Czeslow Milosz. And he pointed out the same, that-- that there were the Jews in Poland, many people, many Jews in Poland just didn't know the language, didn't understand the language. 

INT: Taking as an example, the-- the generation of your parents. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: How many of those, roughly, would you say knew Polish well in your childhood? 

MV: Knew Polish well? I would say, knew Polish well, would be about 40%. They knew Polish well. Because, normally, you take the Jews, shopkeepers, who-- who-- who were attending the shops, themselves, peddlers, they had to know the language. 

And the Jews who really didn't come in contact with Poles were a very small percentage. Let us say, could have been about 10%, 15% of Jews who didn't come-- Jews-- Jewish males who didn't come in contact with Poles. And they didn't know the language what-- whatsoever. 

In the middle, between them and the other, and another 35%, I would say, knew a little bit of Polish. They were-- they-- they-- they knew-- they may-- could make themself understood, but if you would ask me if their Polish was a proper one, I would say, no. But neither was the Polish of the peasants a proper one. But on the other hand, you know, the-- yeah, the-- the language of the-- of the peasants became, later, very, very popular-- very popular. 

A friend of ours, a schoolmate of ours, Voitech Scuza, was a colleague of ours in the gymnasium. And he became known as a-- as one-- as one of the first, or he may-- may be the first peasant, the first poet writing poetry in the peasant language, Voitech Scuza, a very nice man. 

Matter of fact, he died a few weeks ago. He died of tuberculosis-- a very nice man. But his-- his poetry was a-- was a poetry of the-- of the-- of the peasant, of-- of the peasant language. It was amazing. 

While he was in the high school, in gymnasium, it was known that his orthography-- orthography, yes-- his spelling, his spelling was terrible. He couldn't spell properly. He-- he could see, could write a [NON-ENGLISH] with a C-Z, you see? Really, his-- his-- his-- his spelling was-- was horrible. But still, he became like a-- became a well-known poet. 

INT: And what would be the general picture for the population? Amongst the Gentiles, how many of them would have been fully literate before the war? 

MV: How many were? 

INT: Fully literate, in your childhood, of the people in the town. 

MV: [SIGHS] Look, that is a question, which I-- which I would like to answer. Because people-- people tell me that I made a mistake, a terrible mistake, slandering Poland by-- by writing that they were 75% illiterate amongst the Polish. 

And they told me, look, there were-- there was a law with compulsory schooling. And I answered them. When there was a compulsory school, the kids were Jews. After the Polish-- after the-- after the-- the-- the establishment of the Polish Republic after the war, how long did-- did it exist? 20 years, from 1920 to 1939, which would mean a quarter of the population were subject to compulsory schooling. 

But what I am pointing out is this law of compulsory schooling was not enforceable. And it couldn't be enforced. In the towns, somehow, but besides this, somehow, to a-- to a certain extent, only. 

I know that along a road, for instance, of 10, 15 kilometers, there could have been a one-- only one [NON-ENGLISH], one, small, four class-- four-grade, four-grade school, 10 kilometers. The infrastructure-- who, in the winter, would take the children to school? There were no buses, no school buses. There were no cars taking-- taking children to school. 

There was an impossibility to bring children from Sadkowa Góra or from Gliny Wielkie, from Gliny Wielkie to Borowa, seven miles-- kilometers. Besides this, people, the farmers in Poland, I mean, 75% of the population were in-- in-- outside towns. 

And no-- they-- they were-- were-- were farmers, were farmers. They worked from dawn to night, from the-- from the-- from-- from dawn till sunset. They didn't have time to take children to school. 

And in winter, when there was no-- not much work, they didn't have-- their children didn't have a-- a-- shoes to walk to school or to bring them to school. Look, that's impossible. It was impossible to bring children to school. 

In Sadkowa Góra, in this area, I knew only of one man who could read and write. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He used to tell me, I have open eyes. I can read. And I know what's going on. 

When you sent somebody from Sadkowa Góra to town with a few letters, here, there, and there, the messenger used to make signs on the envelope and to remember that this is for, let us say, [NON-ENGLISH], and this is for [NON-ENGLISH], and this for them. He never made a mistake. 

Now, walking through town, he took off his shoes, because leather, sole leather was expensive. So he carried his shoes on his elbows. Before he came to town, he put on the shoes. Now, do you expect such people to-- to take their children to school? 

So I talked to a friend of mine, here. His name is Stern, Leon Stern. He comes from Buchach, 20,000 people in town. I asked him, how many-- how many primary schools were there for 20,000 people? He said, two, two seven-- seven-grade schools. Now how many people, how many pupils could you put in? 

So that means the law in-- for compulsory schooling was a fiction. And people couldn't read and couldn't write. 

INT: When-- 

MV: By the way-- by the way, people who are telling me that I am slandering Poland, telling of 75%, they should look up. There was in Poland a book called [POLISH], the small, yearly statistical-- statistics, issued by the government. It had red covers. Let them look there, in this small-- in these small books, in these small [POLISH], to see how many. They wrote about how many people were literate and how many people were illiterate. 

It's unbelievable. If I am not mistaken, I am-- I am always working on my memory. I could make a mistake here and there, but I'm always working my memory. My memory tells me that in this [POLISH], there was a-- there-- there was a figure of 29 years of the average life in Poland. Do you believe that? 

What does it mean? That a lot of children who were dying. A great percentage of children, infants were dying. And they were pulling down this average figure for-- this average figure for the-- for the life expectancy, put it that way. That was something else. 

It-- and I think it's unbelievable. I-- I-- I didn't put this figure in, but they were talking-- by the way, they would have had-- again, tell me. They would have told me that I'm slandering. And let them take out the [POLISH], and then we will see. I-- also I-- I haven't got it. But I'm working on my memory. 

INT: So to put things in perspective, would it be fair to say that, in the small villages, for the peasants, themselves, they were usually completely illiterate? 

MV: Not completely, but very few were-- were-- very few were able to read. When, for instance, during the war, for instance, where I was in the last year when I was in hiding, [NON-ENGLISH], he used to bring, from time to time, when he went to town, let's say on a Thursday in a market, in to-- to the market day, he used to bring a paper. 

Now, a lot of people went to town at that time. But he, only he, in this neighborhood, would have brought a paper. And then, a lot of neighbors used to come. And he used to read to them the paper. This is almost like-- it was a completely different world. Because I am mentioning to you, the compulsory schooling could not be enforced. 

INT: To put this into perspective, what about a-- a smallish town, like Mielec, what percentage of the Gentiles would be completely literate? 

MV: Oh, quite a number of-- of the-- quite a number of them, completely-- completely literate or illiterate? 

INT: Completely literate. 

MV: Well, completely literate? 

INT: Yes, reading and writing. 

MV: Reading and writing, at least 50%. 

INT: So a majority? 

MV: Majority. A majority, at least 50%. And there were a number of them who-- who went to school and had the four-- four-- four years' schooling. But they became illiterate later. And this happened even in Australia, and it happens in the United States. It happens in the United-- 

INT: What about a larger city, like Cracow? Would that percentage change? 

MV: I couldn't tell you about Cracow. Cracow, for instance, in-- in big towns, in big towns, the majority, a great majority of people were literate, a great majority of people. They could read and write. That was quite-- quite a different story. 

INT: And what about the difference between the Jewish organized education and the Gentiles? Was it rare for Jews to be illiterate? 

MV: A Jew couldn't be illiterate, because, first of all, he could write his name in Hebrew letters. And he could start writing-- he could start writing very early. Although, it was-- it was not-- it was not the rule. 

It happened to-- with my wife. She couldn't write Yiddish. She couldn't write Yiddish. And then it happened that, during the war, when she was in hiding, a lot of it, and her parents wrote her a letter in Yiddish, thinking that I am around. 

But I was in Warsaw. And she couldn't read. And she was very, very-- she was very depressed. And she was-- she was very depressed, because she couldn't read, and that might have been their last letter. So-- 

But normally, Jews were, first of all, were able to read, to write their name in-- in-- in Yiddish or in Hebrew. And secondly, there were people who-- who could-- who were-- there were people who did business. They were in business, even-- if even peddlers. They had to be-- they-- they were able to read. So they-- they had to be able to read and to write. 

But taking into consideration the number of people, of Jewish people, who didn't come in contact with the Polish population, and I think that must have been at least 15% and maybe even more, they could not read and write Polish. 

But they could sign their name and they could read German, because Yiddish is a German language. So they could read and write. They didn't need-- they-- they didn't need anybody to sign their name. And as far as this complete illiteracy, to-- to make a cross and to-- somebody else should sign your name, I don't think that there was a-- there was a fraction of 1% of the Jewish population. It was not even a fraction of the-- of 1%. Everybody could read and write. 

INT: Could we begin to start now and ask you about your schooling? How did it begin? 

MV: About what? 

INT: Your schooling. 

MV: My schooling, how it started? My schooling started in the cheder. And the cheder was a Jewish school for small children. They used to be from-- from-- from four years, or three, four years. So there used to be a so-called [NON-ENGLISH] or a [NON-ENGLISH], some-- some German, but, I mean, an assistant, an assistant of the-- of the-- of-- of the rabbi, of the teacher. He used to collect the children and to bring them to this-- and to bring them to this teacher. 

INT: Mixed? A mixed school? 

MV: No, no, no, no, only for boys. Only for boys. No, the school-- the school, the cheder, it used to be in one room. The same room was the kitchen, where his wife baked bread. And he was-- there was a table. And he was sitting there, with his children. 

And he learn-- he-- he taught them, first of all, alef-bet, the alphabet, alpha-- the Hebrew alphabet, and later to read. Although they didn't read Hebrew from the-- from the scriptures, although they didn't have to understand what they are reading, but at least to be able to read. 

And we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. In Hebrew, it would mean Hebrew. But that was-- but in-- at-- in that context, it mean-- it meant only to be able to read without understanding. 

And that was still a small little room. I can tell you about my cheder, a small, little room, which was a kitchen and the bakery, everything. And outside, outside there was a courtyard. 

And this courtyard, sometimes, in the summer, you could go out and play with a-- a-- with a-- with a ball made out of a-- of-- of a-- of-- of socks, of socks, which were-- which were sewn together, sewn together. And this-- this ball-- this ball never went through the air. You could-- could-- couldn't. That was not the case that a ball like that, you hit a-- hit-- hit a window. 

And the same-- and that was in summer. And the same little courtyard was used by the children to piss. 

Most of the little houses in the shtetl didn't have toilets. The only public toilets-- the only public toilets were-- were Jewish public toilets. Behind the synagogues were Jewish publics. There was-- there was only one public toilets. 

You know, in the whole town, there was not a single public toilet. It looked like-- it looked like Anatevka, if you have seen the film, Anatevka. You see? 

That was-- that was during the summer. And how-- that was the schooling, the primary schooling. From there, we went to Talmud Torah. That was like a secondary school, like a secondary school. Again, only-- with us, it was only a religious education, religious education. 

People-- pupils used to come either before noon or after noon. Because of the compulsory schooling, they had to go to school, to public schools. 

Myself, I was relieved, because my mother did everything possible that the school should recognize that I am not healthy enough to go to school, so I shouldn't come to-- to the public schooling, to-- to public schools. 

So I had a teacher coming to my-- to our house, at-- after I came back from this Talmud Torah. And he taught me to write and to read Polish. 

That was, more or less, yes, the schooling. 

INT: Can I just interrupt you there? How many years did you attend the cheder? 

MV: I would say, from three years-- what I say, to seven, to six. From six, I went to-- from six, I went to Talmud Torah. And there, I went, in Talmud Torah, about four or five years. It was four or five years. And there, we-- when I was 10 years old, we started with Mishnah. And 11, 12, it's a-- it's the Gemara. And then I went Yeshiva. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: My mother wanted me to become a rabbi. 

INT: We'll continue next time. Thank you.

INT: Tape five of Mark Verstandig. Yes, you're telling us about yeshiva. You started yeshiva. How old were you? 

MV: Before I started yeshiva, you see, my mother wanted me to become a rabbi. And she could see that I'm not going to become a rabbi from the Talmud Torah. So we had-- she-- we had a relative who was a rabbi himself, but he didn't have a job. He was not-- he was unemployed. 

He-- he was from another town, from Sokolow. So my mother arranged that he use to-- that he came to live with us on his own. And he became my full-time teacher, from the morning till the night. He was to be maybe at least two-- [NON-ENGLISH], which means two seasons. Two six-month seasons he was there. 

He was a very-- he was a very intelligent man, and a great scholar. And he understood psychology, the psychology of children. He knew that a child 12 years old or 10 years old is not interested in legal-- in-- in-- in legal arguments of the Talmud. You see, if a-- if an ox bulls a cow, who is responsible, and so on. Along-- this is the Talmud. And he understood it quite-- quite-- quite well. 

Now, he used to-- he used to start in the morning with the Talmud, with a page of-- of the Gemara. But later, after-- after a quarter of an hour, half an hour, he said, look-- look, Mordec, in the afternoon we will be reading the scripture. We will reading the Bible. A really good story about spies and trumpets, how the Jews conquered the city. 

And that I was looking for, the story-- the story of Joshua and so on. And in the afternoon, he picked out stories in the Bible which could interest-- which could interest a child. The-- the wars of Sisera, and, you know, how-- all kind of wars and so on. And he understood that, and that gives-- gave the child some incentive to be attentive in the morning for the Gemara. 

But after a year, he went away. He got a job after he went away. And my mother took me to a yeshiva. To the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the yeshiva of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi. 

And I will never forget the introduction. My mother came with me, holding me. Said to the Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], said, this is my [NON-ENGLISH], my-- or my youngest son. For nine years, I waited for a son that he should become a rabbi. I will pay you with golden coins. But see to it that he should learn and know the Talmud at least as your Yankele. 

His son Yankele was 16 years old, and he was a genius. People knew he was-- if it wouldn't be a war, he would've been-- he-- he would've been a Mendelssohn. He was really a genius. In 16 years, he had-- he knew the Talmud back and forth. If you-- if you-- if you told-- if you told him a-- a-- a sentence from Talmud, he will tell you this is in volume so-and-so, page so-and-so, three lines from the bottom. He had a photographic memory. 

And making some notations from the Talmud to-- by-- by-- by-- by 16 year, was a genius. And here comes my mother and she thinks that with gold coins, here I would learn-- I would soon know at least as much as-- as-- as his son. And I became the laughing stock of the whole town. That was the problem, the laughing stock. Everybody thinks that this is the man-- that this is the youngster, at least as Yankele. 

INT: How old were you when you started yeshiva? 

MV: I was at least 12 years old. 12, 13. Maybe 13. 12 years, 13 years. 13 years. Would be 13 years. So that was my yeshiva, my-- my yeshiva. 

INT: Was that in Mielec? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: Was that in Mielec? 

MV: In Mielec. Mielec. That was in Mielec. That was around a year-- was-- he was real entitled to become the rabbi of Mielec. Because there were a few brothers, and he was the oldest. And-- but he married a daughter of a rabbi in another town. And this rabbi unusually was quite a wealthy person. So he went over there to [POLISH] to live with his parents-in-law-- with his parents-in-law. 

But later when the parents-in-law died, he came back. But in the meantime, his younger son, who was a fool, became the rabbi. And he couldn't unseat him because he was already employed. So the only thing he could-- he could have done is to open a yeshiva. So I was there in-- in-- in his yeshiva. 

And one-- but I was not very happy. I wanted-- I-- really after-- when I was 14-- 14 years, I decided that-- that enough is enough, that I want to-- not to become a rabbi. Matter of fact, I have to tell you a story about it. 

It was usual that on Shabbat, you should visit some dignitaries in town. A dignitary in town means a Jew who knows the Talmud. And you used to come to him in the afternoon and he used to show off-- he examined you. Well, you should go through a page of Gemara and read it and explain it, how it-- what it means and so on. 

So there were two such people, two such learned people. I used to go every Shabbat, once-- every-- a fortnight. One Shabbat to [NON-ENGLISH], and another Shabbat to [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH], people knew about him. He was the son-in-law of a wealthy family. But everybody knew that he is a highly educated man. He was orthodox. 

But he didn't wear a shtreimel, but a black-- a black hat. He used to go-- he didn't have a long beard, but a very short one. But people knew that he's a highly educated man. And I used to go to him every fortnight.
