And one day, I was certainly 14 years old. One day after-- in the middle-- in the middle of my page, he said to me, tell me, Motel, what books did you read? What books have you read this week? I said, my god, how does he know that I'm reading books? 

But then I realized that he already knows. I said to him, what do you mean? He said, books, books, from libraries, from the Jewish library. Said, my god, he knows already? So if he knows, I may-- I may confess. 

So I told him I read a book which is called The Hero in Chains. He said, a book Hero in Chains? Who is the author of this book? I-- I told him Victor Hugo. He said, it's-- first of all, it's not Victor Hugo, but Victor Ego. Said, look, I read the Hugo, and he tells Hugo-- Ego. He means some-- somebody else. Said, no, it is Hugo. 

What is it? Who is it-- I've never heard of such a title. He said, who is the hero? I said, Jean Valjean. Ah, he said. Went to the other room, took out from his books-- bookshelves a book and showed me the book. And I read it. So I've got the French title. I read it, Les Miserables. I said-- he said it's not Les Miserables, it is Lay Miserable. 

And I said, look, if Victor Hugo is Victor Ego, why shouldn't it be Les Miserables, Lay Miserable. But I-- I am not in a position to argue with this fellow. Anyhow, he was very pleased with me. He was very pleased. And we didn't even finish that page of Gemara, but he was was an-- he's still an orthodox Jew. He stopped to say milah prayer which you-- you have to say before you have shaleshudes, before the third meal on Shabbat. So he stopped to say milah. 

He gave me-- he-- I had to-- he gave me a few-- a few things to eat. I mean, I have to join his-- his shaleshudes. And later he-- he went with me to the door. And I told him, look, I will tell you about your Victor Ego. That the Yiddish translator who translated your Lay Miserable has Hero in Chains understood better the-- the-- this Jean Valjean than Victor Hugo. 

Now he smiles. I just knew. He smiles. He said, who is hero? Why is the hero in chains? Who is the hero in chains? I told him, Jean Valjean. He is in chains of cruel-- old-fashioned cruel laws which doesn't give him a chance to-- to-- to have a-- doesn't-- doesn't give him a break. Doesn't give him a chance to rehabilitate. 

He looks at me. He said, Mordec, I'll tell you something. You will be a good lawyer. Believe me, it's a better-- it's a better job than to be a rabbi in a Jewish community. Remember what I have told you. Be a lawyer. 

This what I am-- and then I decided one day it's enough enough. I have to go-- I have to go to the-- to high school in order to start-- in order to start as a lawyer later. But from-- from that time, he gave me the idea. From that time I thought, really, it's better to be a lawyer than a rabbi. 

INT: So how did you end up starting at the gymnasium? 

MV: I end up or I-- I started? 

INT: Started. 

MV: I started. I started by entering a-- I had to enter a-- an-- an examination. Because I didn't have behind me the-- the schooling, the primary schooling. So I entered into the fifth-- you call it grade-- form or grade? The fifth form. The fifth form. 

My instructor, tutor, wanted me to work with-- he wanted to work with me for another few months that I could go to the seventh form, because I was not ready to the seventh form after a few months tuition. But I was afraid that, in the meantime, my mother, my father, something will-- will go wrong. Because I told them already, look, whatever it is, I'm going. I'm going to-- I'm going to the-- to the gymnasium. 

So I said I would rather go now to the fifth than to wait another few months, which means a year, and to go to the seventh. So I passed the exam. I remember there were two-- there were two-- there was a music teacher, who tried to-- who tried-- I didn't-- I-- I didn't have-- I didn't know music and I didn't know notes. And I don't know up till now to read music. 

And he started with me with do-re-mi and so on, and then said, look, you haven't got-- you haven't got an ear. I said, look, I have. You haven't gotten an ear. You'll-- you'll not-- but that doesn't matter, he said. This subject is no more at the end of this year in-- in the-- in the form, in the sixth form. So I will let you through, because I heard that you are all right in the other-- with-- with the other subjects. 

And so I went to gymnasium. And they didn't have a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] for me. I had a very interesting case over there. As a matter of fact, Paul Barber picked up this from my book, the question of-- of a-- of a-- of a professor so-called Kozak. He taught me to tell him-- to tell him this story. 

There was a teacher. All teachers in gymnasium were called professors. There was a fellow-- a fellow called Kozak. Everybody knew-- everybody knew that he is a very difficult person. And the moment I came in, or he became-- he came to my class, it was the sixth class already. He came to the class. And he wanted the children to introduce themselves. 

So he started to read out the children from the book. Everybody had to stand up, and he called his name and he looked at him. Stand up. And it came to me, at my name. I stood up. And he looked at me and looked and looked, and didn't go further. I'm sure that he's-- he-- he's asleep with open eyes. Didn't say a word. 

I knew already at that time that he had some-- my-- that he had some trouble with my brother. And in the end, my brother made-- my father made a complaint. But it didn't succeed and my brother had to leave this gymnasium, go to another town because of him. So he looks at me, looks at me. 

You know, OK. Few days later-- for a few days he was lecturing a certain period of law in the-- or they call-- before the Middle Ages. How you call this period, [NON-ENGLISH]? The period of-- the Roman period of history. 

He was-- he was lecturing about this Roman period of history, from [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was-- was the historian who wrote-- who was the author of this school-- school text-- of the textbook. And then a few days later, he asked me a question. He-- he-- he asked me. This was the way of examining children in-- in Poland. 

You used to call them up and ask him about all these lectures, about all this period he was lecturing about. I told him everything. Told him more than it-- than it was in the-- in-- in-- in the textbook, in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] textbook. He listened, listened, listened. And then he said to me, sit down, you idiot. 

I sat down. A moment later, I jumped up. I said, look, if I am an idiot, so is [NON-ENGLISH] an idiot. And so are all people who are relying on [NON-ENGLISH] are idiots. He jumped down from this place to me. I was in the first row. He grabbed me like that at the collar and dragged me to the door, opened the door, and pushed me out. 

I was standing outside walking around the corridor. In the-- in the meantime, the headmaster went by. Asked me, what are you doing here? I told him the story. And he looks at me and said, I will look at-- I-- I will look at this-- at this matter. And after the hour, I went back to school. But when I went home, I knew already that my schooling in Mielec is finished like it was with my brother. 

Next day in the morning, I went with a heavy heart to school. I knew that it's my last day, that I will be thrown out. And then when it came the time for Kozak's-- Kozak's hour, another teacher came. Another professor came in and said that Professor Kozak is not well. So he will be in the meantime. 

A-- a couple of hours later, by the-- before the end of-- before the end of the-- before 1 o'clock-- the school only goes from 8:00 to 1:00-- everybody already knew that during the night, Kozak has a break down. They had to take him to an asylum, to a-- he was-- he-- he became just insane. He was-- he was insane. So he-- they locked him up in a-- they-- they-- they locked him up. That was the end of this period. 

Oh, that was that. But with the other teachers, I had a very nice-- very-- very nice relationship. There was a teacher, a Professor [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was not an antisemite. And he-- but he was a little meshugga just the same. 

His method of examining the children was similar to Kozak's. He used to go-- but he used to go on for a couple of weeks, for three weeks sometimes lecturing history. Later his meshuggas was that he used to come open his notebook and read from the notebook the names of the-- of the pupils, from A to Z. And he asked a question. If you didn't start with the right word as he imagined that you should start, he used to say [POLISH] and put in a form, which means the-- you didn't pass the exam today. 

And so it was. I was on V. There's a long while-- a long while to go. And it took about half an hour until he came to me. All other people were-- were already [POLISH]. And the question was, I remember, the-- the-- the history of the Reformation. 

I said, he's not going to tell me [POLISH]. And it came to me. I started. There is a a mime comedy reflecting the history of the Reformation. He looked up. And I told him scene after scene of this mime comedy. And I-- I still remember the last-- the last scene. The last scene was that the emperor comes up to the stage. There is a fire on the stage. The emperor comes up and he want-- he wants to extinguish the fire. 

There was a barrel at the corner. And he grabs the barrel, pours the barrel on the fire. But-- but there was no water. It was oil, and the whole scene is full of oil-- is full of fire. And I told him this comedy was written by that-- that-- there-- there-- there-- there are-- they-- they supposed that the author of this comedy was Erasmus from Rotterdam. And it's written in Latin. 

He looked at me. He came back to me. Put hand to shoulder. It's a privilege to have a pupil like you, just a privilege. Look at him, he said. And next day-- next day he brought me essays-- historic-- essays of history, and to-- told him-- he used to-- he used to meet my father because they were councilors in the city council. He said, look, your son will be a great scholar, and so on and so on. 

Until-- until-- until-- until he had an idea that I am a communist. The reason was that I was-- I befriended-- there was a-- a friend of a-- of a-- of a pupil called Zbreschavski, [POLISH] Andrei Zbreschavski. He was a-- a-- a highly skilled mathematician. A brilliant mathematician and a very intelligent fellow. 

And then-- you see-- and I-- in the meantime I organized the Betar. And he used to come. He was a good-- a personal friend of mine. We used to spend hours reading and so on. So I used to-- he used to come with me to the Betar. And he used to dance the horah. He used to sit and listen to the conversations, to the [YIDDISH]. This is the-- this is the conversations about Jewish history, about Jewish problems, and so on. He used to sit down together with us. 

What we didn't know was that the Polish government was suspicious of Jewish organizations, especially of Jewish youngsters' organizations, thinking that they are communist organizations. 

So they were spying on them. Now, what would Andrei [POLISH] Zbreschavski-- top from the gentry-- Andrei [POLISH] Zbreschavski do in the Betar? 

In a Jewish Zionist organization? It must be a communist organization, which Andrei Zbreschavski interest in. They are running a communist organization. And he came to my father-- the professor came to my father and told me, look, I've got all information about your son. It is not a Betar. It is not a Zionist. He is a communist. And look, look around and you'll see that every [YIDDISH]-- he used the expression-- [YIDDISH] is a servant. 

Every [YIDDISH] is his colleague, is his friend. He doesn't-- he-- he doesn't push away that-- and that shows you that he and Andrei Zbreschavski and so on, they all are communists. And that was the end of this-- of this nice relationship between me and [NON-ENGLISH]. But still later he realized that he made a mistake. He went to see-- in Borowa he went to see his-- Zbreschavski's father who was a doctor. And he was an invalid doctor. 

And he was-- and he-- he really saw-- he really-- he-- he realized that he made a mistake, that I am not a communist. And-- but I'm-- I wasn't sure about it until one day, there was an-- an assignment to write about the-- the history of privileges in Poland. You know, the privileges of the classes of this-- in-- in Poland. You had to make a-- and it's an assignment. It would have been enough, a couple of pages. 

I wrote-- I finished a whole exercise book from A to Z on the principles of Marx, just on the principles of Marx. It's a class war. You know, from A to Z, how they extracted-- the privileges were not given, but they were extracted by classes. The class war, and so on and so on. And I gave all the kind-- all kind of-- of Marx-- of Marx-- in the-- teaching in this exercise book. 

At the end, I-- I wrote, 'If required, I will write again this whole essay proving that Marx was wrong.' That this has nothing to do with-- that-- that it was-- it was-- it was a fallacy from A to Z. Yeah. I got an-- I got a note, 'Excellent. I don't need-- we-- we don't-- we know-- we know it is wrong, but for the serious approach it is an excellent essay.' 

INT: How old were you when you started gymnasium? 

MV: How old I was? Must have been 15 years, because I finished by the-- when I was 19. 

INT: And you were five years in gymnasium? 

MV: Yeah, five year. Five, six, seven, eight. Four years. 

INT: Four years in gymnasium. 

MV: Four years in gymnasium. So I started when I was 15. And I-- yes, I started when I was 15. And I finished when I was 19. 

INT: What was your command of Polish like when you started-- 

MV: Oh, excellent. I knew Polish very well. I knew Polish very well from the-- from-- from-- before entering. Before-- before entering this, because I was reading a lot of Polish literature before I entered, before I went there. And besides this, I-- I-- I was reading the papers. I-- my command of Polish was perfect. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue in the next tape.

INT: Tape 6 of Mark Verstandig. In finishing off the section about your schooling, secondary schooling that is, how many Jewish students would there have been in your year in the class? 

MV: In the class would have been four or five, a small percentage. 

INT: Out of how, how many total? 

MV: Well, there would have been about 10%, so-- well, less than 50%. 

INT: Was there any-- 

MV: There were not many-- 

INT: Was there-- 

MV: --not many Jews. 

INT: Was there any ethnic tension at all? 

MV: No, not at all. Not at all. Not at all. 

It is my-- still my belief that without outside agitation and incitement by a few, there wouldn't have been any problem. 

INT: And when you finished gymnasium, what did you go on to do? 

MV: When I finished gymnasium, I went to Cracow to study law. And I enrolled. But then a law was passed by the government to increase the fees, let us say from $20-- from 20 zlotys, so even less, to 350 zlotys per annum. 

That was a-- that was at that time a large amount of money. I could not afford it. My father didn't have it. It was the time of the depression. 

INT: What year could that have been? 

MV: Would have been '29, '28, from '28 to '29. That was the-- the rural depression started in Poland already year before. In America, it crashed in 1929. But in Poland, the rural depression-- but I didn't have the money. 

See, because-- you see, it started in, in October, the, the university year started in October in Poland. And you had to pay at the beginning of, of '29. It was a lot in the deep. It was a lot in the depression. We didn't have the money. 

Everybody knew that this increase in fees was designed to push out the Jewish students from the university, because at the same time, there was a regulation that if one could supply a-- not an-- a-- if one could supply some kind of a document stating that the parents of this student are not able to pay the money, they could be free altogether. 

The catch was that, that people who are signing such documents-- they're called [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's not a poverty, but it was an inability of paying-- that this document was-- had to be issued by the city council or the village council. And non-Jewish students had no difficulty in getting such a document that the parents are not able to sign-- to-- to pay the $350. Whereas, Jews were normally-- were normally considered rich people. 

There was one case-- I mean, there was a case that a Jew in a-- in a little town, in [NON-ENGLISH] or where I don't remember, in, in a little town not far from the Russian border. I remember this case because it was-- it was mentioned in Parliament in the same-- it was in the same mentioned by a Jewish deputy, called-- I don't remember him. It was a-- he was a representative of the Mizrahi. I'll remember in a minute his name. 

He-- he-- he brought up this method, that the fellow peddler, a hawker, was a who-- who-- a fellow hawker was given a tax assessment of 100,000 zlotys. This whole village where this lived-- where this fellow's whole little, this whole little shtetl, all the-- all, all the-- all the little sheds together with the size, you know, with-- were not worth half the money. It, it was at that time-- it was at that time a kind of a forcing Jews to emigrate. 

So they're forcing Jews to emigrate in a way that they are putting-- that they are issuing assessment to pay taxes out of the air, 100,000 zlotys, 20,000. This Jew didn't have in his-- didn't, didn't have any-- in his capital even 100 zlotys, not, not only 100,000 zlotys. 

And a lot of Jews at that time left Poland. They went to France. They went to Germany. And they went to Palestine. But they left. 

And that was a case. So it was impossible to get-- for a Jewish student to get a-- oh, to get a document of hardship. That was the expression, a document of hardship, which would relieve him from paying 350 zlotys, entry fee 350 zlotys. And therefore, the Jews will disappear from the universities. 

I was very distressed. My father didn't have the money. And I went home. I asked him. And he told me, look, what can I do? 

He was-- he was lying in bed. He had a cold. It would have been the night. 

And he, he told me, look, take my fur coat to Cracow. Sell it. And you will have enough money for the 350 zlotys and to live on for a year or two, 'cause it was a very expensive fur coat. 

But I-- I-- I didn't-- I couldn't accept it, because this fur coat was the last sign of prestige for my father. Without his fur coat, he would-- he would die. You know, a fur coat was his kind of-- of-- of status. I couldn't have done that. 

I went to back to Cracow. And we were-- I was waiting for a miracle, because he was going on, what was-- what-- what will happen? Anyhow, it so happened that I had that-- a-- a kind of a-- a kind of an organization called B'nai B'rith, a secret organization called. But I never heard of this organization. 

Like the, like the Freemasons, that they have got a fund. They establish a fund. If there is real hardship, they will pay the 350 zlotys. 

But I didn't have a chance, because I was the son of a landowner. I was a rich man. So I didn't have a chance. 

But I went to a friend of mine, a certain Leon Selpeter. He had a-- he was a big shot in Cracow. He comes from-- he came from Mielec. His brother was a famous lawyer. And he was the brother-in-law of my-- my wife now, but at that time of my fiancee. 

And I knew this Selpeter from, from childhood. He was a colleague of my brother, this Leon Selpeter in Cracow, who was a-- was a pharmacist. He was the president of the Zionist organization, not only for Galicia. But he was the president for Galicia and the vice president of the- of the so-called-- the so-called Zionist B organization, Birthright. 

I went to him. And he fixed it. He fixed it. 

But before I knew [INAUDIBLE], before I knew that he fixed it-- because he had to fix it in such a way that the Jewish student organization shouldn't know about it. Because the Jewish student organization had to submit a list to the B'nai B'rith. Who was not on their list shouldn't have got it. And then I said-- I told him, when everything came together, three, three miracles came together. That is Leon Selpeter fixed, fixed the fee, that they are going to pay the fee. 

At the same time, on a-- I think on a Shabbat, on a Shabbat at my-- at my cousin's place, who was good lawyer in Cracow, my auntie's place, who had one son only. We were sitting there on a Saturday and listening to music over the radio. We heard an announcement about the tickets, about the lottery ticket. And I won a lottery ticket together with a friend of mine from another town, a friend of mine who lives next door to me in this house, in this academic house, in the hostel. This is called hostel, yeah, Jewish hostel. 

We won 1,000 zlotys. And 1,000 zlotys was a hell of a lot of money. I won 500 zlotys. 

And a few days later, Professor [NON-ENGLISH] called me in. I knew he was a famous professor of Roman law. And one day, he lectured. And he gave to-- he, he explained that the Roman law introduced, introduced the differentiation between interpretation of a document inter vivos-- between people alive-- and a testament, because between people alive, a document, the magistrate has to-- a magistrate has to interpret the document as it is written there. He has to try to understand what is written there, and this is how it should be interpreted. 

Whereas in a testament, the magistrate has to try to establish what was the will of the testator, of the man who wrote the testament. He has to go into, into his-- into-- into-- into-- into his will, what he meant by that, not what is written, but what he really meant by that. He explained it. And that was, he said, the great innovation in Roman law, which was accepted later by all-- by-- by-- by all-- by the jurisprudence all over the world. 

After the lecture, I went over to him. I told him, look, it's a nice-- it's a nice-- a nice way. It is this is how it should be. But it-- they were not the first one. 

The Jewish law, the Talmud provided for it before that. The Talmud said that you should interpret the will, [NON-ENGLISH], to fulfill-- the right-- to fulfill what the-- what the testator, the-- the-- the dead man wanted to say. 

He says, come, come with me. I came, I came to his office. He told me, look, I had rabbis here, and they never told me this. I said, maybe they had a great respect for you, as I have, but they didn't dare. And I have got a bit of chutzpah, so I am telling you, this is-- this is this is Jewish law. 

Wait, I've got something for you. He took from a-- from the shelf a book written by Asher Gulak, Professor Asher Gulak in Hebrew. There are four books, four-- four volumes. He gave me the first volume. 

He said, this is-- Asher Gulak is a friend of mine. And he-- somehow he-- he codified the Jewish Talmudic law in the way Gaius did in Roman law. Do the first chapter, the personal laws, the personal Jewish law, and compare with Gaius. Compare it with the Roman law. And I will pay you for it. 

I did it. And it took me a bit of time. But I-- and they-- he paid me nicely. That is true. He paid me nicely. 

I had the impression that he didn't need it. He needed this it himself, not for me. He needed it, because he couldn't read it. Anyhow, this is how it looked. 

Now, what happened is I'm telling you that just a few days after this winning the lottery and having the fees paid by B'nai B'rith, he called me in. I went to his lecture-- his lectures. And after the lecture, he called me in. And I thought certainly he is going to give me another chapter and to tell me that when I finish the other chapter, he will give me some money. 

But when I came in, he gave me a note to the bursar-- for the bursar for 50 zlotys or for 100 zlotys-- and to me for 100, very nice sum of money. He said, I was very pleased with that. And here you go, and-- and-- and get your money. And here is you have to do another chapter. 

And that is how I started with this, which ended up in a dissertation of-- from Asher Gulak-- from Asher Gulak to Gaius. Now that was- that- and the university. It was not an easy life. It was very bad, because wherever I came, I was the son of a Jewish landowner-- of a Jewish-- of-- of a-- of an owner of a Jewish estate, of-- of-- of a land estate, a very rich man. 

I didn't have a penny. And I had to chase-- I had to chase money. I was running around like mad the whole day. I was running around with-- with tuitions, giving-- instructing students, these high school students with their work. 

And I couldn't afford even to run-- I couldn't afford a train ticket. So I had to-- I had to march from one-- from one place to another place. I came home very late. 

I was active in the Revisionist organization, the Betar-- in the Betar. In the end, I did not attend even the-- I attended only the lectures of [NON-ENGLISH], because he was a great lecturer and a great scholar. I attended the lectures of Professor Kutcheba. He studied Polish history-- well, the Polish-- he-- the-- the old Polish laws. 

Again, because he was a great scholar and a-- an excellent lecturer, a great scholar-- and he was the author of a book about Polish laws pertaining to Jews from the-- the last 1,000 years, from-- from A to-- from-- from the-- from the-- from the time of [NON-ENGLISH]-- how the Jews came in and then they wanted the cemetery and so on and so on and so on. 

Now, this is-- to the other I didn't attend. It was not necessary, because the exams were oral exams at the end of the year. And you could buy this-- you could buy on the black market the lectures of all-- of all-- of all professors who were-- of all subjects. There were even some-- there were even some subjects where you could buy, on the black market, the questions and answers, short questions. And if you go through that one night, you make the-- you-- you pass the exam. 

So I didn't-- I didn't attend. I had my own business. I had business to attend. I was running around. I was running around from day to day, from the whole night. I came back sometimes 10 o'clock, 8 o'clock at night. 

And I was reading till 2 o'clock. I was reading late at night and getting up late in the morning, running down, rushing down to the next-- to the kiosk, where I used to borrow the paper, the daily paper, the Moment and the Haynt, the Yiddish papers. Not the Courier, because the Courier one could read in-- in-- in the-- in the library, in-- in-- in-- where there was the Jewish academic house, the hostel, there was a library over there. You could read it there, though I used to borrow from him the Haynt and the Moment. 

And on Friday, I used to borrow-- Friday was a very-- was a very good paper, a lot of articles of the best Jewish writers in Moment and the Haynt. And I used to pay him a few pence a week and borrow the paper and give it back-- give it back to him. 

Now, this is how I started in the morning. On the way back, I used to buy-- have a-- a bottle of milk. And I had this chocolate. I used to mix it with this cacao-- and a piece of bread. And that was how I lived. 

I didn't have money to go for it. With all the-- with all the work done, I didn't have money to go to a proper dinner, to have a proper dinner. This is how-- but the moment I won the-- I won the lottery, I became a rich man. 

I went up. I bought a pair of shoes, new shoes, and to re-sole the older ones. I bought-- it was winter. I bought a nice-- a nice wool coat and a nice hat. And I bought two, two tennis rackets. 

And there was snow all over. And it was the middle of winter, maybe minus 20 degrees. And with the two rackets, I bought a return ticket to Mielec to give one racket to my-- to my fiancee. It was-- that was the wealthy year. 

INT: How many years did you attend university for? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: How many years did you go to university? 

MV: Four. 

INT: Four years. Were Jewish students a very small minority? 

MV: I wouldn't say they were a small minority. But they were a minority. They were a minority. 

And again, the-- it was not an easy-- it was a not-- not an easy life at the-- at the university. As far as the professors are concerned, was no antisemitism. I never-- I could-- I-- I could never trace any antisemitic word from a professor. They were very nice to everybody. 

But the student organizations, you see, they were organized-- there was an organization by the [NON-ENGLISH] and later by the falangists, [NON-ENGLISH]. 

And they organized almost pogroms every year, because, unlike the university in Australia, a policeman could not enter the campus of the university unless called in by the vice chancellor. 

And there they were-- they were kicking. And they, they were beating up Jews. Every year that-- the year started beating up Jews, Jewish students, and marching and there was anti-Jewish slogans. 

And I had a brush-- the second year, I had a brush with them. I was sent out by the Jewish student organization in the-- the-- a Jewish academic house. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. They sent me to have a look what's going on. There was going-- there was-- in one of the halls was a meeting of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the [NON-ENGLISH] to see what's going on there, because from time to time, they organized marches against the Jewish academic house, which was in the Jewish quarter. 

So I went over there. I listened fine. I listened to everything. And then I realized that they are going to march to the Jewish academic house. 

I said, it's time to telephone them to be prepared. So I stood up a couple of minutes too early to walk off. And then somebody must have recognized me. Like a pack of wolves they came on me. 

And I don't know what happened. But I found myself a flight of stairs down, lying in blood. They-- it was-- I was-- they hit me. They hit me and kicked me. Really, I-- I-- I was-- I was almost half killed. 

Somebody recognized me. That was all the time. But I didn't go in anymore to such meetings. 

As a matter of fact, I remember a-- a couple of years later, there was a kind of a-- of an alignment between the Jewish students and the [NON-ENGLISH] students, it means the [NON-ENGLISH] students against the [NON-ENGLISH], because the [NON-ENGLISH] were in power in the student union-- was in that-- in the student union, using this expression. They had these libraries and everything. And they-- they were selling on black market. They were selling the-- the-- old-- old lectures. 

Anyhow, there was an alignment between the Jewish students and them. And I still remember a fellow called Bierszetczki, Borislav Bierszetczki, from the falangists. He came along to try to convince the other students, the [NON-ENGLISH] students, the Szalasi students, to to abandon-- to split them from the Jews and to go against the Jews and so on. 

And I was very impressed with this fellow. He was an excellent-- an excellent debater, an excellent speaker. He didn't succeed. 

But although he didn't succeed, he worked very hard. And I was very impressed. [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah. I, I was very impressed with him. 

And later, you know, this was this Borislav Bierszetczki, who in 1937 said about-- about-- about Mussolini that Mussolini is naive, that he should take-- he should take the-- he should take the lesson from Hitler how to deal with the Jews, that there is no possibility of having any alliance with Jews, because Mussolini had some, some Jews in his parliament. He said, Mussolini doesn't understand the Jewish question. He should follow Hitler. 

That is the Bierszetczki. And that is the Bierszetczki who, after the war, when the Communists came in, he was against the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was-- he was one of the top men there. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

value.text: Constitution of the 3rd of May of 1922
Constitution of the 3rd of May of 1922

data.text:
INT: Tape seven of Mark Verstandig. We were talking the previous discussion about your time at university, and you talked about some of the far right-wing students and the trouble they caused. When you look back at the day-to-day experience with other students in general, was there any ethnic tension or religious conflict at university? 

MV: At university, yes. That extension was in Cracow. And the attitude of the Polish students against the Jews. But again, I wouldn't say the Polish students as a whole. I mean the so-called [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE], they were the troublemakers. 

The other Poles were indifferent. No, I-- I have-- I can't remember a Pole coming out in defense of the Jews. But they were indifferent, but they didn't take part in it. Whether they like it-- liked it or not. Maybe they didn't. But it was, like always, a minority of troublemakers, a minority to-- which was seeking power riding on the Jewish horse. And that was a very convenient horse. 

INT: Where do you think this prominence of the far right-wing students came from? 

MV: The prominence came-- to a certain extent, there was always trouble from the beginning of the-- from the beginning of the Polish independence. The Polish independence, as I mentioned earlier, was created-- I mean, the-- with the Polish independence, there were pogroms all over Poland. All over Poland. 

It does not mean that the population was eager to pogrom Jews. In Galicia, for instance, in our town, the population came in because they were told to-- the farmers were told that they will be able to loot, to get something. But it so happened they came for three weeks. In the third week, I could see the-- the-- the government sent in some military. And our balcony, I remember-- I was six years old-- there was a machine gun. 

And I remember that there were-- I have seen because I was on the balcony, as a child, a six-year-old child, and I-- I could see the-- like today, I remember it. There were three soldiers, and they were hitting the Poles with the butt of the rifle. Was not much. But then suddenly there were-- there-- they could see smoke coming out-- coming out from two different directions in the villages, enclosed by villages. From one side and from the other side. 

And within minutes, the Poles, the-- the peasant disappeared because they could see that somebody burned this-- this-- this-- their houses somewhere could be on fire. It so happens that [INAUDIBLE] it was later I heard that there were a few people, young people, who-- who were in the Austrian army. They were serving in the Austrian army, and they organized for the-- it was the third week. They organized some kind of a self-defense. 

They placed themselves during the night in a couple of villages. In one village on the north and the other on the south, for instance. And they put on fire a couple of barns. And that was enough to keep the Poles away-- away from-- from-- from town. There were no more pogroms whatsoever. In other towns, it was much-- this was worse. But the worst pogroms during-- connected with the-- in the Polish independence were instigated by army officers. Just like that, by army officers. 

For instance, in Vilna. In Vilna, they arrested a number of Jews. The army arrested a number of Jews. And they did the same as the SS did 20 years later, 23 years later. They-- they-- they took them out and told them to dig their own graves. 

A famous Jewish writer was shot. He was arrested together with another, the Jewish poet-- poet Yaffe. I don't remember the name of the Jewish writer. He was a Bundist. I-- I'll-- I could-- I could check on it. They shot him at that time. Yaffe was arrested. Yaffe was a Hebrew poet, a Zionist, a Hebrew poet. And eventually he was relieved and thrown. 

But there were pogroms in Vilna. In Lindbergh, in Lwow, there were terrible pogroms. Because the officers said that the Jews should help-- should come out against the Ukrainians. Because the Jews did not come-- come out against the Ukrainians, there were pogroms of Jews and there-- quite a number of Jews. A very big number of Jews were killed. 

So what I am driving at to say is that it was not the population of Poles. There were the instigators. So Polish officers in the army, mostly Polish officers in the army in the beginning. And later was the element-- the elements of the [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE]. They instigated Poles to do-- to-- to-- to do the same as Hitler did in Kristallnacht later. 

So that was the problem. The minority-- a minority of Poles poisoned the atmosphere. Take, for instance, the establishment of the Polish-- of-- of the Polish Republic. 

As you know-- as you know, the moment the Poles established themselves, Pilsudski marched against Kiev and conquered Kiev. You-- you-- you know about it. They entered Kiev. They went over there to Ukraine to establish the Polish boundaries from the 16th century, including the Ukraine as it should belong to Poland together with Kiev. 

Now, who went over there? The legionnaires of-- of Pilsudski, and they were to-- they were going together with Haller's army-- General Joseph Haller, Jozef Haller. Jozef Haller established himself a name in the Jewish history. They were killing and-- and beating up Jews and tearing the beards of the Jews out with the flesh. They did it in towns, in shtetls, and they did it in-- in-- in the trains. 

And what is interesting, they came to the-- Haller's army came to Poland under the leadership of French officers. But the fact is that the French officers did not take part in the pogroms. Now, Haller himself, his army, although Poland was a sovereign state, his army came in on the condition that it will not be under the Pilsudski regime. It will be like a sovereign-- like an independent-- an independent army, under the French officers. 

But the French officers [INAUDIBLE] didn't take back-- didn't take-- didn't pogrom Jews. Only the Haller-- Haller's army. That is well-known. In Poland, they used to say Haller chicken. That means they-- they-- they used to tear out the beard together with the flesh. 

Anyhow, that's-- but what is not very well-known is that Pilsudski made to use an alliance with Petliura. And you know-- you remember Petliura? Hetman Petliura. The man who was shot later by a Jew in Paris that he organized the pogroms, the most bloody pogroms in Ukraine-- in the Ukraine, and the French court acquitted him. He was a Hitler. 

And Pilsudski went to Kiev together with General Haller and together-- and together with Hetman Petliura. In the Polish-- matter of fact, in the Polish history you never-- they will-- they never mentioned the shameless story-- stories. I discovered it recently on the internet. On the internet. And it was-- the internet quoted-- it was an essay published in the Gazeta Polska. 

Wouldn't have-- if it-- if it wouldn't have been the truth, Gazeta Polska wouldn't have dared to connect Petliura with Pilsudski. Now, in the end, he left-- Pilsudski came back, and he left the [INAUDIBLE] over there in Kiev. You remember him? Rydz-Smigly. Rydz-Smigly was a Polish [INAUDIBLE] in Kiev. 

In the meantime, Budyonny marched on Kiev on the one side, and took [NON-ENGLISH] from the north, and Poland suddenly became squished, and they marched. They were on the outskirt of [NON-ENGLISH]. Polish independence was just hanging on the-- on-- on a hair. 

And at that time, the Polish government-- the prime minister was Witos, Wincenty Witos, The leader of the Piast. Piast, peasant party, or the [INAUDIBLE] farmers' party. He was the prime minister. He was from-- from our neighborhood. He was from Tarnow, from-- from a-- from a village close to Tarnow, between Tarnow and Mielec. 

And his deputy was Ignacy Daszynski, who was the leader of the PPS, of the Socialist Party of Poland. They issued an appeal, a-- an appeal through the people of Poland to the intelligentsia, and to the people of Poland. Whoever served in the-- in the Austrian army, whoever served in the German army, in Russian army, and feels that he is a Pole, that he is-- that feels that a Polish independence is-- is really in danger, he should volunteer-- he should volunteer to help defend Poland. 

About 10,000 Jewish intellectuals, most of them former officers of the Prussian-- of the-- of the Austrian army and of the Prussian army and of the Russian army, most of them. But there were some youngsters who never served in the army. They all volunteered, 10,000 of them. 

And what happened? They came to the point of-- to-- to-- to the-- to the point showing indicators that they should come over there. They all were arrested, and they were put into a concentration camp in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the first concentration camp of Jews, Jews who volunteered to defend Poland. 

As a matter of fact, here in Melbourne, if you go to the cemetery, you will see that some 15 months ago or something like that died a man, 95 years old, Dr. Handelsman. 

Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] Handelsman. He was there. He was a 20-year-old man, and he was there in this camp. Now, Dr. Handelsman was a scholar, was later a French citizen. I-- he showed me his photographs with the-- with-- with men in France, with-- with-- with-- with a number of Poles, a number of-- of French politicians and so on. He studied in Paris, in Sorbonne, and Sorbonne published a few books of his in French. 

Then before he-- matter of fact, before he died-- before he died he phoned me that he want to show me some documents. I remembered him, but I didn't remember his age. I remembered the name. I told him, all right. So come along. I live here and-- said, look, I'm 95 years old. 95 years old. I can't travel. Said, if you are 95, I'll come to see you. 

He shows me all the documents of-- all the documents relating to the [NON-ENGLISH], and all the correspondence he had with people like-- who were like him in-- in this [NON-ENGLISH]-- in this [NON-ENGLISH] concentration camp. After quite a time-- quite a time, a few years, they were released because there was an outcry in the-- in the whole world. So it was the Polish-- it was the-- the prime-- by the way, this appeal that they should come along, it was signed-- no. 

The document to arrest them was signed by General Sosnkowski, the-- the-- he-- Pilsudski's deputy. So you see, there was a kind of a-- of an anti-Jewish approach. They thought that the Jews are-- that-- that the Jews will-- will be in the army and will help-- will help the Bolsheviks. Now, that is how it started. 

Then Poland was already established. There was-- no more was-- was peace with Russia. And the boundaries were established. We are not in Kiev, but the-- but the borders were established. Everything was right. But then there was the Sejm. 

And Pilsudski realized-- at that time he was [NON-ENGLISH]. It means the-- he was the head of the state. And there was this constitution, and then he realized that the majority in the Sejm are always against him. The majority of the Sejm were the peasant party, the [NON-ENGLISH], and the [NON-ENGLISH], the Christian Democrats, the national-- the-- the antisemitic national party [NON-ENGLISH], and the anti-Jewish party that was the party of-- of-- of-- of-- of-- of the peasants-- what is-- led by Wincenty Witos. 

He realized that the majority is against him. So he withdrew. And the government was a government of [INAUDIBLE]. Later was the Grabskis. Yes. With the Grabskis-- with Wladyslaw Grabski and Stanislaw Grabski. One was a minister for culture, for education. And the other was minister-- the-- the other was a treasurer. And then they were in government, and they were anti-Jewish. 

And although the Constitution of the 3rd of May of 1922 was democratic and contained all-- all kinds of democratic stipulations, but it was on paper only. What they first of all did is to gerrymander through geography. 

I mean, this an example. If Mielec had, let's say, 5,000 Jews, and Tarnow had 40,000 Jews, and that is one close to the other, what they did is they joined Mielec with a different region where they were a few hundred Jews, where-- where Jews did not live. 

That means that this Mielec, Tarnow, Jaslo, and so on couldn't-- couldn't elect a deputy to the-- to the-- to the parliament because geographically, for-- for the sake of elections, [INAUDIBLE] constituencies was created in such a case-- in-- in-- in such a way that people that-- cities with Jews were-- were cut off, were connected with large-- with-- with large territories, villages, where there are no Jews whatsoever. So they were not able to elect anybody. Only, let us say, a few people here, Cracow, and where the Jews themselves had enough votes to elect somebody. 

And then came Greenbaum with an idea. This is what they did not only to the-- the Jews, but they did similar things to-- to-- to the Ukrainians. So Greenbaum, being a deputy, organized a-- a coalition between the Jews, Ukrainians, the Germans. 

All ethnics were joined together in a coalition. And they decided that if an Ukrainian will stand for election in a place where there are Jews and Ukrainians, the Jews will vote for Ukrainian. If the majority there are Ukrainian, the Jews will vote for Ukrainian. 

And in places where there were more Jews than Ukrainians, the Ukrainians will vote for the Jews. And the whole gerrymander collapsed. If Poland wanted to hide that 35% to 37% of the population are minorities, and in the [NON-ENGLISH] they committed themselves to give all the rights to the minorities. If they wanted to hide the problem that Poland consists of only 65%-- the most, 62%, 63%, the most 65% of Poles, and 35% are minorities. They wanted to hide it with-- through this gerrymander. 

Here suddenly the Jews alone came up with 35-- with 35 deputies in parliament with a number of-- with a number of senators. I mean, the real number-- the number of the-- of the ethnic representatives in the Polish parliament in the lower house and in the-- and in the senate really reflected the real population percentage in the country. It was something, you see, they didn't expect to. 

Then with that up, they came-- there was a-- there-- there was a-- there-- they came up with an idea of the so-called Ugoda, which means with an idea to make an agreement with the Jews. This happened on a Friday night. As I mentioned to you, there was in Cracow a Jew called Leon Selpeter, was a friend of my brother. Comes from [POLISH], was a pharmacist and a-- a great Zionist. 

He is mentioned, by the way, in Schindler's Ark-- in-- in Schindler's Ark as the man who gave Schindler letters to the Jewish Congress, the old Jewish Congress. He was in Schindler's Ark. 

So Leon Selpeter told the story secretly, in-- in-- in still secrecy told it to my brother. And-- and, oh, the story went up from one-- from mouth to mouth, houses-- from houses [INAUDIBLE]. It was a Friday night. And before Friday, Stanislaw Markowski-- Stanislaw Grabski. Stanislaw Grabski suggested to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], who was the leader of the-- of the-- of the Jews in Galicia. He suggested to him that he would like to come to see him on Friday night for so-called Jewish fish, for gefilte fish, which you will-- well, there was very-- they were-- the-- the Poles very much liked Jewish fish. 

So he invited himself to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] for the Jewish-- for gefilte fish. And Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] invited him and there was this great-- this-- this reception, the kiddush and so on, and the gefilte fish. But after the fish, Stanislaw Grabski came out with an idea that the Jews should make an agreement with the government. The agreement be Ugoda, the agreement being that the Jews will declare their-- you call it [NON-ENGLISH]. Their-- 

INT: Citizenship? 

MV: Huh? 

INT: Citizenship. 

MV: No. They declare their loyalty. They will-- should declare their loyalty to the Polish Republic in the borders which are now-- in the present borders, and in-- and for that, the government will see what can be done to improve the Jewish Polish relations. 

Surely Greenbaum was against it. And so were a number of Jews. They said first of all why should be the Jews singled out, that they should declare loyalty to Poland in the present boundaries? The present boundaries were disputed by the Ukrainians. That means they wanted to put a [INAUDIBLE] between the Jews and the Ukrainians. The present boundaries were not recognized by the-- by-- by the League of Nations. 

So why should the Jews be singled out? That's number one. And number two, the promise was nothing. We will see what we can do to improve the Polish Jewish relations. It was a great-- there was a great affair in the Jewish-- in the Jewish community all over Poland. 

There was the-- Greenbaum in Warsaw was against it, and there was Dr. [INAUDIBLE] who was always in [INAUDIBLE]. Galicia was always trying to-- not to fight, not to fight. To try-- to get out whatever is possible, although he never believes that the Poles will-- will-- will stick to the constitution. 

INT: What year was that in? 

MV: 1922, '23. 

INT: Thank you. We'll have to finish next tape.

INT: --eight with Mark Verstandig. 

MV: Yeah. Well, I'll tell you why it was. It must have been the end of '24. The reason is that one and a half years later, there was a bloodless putsch, that was this year. He went with his soldiers and officers of the parliament and sent the Endeks packing. 

And they took over. And the Prime Minister, at that time, by him established, was Mr. Bartel, Kazimierz Bartel. He promised a lot. But this Grabski business died with, with this putsch, anyhow. 

But, but what I am driving at to say is that antisemitism in Poland was instigated by the government, by leaders of the antisemitic parties, who manipulated the Polish population. And that has always been the case. 

INT: And was there any-- in your view, was there any role played by the Church in this? 

MV: The Church was always anti-Jewish. The reason is, first of all, that the Church, that the dominated elements in the Church were the village priests, which were not educated. They were really ignorant people, ignorant people. 

And they, they believed literally in what was written in the-- in the Bible. And they kept the Jews responsible for the crucifixion of Christ. They did whatever they could-- they did whatever they could to incite the Poles against, against the Jews. 

For instance, Good Friday, [NON-ENGLISH]-- in Poland, [NON-ENGLISH] was not a great holiday. It was a fast. But it was not the same as, as Good Friday in the, in the Protestant countries. 

But Jews were afraid to walk the streets of the shtetl or even-- or even of the-- especially of the shtetl. Because, at that time, the priests incited them that the Jews, up till today, until the end of the world, the Jews took upon themselves, according to Matthew, the Jews took upon themselves the responsibility for the blood spilled, for the Christ blood spilled by them, and so on and so on. 

And there was-- there was tension, tension. And it was not a pleasant atmosphere. But again, again, I would say the population, as such, was not very much moved. 

But there was-- there was this-- there was tension. The trouble is that it was Hitler who brutalized, to a very great extent, the whole nation. But up till the war, up till the war, I couldn't see. The only thing there were-- surely, there were pogroms. In Przemysl, there was the pogrom. 

There were in Radom and in many other places. But they were always instigated by people who wanted to have a pogrom. And the people were-- the people were very-- very easily manipulated when it was a question of looting. 

And that is-- that is what happened in Przytyk in 1937. The Poles, the, the farmers came along, the peasants came along, instigated by, by people who, who told them, they say, we'll have an easier life. They-- they will have an easy jobs, to make a pogrom and the-- the-- to, to, and to loot the merchandise in the, in the marketplace. 

The Jews used to have stalls in the marketplace with merchandise. So they said to them that they will-- they will have-- they will make a commotion, and they will grab whatever they could. 

But they never realized that the stall owners, young people, will turn around and will organize themselves, immediately, for a self-defense in Przytyk From the stalls, they grabbed out whatever they could-- poles and so on. And they started to defend themselves. 

And it was that moment, the people ran away. But being on the outskirts of Przytyk they were turned back by these inciters, by, by these manipulators, including Polish police. Go back. 

And this is how the pogrom started. They came back, right like that, and they started to kill the Jews. And when, in the end-- in, in the end, when they were hitting-- they were hitting the doors, they were trying to break the doors of one house, there was, upstairs, one Jew. He has-- he had a rifle. And he shoot and killed a Pole. 

And with that moment, everybody ran away. There was no more pogrom. There were-- there were quite a number of Jews killed-- a number of Jews killed. 

And they prepared a case for the court. The court was in Radom. A famous Jewish journalist and sociologist, Jakob Jasinszki, a great scholar, was, on behalf of the American press, he was recording this trial. 

I knew him very well, by the way. And so what happened was they did then-- the Polish prosecution said that it was the Jews who made the pogrom against the Poles, who came to town in order to feed the Jews. Because they came here to buy the goods and to-- to make possible that the Jews could make a living. 

And instead, they were hit by the Jews. And the end, there were a few people, one family-- one who, who, who killed the-- who killed the Jew and his wife and the children, I think. I don't remember exactly. He was sentenced to a few months, some months of imprisonment. 

There was quite a number of followers, a number of followers-- small number of followers, who were sentenced to, somehow, to some months of imprisonment. But the Jews who defended themselves were sentenced to years of prison, many years of prison. That was, that, that was true. 

But, but that's not-- that was in 1937. The main trouble came-- of course, as I mentioned to you, there were instigators. There were Falangists. There were Endeks. There were the Polish had a Chrzescijanska Demokracja, Christian Democrats. 

A Christian Democrat meant that Christian democracy, that democracy in Poland is only for Christians. That is what they said of-- what they said. I mean, we want democracy, but only for Christians. 

And not only Christians, only for Catholics. They don't want for the Ukrainians and so on, only, only for Polish Christians. 

But the main problem came in 1934. Up till 1934, there were-- it was antisemitism here and there. The Poles still behaved decently, with an exception of the, of the instigators. 

But in 1934, Pilsudski made a pact with Hitler. And too, it said that Hitler's influence on Polish politics, on Polish internal politics got hold. Then now after, the Falangists, they all started openly to be anti-Jew, to, to pay anti-Jewish excesses. 

And what's more, the government did not say no. Then came this new party. Pilsudski died a year later. New party, OZN, Obóz Zjednoczenia Narodowego, something like that. 

And this was already a fascist party. The government party became a fascist party. And after Przytyk, it was General Slawoj Skladkowski, who officially said, I will not allow any pogroms. But economic boycott is pleasure. 

[NON-ENGLISH] yeah, in Poland. Yeah, but how do you say in Polish? [NON-ENGLISH]. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. They used the excuse of [NON-ENGLISH], economic boycott [NON-ENGLISH]. It means it's, it's accepted. 

And it was that time they tried to make, they decide to really implement it. How could they implement it? How could they have implemented it? 

The only way was to bring Polish merchants from Pomerania, from, from Poznan, and this western part of Poland, where there always were antisemites, although this was the place where no-- where the Jews didn't live. That's very important. 

The antisemites, the real antisemites, Poles, came from the western part of Poland-- Pomerania. And Jews didn't live there. So they organized in a-- in my-- a migration of Polish merchants, from Western Poland, from Pomerania, to Galicia and to all other parts of Poland, to take over the Jewish trades, Jewish, Jewish, Jewish shops. 

Now, they did it with large credits. And I still remember, in my, in my, in my town, there were two cafes. They opened two cafes. This was people they, they bought from Pomerania, from Posen, from Poznan. 

They opened two cafes. And that was the first time a cafe put up a sign-- Jews are not permitted to come in. And I remember, Jews were just bewildered, going over to see this sign. 

INT: What year was that? 

MV: Huh? 

INT: What year was that? What year was that? 

MV: That was in 1937, 193-- end of 1936, '37. 1937. Either, there were-- this, this cafe, this restaurant, cafe did not do too good. There was another cafe, by a man from the same Pomerania, who did well and invited Jews. 

I used to go there. He was my client. He was my client. He was-- we, we-- he-- we did all, all the legal work for them. 

INT: Can we just go back, quickly, to the end of your university years. What year did you finish university? 

MV: Beginning of 19-- 1936, middle 1936. 

INT: And you mentioned earlier that one of the student parties was from the far right. Another one represented Pilsudski's. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: What were-- were there any other major-- 

MV: Sanacja. 

INT: Were there any other major student parties? 

MV: No. There were-- yes. There were [NON-ENGLISH], the peasant party. But they were quite-- they were not a large-- they were not a large, large amount of them. 

Mostly, they were either Sanacja or they were Endeks, or they were Endeks. As a matter of fact, in the Sanacja students, put it frankly, they were not people-- to put it that way-- in-- who would be friends of Jews. They were mostly opportunists. 

They didn't believe that the Endeks will take over the government. And they studied at the university, thinking that being Sanacja, being the organization of students representing, represented by the government, they will get good jobs. 

So they were mostly opportunists. So it was-- this is the, the party which they created later, the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the, the non-party bloc supporting the government. 

They were all opportunists, because they thought that being with the party, being with the party of the government, they will have better jobs. They will be better. They will be able to get a better loan from a government bank. 

INT: Were the Jewish students organized? 

MV: The Jewish students were organized. The Jewish students, we organized. And then we organized most-- first of all, there was the Jewish Student Union, [NON-ENGLISH], officially. 

For instance, m this beautiful house-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- which you would say, it was the student house. It was partly, quite in a large extent, funded by the university. The university pays a lot of money for helping, see? They're helping to establish it. They couldn't take-- according to the Constitution, they couldn't have done otherwise. Because they were-- there were similar houses for Catholics, and there were similar houses for Protestants, even, for-- 'cause, 'cause-- because they were German students. 

They couldn't say that they will not give some money for the Jewish students. So that meant that house was partly funded by the university. It was, just was a student union in Cracow called [NON-ENGLISH], 'wreath of fire.' 

[NON-ENGLISH], you would say, well, you would say, it is a fire. 

INT: Were you involved in the Jewish-- 

MV: Oh yes, oh yes. I was involved, but not in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was involved in the political Zionist parties. 

INT: What was that name? 

MV: The Zionist-- first of all, after the second year, I was invited to take over the academic-- the university students' organization, called [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the revisionist student Jabotinsky's group. 

INT: And what was its philosophy? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: What was its philosophy? 

MV: Philosophy was, it, it was Zionists for a, for, for a Jewish state, for a Jewish state in Palestine, for a large Jewish state in Palestine, against the, the immigration, the immigration-- against the English prohibition of immigration. 

It was, it was representing Jewish Jabotinsky's philosophy at the campus. I would have to start with Jabotinsky's philosophy altogether. 

It was, he, he-- Jabotinsky was the man who, at that time, traveled along Poland, from shtetl to shtetl, warning Jews that the catastrophe comes, that the Jews will not survive the next war, that the Hitler rise, the Jewish will not survive that. 

And he, he, he went. He made-- he went at-- he made him an agreement with the Polish government, because England did not allow to-- so they did not-- England did not allow the Jews to migrate to, to Palestine. There were very strict quotas. 

Quota was the, the so-called certificates or visas were issued through a Jewish agency, but there were a very small number. And then, you could go on as a capitalist, with 5,000 pounds or $5,000-- I don't remember the amount, the amount of money. 

And besides this, they didn't allow anybody to go in, to go in. Now, he makes an agreement with the Polish government. And the Polish government was only willing to help Jews go to Palestine. And so they smuggled him, at that time, through Romania into Palestine. 

Because in Palestine, on the other hand-- when they, when he arrived in Palestine, the, the illegal immigrant when he arrives there, he didn't need any papers. It's like here and England, you don't need-- you don't need any papers. 

They came there, and they disappeared in the kibbutzim. They disappeared amongst the Jewish-- amongst the Jewish population. Anyhow, a lot of people were sent illegally, illegally to, to Palestine through the Jabotinsky movement. 

And they were for a Jewish state at the time, when the Jewish agency, or the Zionist-- the main Zionist organization, they said that they would be willing for to settle for a Jewish entity within Palestine. 

Well, we said, no. We are not going to kill-- not going to kill anybody. We are going. We are going there to have a state of ours. And now, this was more or less there. And Jabotinsky traveled from town to town, calling on Jews. 

INT: What year was that? 

MV: 1936, '37, '38. 

INT: Did you agree with that prognosis? 

MV: If I agreed? Of what? 

INT: Did you think the same way, did you think that the disaster might come? 

MV: The same way. I thought the same way, but later, I changed my mind. I, at first, I thought that we-- we are going to stay here, and we are going to fight for our, for our rights, here. 

But this was when I was in that university. At the university, we are, we were then, at that time, fighting for a Jewish-- for a Jewish state against the English restrictions to go to, to go to Palestine. 

Now, I became the president of this organization in Cracow. And there were-- I wrote in my book about it-- that Haim Landau came to see me, that I should take-- so Haim Landau, at that time, went to Israel. 

He became later, as you have maybe heard, he became later the chief of staff of the Irgun during the war against England. And he was a member of the, a member of this-- of the Knesset, a member of the Parliament. 

And he was later a minister. He was one of the-- he was one of the greatest orators in Israel, this Haim Landau. So he came to me together with another delegation. 

As a matter of fact, I didn't remember the other people who was there, and, and maybe I told-- I'll show you later a letter from an English writer, a writer in London. Rafael here-- Rafael Scharf, Rafael Scharf. He was in this delegation just the same. 

Now, he went to England. And he, he lives now in England. And he's a-- he's a, a quite a known-- a well-known writer and intellectual. As a matter of fact, there is a book, written by Anne Karpf. Maybe you have seen it, you have seen this book. I got this book from the library. 

This book is called Another War. It is written by Anne Karpf. She is a columnist at The Guardian, Guardian columnist, an intellectual. And she was a child of survivors. Her mother was a concert pianist in Berlin, Philadelphia-- in the, in the Berlin Symphony Orchestra. 

Her father was a the consular at the embassy, at the Polish Embassy in London and defected. And the-- but, but they-- they-- she was a child of survivors. It's a very interesting book, because that's the only book with a deep knowledge of psychology. 

To see the chasm and the love, the love and hatred between the survivor, the child, with-- with-- between the child of survivor and their parents. It's a beautiful book. I think Felicity is going to review this book. 

It's here in the library-- Heinemann, Heinemann. But in the-- in the introduction-- in the acknowledgment, she thanks her father, Scharf, for his help. And in the second part, she writes quite a-- quite a few pages about this-- her father, Scharf, who was, at that time, the, the member of this delegation in Cracow, who came to me, who came to me that I should take over. 

The only thing is, you see, I went into hiding in Poland. And Scharf was clever enough. Haim Landau went to Israel to become later a minister. And Rafael Scharf went to England, where he studied at Oxford and served in the, and served in the British army and later became one of the intellectuals, the Jewish intellectuals in, in London. 

He is, he was one of the founders of the Jewish Quarterly. 

INT: How long, how long did you remain president in Cracow of the Jewish organization? 

MV: Until the end of my studies. Until the end of my studies, I was the president of the Jewish, of this Jewish organization. And there were other organizations. 

There were fraternities, [NON-ENGLISH] fraternities, the same fraternities as the Polish, as the Polish corporations-- [NON-ENGLISH] corporations, or fraternities. 

I used to, they used to invite me for lectures. And I used to lecture very often at the [NON-ENGLISH]. And interestingly, I received from a fellow, here in Australia, called Alexander, a photo-- a photocopy from a book, written about the [NON-ENGLISH.] 

And there, they claimed that I was a member. I must have been an honorary member. Yeah, I've got it here. They made me a member, but I was never a member of the fraternities. 

INT: During that time, at the end of your university studies, 1935, '36, as a young man of very strong Jewish tradition, background, did you feel that being Jewish was an issue in the day to day life around you? 

MV: Sure, it was an issue. We knew in advance it was an issue. But we accepted it. We knew in advance that the Constitution, giving us full rights, was just a fiction, though we accepted it. We accept. 

For instance, the Poles killed themselves to get honors at the university, because with that, with the honors, they will get better positions. The Jew knew in advance that he will not get any position, any position. 

INT: Why was that? 

MV: Because, because, because he was Jewish. And he couldn't get out of it. You know, the system in Poland was such that when you lived in a town, you were registered there. And there was a column stating your religion and your nationality. 

You could have said your nationality is Poland, but your religion is Jewish. You couldn't change your religion. You couldn't, even, even if you were an atheist, you were born a Jew, and it is written there. 

If you want-- and besides-- and because of that, you belong to the Jewish kahal, to the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] to to, to the Jewish community. And the Jewish community could impose taxes on you. 

And if you didn't pay the taxes, the sheriff, the government sheriff, will come and collect your candlesticks and sell them at an auction to pay the Jewish taxes. Now, you may think, if I am running away from my shtetl in Mielec to Cracow, I'll be able to say, I'm not Jewish. 

Can't do it, because according to the Polish regulations, if you-- if, if you went-- if, if you migrated from Mielec-- not only migrated, if you went for, for a holiday from Mielec to Cracow for four weeks, you had to fill out a slip, where it is written-- I, Mark Verstandig, nationality, Polish, or nationality, Jewish-- whatever you like to write, and religion, Jewish, or Jewish religion, and so on-- I'm going to Cracow. 

And you can't, when you came to Cracow, you had to come to the city council and fill out a new, a new slip, and there it was written, you showed. It is written there. The full slip said you are Jewish. 

You couldn't say that you are not Jewish, because the moment you are born a Jew, the only way of getting out of it was conversion. If you did not convert, you stayed a Jew. You could have shouted to heaven, I don't believe in God. You are still a Jew. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next time. 

MV: Next time? But we had to--

INT: Part 2, tape 9, May 12, interview with Mark Verstandig. Last tape we were talking about what it means, or what it meant at that time, in the '30s in Poland, to be Jewish. We were talking about, or rather you were explaining about the whole process of registration and how people had to have their identities in place. Can you tell us more, please? 

MV: Yes. [INAUDIBLE] to this, were in order-- were-- were designed to keep a check on-- on people, on people's movements. Since it's for that reason, that when one, whether it was a Jew or whether it was an Ukrainian or a Pole, when he moved from one village to another, from one town to another, he had to fill out a form, stating his religion, his nationality, where he lives here, and the-- the destination of his travels, and the address where he is going to be, where-- where-- where he is going to reside in the town and village of his destination. 

My opinion, it was a part of keeping a check on people, of movement-- of-- of-- of the movement of people. Now as far as-- as far as nationality is concerned, people had to state nationality, their nationality, because the Versailles agreement or treaty, which established Poland, took into consideration that at least 75% of the Polish, of the population residing in the boundaries of Poland, belonged to minorities. And there were Jews, Germans, Ukrainians, and the others. 

In some places, let's say in western Galicia, in western Galicia, there were places where the majority of the population were Ukrainians. And north of-- of this part were Belarusians and-- and so on. But every, though is the-- is-- the minorities were, at that time, also interested that people should state their nationality. But the government, who did not-- did not try to force you to state your minority national-- your-- your-- your minority nationality. They would rather-- they would have rather been pleased if the Ukrainian will state that he is of Polish nationality. So that could be changed easily. 

Now, however, religion could not have been changed, because the parish has got the register of Catholics. And the-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] the Jewish autonomic institutions, had the register of all Jews. If a Jew, as I stated, maybe, before you-- if a Jew was not circumcised, he could not be registered as a Jew. He could not be registered as a Jew in this register, which was-- which was held by an-- by a-- a clerk appointed-- appointed by the Jewish-- by the Jewish kehilla. I wouldn't say kehilla. The-- the-- Jewish-- here I would say a board of deputies, but it was not a board. It was a-- the-- the Jewish-- 

INT: Governing council. 

MV: Council. 

INT: Council of some sort. 

MV: Yes, it was the Jewish council. So he has to be registered there. And they would not accept him if he is not a-- if he is not circumcised. And the Catholics will not accept him because he is not converted. So he is-- he-- he-- he is as much as not born. He didn't exist. Therefore-- and-- and this state, this power of the religious minorities, of the religious minorities, was guaranteed in the Versailles-- in the Versailles treaty, together with the nationalities. But nationality you could change easily. 

Nobody will take-- nobody could-- could really force you to say today that you are of Polish nationality and tomorrow that you are a Jewish nationality or Ukrainian nationality. You could have-- you could change it any time, any time you wish. But as far as the religion is concerned, here you stayed. And the Jewish council had the right to impose taxes on you. And if you did not pay the taxes, the komornik, the-- the sheriff, an official sheriff of the court would take any-- any-- anything you owned, including the candlesticks, and auction them. 

They had the right to impose taxes. And you couldn't get out of it. Why? Because when you went out of Mielec to Cracow, you had to-- you had to fill out a form stating your religion and where you go. When you come to Cracow, you had to fill out a form that you arrived here, and you live here, and your nationality, and your religion again. 

Now, if you didn't-- if you were not taxed by the Jewish council in Mielec, you will be taxed by the Jewish council in Cracow. The only way out of it was conversion, no other way. And that was-- and for that purpose, the forms controlling the movement of the people were appreciated by the Jewish council just the same. Although, to a certain extent, when I look at it from the point of view of the-- of the situation, perhaps the situation of Australia or-- or in the Western world, I would say that, yes, that was one of the symptoms of a police state, keeping an eye on every person, on every individual in the country, on his movement. 

INT: Were these taxes that were imposed by the Jewish council-- 

MV: Yeah? 

INT: Were they instead of the government taxes or in addition to? 

MV: No, it's addition of-- it was in addition, and-- and their purpose was to keep the institutions of the-- of the Jewish community financed. They paid the rabbi. They paid the ritual slaughterer. They paid the-- the-- the Jewish judges, the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they paid for the upkeep of the cemetery. And they have got-- they take-- they took care of all the charitable institutions, of all the needs, of all the needs of the Jewish people. 

To a certain extent, you can say that they-- that the-- that the Jewish council governed the Jew from the cradle to the grave, because the man who-- who performed the circumcision was paid by the government, by the Jewish council. And you went to the-- you-- you had to be buried in the Jewish cemetery, which belongs to the Jewish council. So, in this respect, one could easily say that they governed the Jew from the cradle to the grave. 

INT: Was this a situation for the majority of Jews in smaller places like Mielec, or also in the large cities? 

MV: In the large cities, it was even better organized. Was-- was everywhere, was everywhere, and that-- that was not only in-- not only in Poland, but in-- in almost everywhere. As a matter of fact, I think that in Sweden, up till now, the Jewish council has got the right to tax you for all those purposes. And they tax you in accordance with your-- in accordance with your income. 

I've got a cousin in Sweden, who told me that he is paid more than the-- the-- people, than the most-- than the majority shareholders of Volvo, because they-- because they somehow can-- can cover their real income. Whereas he was an employee. He was working for the government. So they-- they-- he couldn't-- he-- he couldn't do anything. They just taxed him in accordance with his-- with his real income. Now that-- that exists even in Sweden. 

INT: In this context that we're talking about, the idea of Jews being different nationals from Poles, was there, in your opinion, a-- a conception of people of Jewish origin as being not Polish, before the war? 

MV: Jews consider themselves Jews of national-- of Jewish nationality, living in Poland, being citizens of the Polish Republic. And the Polish Republic committed-- was committed to respect the nationality and the religion of all the minorities. It was on that condition that the-- that the boundaries of the Polish-- of the Polish state and the existence of the Polish-- of a Polish republic was established on the strength of the-- of the Versailles treaty. Because after all 35% were officially minorities. 

So you can't-- you-- you-- you-- you can't impose nationality of-- and a Polish nationality on a Ukrainian who lives in Lwow. And he-- he thinks that, really, Lwow shouldn't be part of Poland. Or in Vilna, who thinks that-- that Vilna shouldn't be part of Poland. But Vilna was part of the Poland on condition that the people living in Vilna could express themselves as Lithuanians, Lithuanians of-- in-- residing-- as Lithuanians of Polish citizenship. 

INT: So is it fair to say that, in the interwar years in Poland, the Poles of Catholic origin-- 

MV: Yeah? 

INT: --considered Jews as non-Poles? 

MV: They considered Jews as non-Poles. But that doesn't mean-- that doesn't mean that they couldn't be citizens of Poland. Because the Polish state was established in boundaries which comprised different minorities. They had all the-- they all were given citizenship of the Polish. But they had the right to keep their-- their Ukrainian, Jewish, German, Czech, and Lithuanian nationality. 

INT: So some of the other approaches towards Jewish nationality, such as English or German approaches before the war, where Jews were considered English or German, was a foreign concept in Poland. 

MV: That was a foreign concept in Poland. Because, first of all, in Germany, in Germany, there was a very small percentage of Jews. Let's say half a-- 0.5% or 0.4% of population were Jews. And other minorities were even smaller. Well, that's German was-- was a country, which was inhabited by Germans, where Poland was a new state comprising different minorities. And this state was a subject on the condition that the minorities will have the right to live as a minority with full citizenship. That is the difference. 

INT: Is it possible, in your-- 

[BACKGROUND NOISE] 

--is it possible in your mind to give a general overview of the relations between Polish Catholics and-- and the Jewish people between the years? 

MV: Yeah, you may be surprised when I tell you that the relations, as far as I could see them, were very good. I can tell you that a Jewish hawker, or a Jewish peddler, dressed like an orthodox Jew, in a black caftan, with the hat and with his sidelocks and beard, could walk through villages safer than, for instance, here in Australia, an elderly woman traveling in the train at night. 

The relationship between the Jews and non-Jews were very good. They knew. They took it from the church that the Jews are responsible for the killing of Christ. But they didn't take it in this minute that they-- that because of that they should kill the Jew of today. But the problem was that the Poles were, to a very large extent, illiterate, especially the people-- especially the people in the villages, the peasantry. They were illiterate. 

They were manipulated from time to time. They were manipulated by a small gang of agitators, of-- of Falangists. Whether they were-- they named themselves Falangists at that time or not, that is not important.
