But this is, is that they can come to town and to-- and to loot, and to-- but not to kill, but to loot. And that was what the-- what happened really when Poland was established in 1919. 

The trouble was that the antisemitic excesses sdfd always manipulated from outside by a small minority. That's one thing. And the next thing is that the-- that the government was behind it. Whether officially or not officially, it was behind it, because the government wanted sooner-- the sooner the better that the nationalities should disappear, that Ukrainians should become Poles, or that the Poles should Polandize the Ukrainian territories and that the Jews should become assimilated. 

The symbol of assimilation of a Jew was [NON-ENGLISH] from olden times, who organized, in the time of-- of Kosciuszko, who organized this regiment to fight the Russians and the Germans and so on. Now, that was the idea that the Jews should assimilate. At that time it was not a racial problem. It was a problem of assimilation, that the Jews should behave, give up their autonomy. And-- and-- and-- and even the better, the best solution would be if they-- if they-- if they accept the Catholic faith, not the Protestant, but the Catholic faith. That would be the best solution for-- that would be the best solution to solve the minority problem and the Jewish problem. 

That later changed. During the war, for instance, the underground papers of the Polish so-called underground, said that a Jew who accepts the Catholic faith became a Catholic, but he is still not a Pole. That was the official-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the official policy of the-- of the underground, of this antisemitic underground. 

So this is what I am telling you, that the situation, the relationship between the Poles and the Jews were not bad. The only trouble was when outside agitators incited-- incited the Poles. And the best-- and-- and the best evidence is that a Jewish peddler could walk tens of kilometers, walking because he didn't have a horse. He just-- he-- he-- he just walked. And keeping-- and keeping in a rucksack his merchandise, which he is selling, or keeping in a basket the [NON-ENGLISH] or the-- or the goose, which he bought in order to sell it in the town. And he went-- you could see him from afar, because he was wearing this black caftan and his wide hat, this black wide hat, with his beard and the-- nobody were stoning. Nobody-- nobody harmed him. 

INT: Was this still the case right up to the end of the '30s? 

MV: No. No. No. No. It was not until the end of the '30s. It was the middle of the '30s, when Adam Adam Koz came to power. The Falangist behind him, [NON-ENGLISH], behind him, were-- were organizing, were organizing already, were organizing gangs all over the place, and especially in-- in-- in-- in the bigger cities, more in the cities than in-- more in the cities than in the rural areas. 

INT: Talking about the rural areas, perhaps we can finish a picture that you were describing earlier about the socioeconomic life of the village, of the shtetl. 

MV: The shtetl and the village-- yes, I remember. We talked about it, and maybe I didn't elaborate. And I said that in the village they were eating meat twice a-- twice a year for Passover and-- for-- for Easter and Christmas. Whereas, in town, the town people had meat every day. There was-- I really should have qualified that, because as far as the rural population is concerned, I referred to the farmhand, to the poorest of the poor. And as far as a shtetl is concerned, I referred it to wealthy people. And there were wealthy people. 

There were landowners. For instance, in our-- in our Mielec, there were at least 10 families, landowners having large estates around. Now, beside-- a little bit lower, there where-- there were business people, merchants, business people, entrepreneurs. They-- they-- they had logging rights in large and vast forests, which belong to the aristocracy. 

They had sawmills, where the timber was processed. There were flour mills. There were-- there were-- there-- there were wealthy people. So, the wealthy people really enjoyed themselves, and they had meat. And they had all the luxuries in the world. 

But you come down to the last-- to the one before the last. Because the last one-- the last one would be the beggars. There were a vast amount of beggars, going from door to door begging. On a Sunday, they used to go to around the houses to beg for the remnants of the-- of the Shabbos, of the-- of the bread and of everything what was left over from Shabbat. Because you have to know one thing. The Jew, at that time, in the shtetl, whether he was rich or small or that, the Jew lived for the Shabbat. 

When it came Friday night, the world, the-- the world of the diaspora disappeared. He lived in his own world in the kingdom-- in the kingdom of Israel, in his own family. He dressed himself like one for-- for a-- for a special occasion, with his best clothes. He went to the synagogue, and he came back. And the Jews saved every penny-- we're talking about the lower classes-- in order to prepare for the Shabbat, to have wine for bless-- to bless the wine, to have fish, and to have a piece of meat, and to have white bread. It is not because it is in-- it is in order to dignify the Shabbat. The Shabbat, like Heine explains it in his poem, was Shabbat [NON-ENGLISH], is a-- the Prinzessin of Shabbat. The Jews lived for the Sabbath. 

And then we were talking about the lower class. There were masses of beggars, who really couldn't-- who really didn't have the-- that didn't have anything for breakfast unless-- unless they-- they-- they-- they could get some charity. And there were not enough charitable institutions to help them. There were not like we have got here, the-- the kitchens, [NON-ENGLISH], Salvation Army. It was nothing, nothing existed there. 

So these were the-- the lowest class were the beggars. And there were plenty of them. There were 10 times, 20 times, 30 times more beggars than on the top-- than the top rich people. But take, for instance, the workers. Take, for instance, the artists. I'll give you one example. 

When I was a student at the university-- when I was a student at the university, my family, my father and my mother, sent me every week a parcel. And the parcel was left-- a food parcel. The parcel was left with a Jew who was a porter. Porters in the town was one of the Jewish professions. Again, I remember where he lived, in a dark-- in a dark flat consisting of two dark rooms, two dark rooms. 

People used to leave parcels in the room-- in this room. His wife was there. And he used to take the parcels to Cracow. This man had a railway ticket, a half-yearly railway ticket. Again, we called him Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] Birnbaum because he was dressed in a black caftan, beard and sidelocks. 

At 3 o'clock at night, he had a cab, a horse-driven cab, taking him to the train. He traveled from-- he traveled from Mielec to the [NON-ENGLISH], which took him an hour. There he had all these parcels around him, watching the parcels. He took the parcels down because he had to wait for another train. There was not a direct train into Cracow for an hour. Took another, later he-- he went to Cracow, another three hours. 

INT: Thank you. We'll have to continue a next tape.

INT: Tape 10 of Mark Verstandig. Yes, please continue the story of this porter. 

MV: Yeah. Now, in the train, at dawn, and first of all, he had to do-- he-- he could doze off a little bit, but he had to keep an eye on all his parcels, parcels around him. Dawn, he put on his tallis and tefillin and said the prayers, in the train. This gives you an example, too, that people were tolerant. He was praying like he would be in the-- in the synagogue. 

He came to Cracow. And there he had to hire a cab, a horse and cab again for the whole day. And he came with my parcel of food to the-- to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was hostile for the Jewish university students. 

The parcel consisted of a loaf of bread, a round loaf of black bread as big as a-- as a cart wheel, [POINTS] and a loaf of butter, at least 2 kilo, and hard cheese. And all this, he schlepped up to the third floor, with his-- with his black kaftan and this black this. He was sweating. He waked us up. 

And later, he collects it. He delivered all the parcels in the town. And he-- then he collected all kinds of parcels, which he carried for them, for the merchants and shopkeepers in Mielec. 

Well, considering that you spent, back and forth, 10 hours in the train, back and forth, with an hour in the middle, because four hours, it was the direct travel, but there's another in the middle is-- it is 10 hours in the train and 10 hours in-- on the street in Cracow. 

He worked 20 hours out of 24 hours from Sunday to Thursday night, five days a week, 100 hours, in order that he live-- to-- to-- to have enough to live in two dark rooms, which never saw the sun, in preparation, because he had to work for the Shabbos. 

When it came Friday night, you wouldn't recognize him. He was a king on Friday night. Now this was the poor fellow. And the majority, the great majority of Jews hacked out a living the same way as this-- as-- as-- as this Birnbaum. 

Shops around-- next door where I lived was a-- a corner store. And when you walked in, you could see a barrel of herring and a board on top. And they were selling herring by the centimeter, a piece of herring a centimeter. And this is what people live. The-- the-- the majority of people, who-- who could-- who did not beg and who were not very wealthy, were lucky to have a little piece of herring with a piece of bread and cottage cheese before they went to bed. 

And that-- and the-- and the staple food were potatoes. Again, the same type of-- of the-- the-- the-- the same as in the village. Now that, more or less, gives you a-- a picture of the strata, of the strata of the Jewish community in the shtetl. And that was the same everywhere. 

There were a few families, very wealthy, landowners, no matter how much they were mortgaged. There were merchants who really had a lot of money. And there were other shopkeepers and so on. But the majority, the majority were people working the same way as this Birnbaum. And a large amount were just beggars. 

INT: What about the people that were professionals in the larger cities? 

MV: Ah, the professionals were the-- were a-- were a-- they were as a-- a really on a very high standard. I mean the doctors, the lawyers. They-- they-- they-- their standard of living was as high as-- as the standard of living of the rich merchants. Yes, there were-- there were such a one, too. 

And the same applies really-- similar situation was you could see in-- you could see in the villages. The peasant, the farmhands were the poorest people. But then there were farmers, small farmers, which had their own plot of land, let's say, two hectares, three hectares. They had meat every Sunday. More-- mostly they had the rabbits, the domestic rabbits, which they killed for-- for Sunday. And from time to time, they killed-- they-- they-- they-- they killed a pig. And that was really a feast for months to go. 

Now these were the smaller farmers. And there were larger farmers, who had-- who really had a-- made a good life. And then there were the landowners, the aristocracy, the counts, the Poniatowskis and the-- the-- and the Potowskis and so on and so on. There were-- there were vast-- they had vast forests-- forests and-- and land. And-- and they-- and most of the time, they spent overseas in Paris. 

No, none of the Jews went to Paris to make a-- I mean, to-- to-- just for luxury. If you went-- if a Jew went to Paris, it must have been for some kind of business, but not just to sit in Paris and to live there on the strength of the wealth, which they have in Poland. 

INT: To finish the-- the socioeconomic picture, would you say that, in the period of the 1920s, 1930s, Jews who were in the professions, would they be one in 20 or one in 50 out of Jews? 

MV: I don't know. I don't know. Let's say, it wouldn't be a one in 20. For instance, in Mielec, I could tell you. And I don't have the statistics. Really, there could have been about maybe 20 or 15 solicitors, Jews who made a good living, and a few Jewish doctors. These were the professionals. 

On the other hand, you take-- and if you take the Polish population-- the Polish population in town were mostly public servants, whether they were employed by the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the district administration, or by local government. And the local government had to-- had the local government from Mielec, a local government, let us say, for a smaller village around. And then there was [NON-ENGLISH], which means, again, a district, a-- a district office for local governments. 

And they employed people. They bought a lot of people. And they had a lot of, again, intelligentsia, whether they were vets or doctors or solicitors. And some of them were merchants, just the same merchants and exporters. Then there were rich people, and there were-- and there were poor people. There was-- it was-- it's-- there were these people who were-- who-- who were the poorest in town. Well, there were such people, too. 

But no, but more or less, it was a-- it was almost the same proportion of rich people and small people. But in the-- in the-- in the between, there was this mass of public servants, whether they were government or local government. And this did not exist with the Jewish community. In this respect, they were much better off than the Jewish community at large. 

INT: It didn't exist because of what reasons? Why didn't it exist in the Jewish community? Why weren't Jews public servants? 

MV: The Jewish weren't public servants because the public servant didn't accept him. In our town, in our town, there were quite a number of people, who-- who were professional teachers, finished the teachers' seminary. Oh, they had higher education, but they couldn't get a job. 

As a matter of fact, my wife's cousin, who died two years ago, at the age of 87, in London, her daughter came now back to Poland. And that's a different story. But she had-- she brought with her, her mother's diploma, a diploma from the-- the Cracow University. She was a-- a scholar of classics, of Greek classic, Greek and classic Latin, Latin and Greek. But she couldn't get a job. 

She couldn't get a teacher's job, not only in a high school, not as a tutor at a university, but she couldn't get a job even in a-- in a-- in a primary school. They were just walking the streets. You wouldn't believe it, but if there would have been a dole-- there was no dole in Poland-- 90% of Jews in Poland, whether it was in shtetl, in Mielec, or in Cracow, would have been unemployed, ready to accept a dole, which didn't exist. That was the situation. 

In larger-- in-- in larger towns, let us say, in Lodz, there was a Jewish proletariat. And how this looks like, how their-- how their lives look like, it's enough to read Bashevis, not-- not Bashevis, Bashevis Singer's brother, this older brother, yeah, the-- his-- his book about this-- this 'Family Ashkenazi.' They describe-- he describes of this proletariat that exists there. 

As a matter of fact, yesterday, a friend of ours, a poet, Jake-- Jacob Rosenberg, launched his book. And he reminded the audience-- it was a large audience, maybe-- maybe 300 or 400 people. He reminded the audience that he was one of five, a family, one of five. And they lived in one room in Lodz one room in Lodz, a family with five children. 

His father never in his life had a new pair of trousers or a new jumper or a new shirt or anything new. He didn't buy any new things, only secondhand. But his three children, three of his children, he sent to private schools. 

See? He works his life, everything, to send the three children to private schools. But he never had, in his life, a new shirt or a new pair of trousers. That was the life of the proletariat in big towns. 

INT: Getting back to the public service or public jobs in Poland, government jobs, was this an official policy or unofficial policy of not hiring Jewish people? 

MV: Yes, that was-- couldn't be official, because it would have been not sanctioned by the Constitution. But that was the real situation, which existed, the real situation. I've got here a friend, in-- in Melbourne. He was a school friend in the Mielec Gymnasium and at the university. 

Now, his father-- it's very interesting. His father was a public servant. His father was a-- a professional officer from the Austrian Army, a professional-- a professional officer. When Poland was established, they-- Poland committed itself to employ and accept all public servants, all members of the army, of the Austrian, of the Russian, of the Prussian, into their new life in Poland. 

So his father was a public servant, an officer. And he was an officer and a-- a member of the-- of the Mielec [NON-ENGLISH]. He was in charge of the military affairs of the district. And he was a Jew. Very interesting. 

But after a few years, he was dismissed. But he couldn't be dismissed officially. He was put-- he was sent to the reserves, a reserve officer. And this [NON-ENGLISH] came to my father, who was a-- who was head of the Jewish council, asking him that he should give Mr. Rota-- he should give him a job as secretary. 

And every-- everybody in town was proud before that. That the only public servant in town is a Jewish officer, a Jewish member of the army, who walked around in his uniform. But he was dismissed, after a few years, to the reserve. But he was very much liked, and, therefore, he intervened that he should be given a job as a secretary of the Jewish council. 

And in this respect, you could see this, that it was official, because the [INAUDIBLE], himself was very friendly with him. He didn't want to dismiss him. But it was from above. And this is what I am pointing out, that the antisemitism in Poland came from above. Until later, it settled down. But it was engineered from the-- from-- from the people who were very friendly. 

You see? And he was a-- he was with the Jewish-- with the Jewish council. And his son is here. He's a friend of mine. As a matter of fact, I'm going to ask him to say something about-- to-- to meet you. 

INT: Let us now turn to the period where you finished your university in the mid '30s. 

MV: Who? 

INT: And let us talk about your period after you finished university-- 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: --in Cracow. What did you do for a professional life? 

MV: I was a lawyer. I was employed as a lawyer. I was employed as a lawyer. 

INT: In what year was that? 

MV: Or the article-- I mean it is called, here, article clerk-- article. But in Poland, it was called concipient. I was employed by Dr. J. Yakov Salpeter, Who was a brother-in-law of Frieda, my wife. And he employed me and-- in town. I used to go to court. And I didn't-- I-- I didn't-- I-- I didn't met any kind of anti-Jewish feelings in court. 

INT: What year was that when you started your article? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: What year did you start your articles? 

MV: In 1936. In 1936 I started my articles. And I was going to court. I was going to go-- but later, and later-- I mean, in the last, almost last year, after Adam Koc came to power a year later. So they started to send to the towns-- they started to send to the towns young judges, recruited from this Falangists, from this new generation of play-- not [NON-ENGLISH] and myself, of fascists. 

And this, there were a couple of-- of judges like that. And they behaved like fascists, not only in relation to Jews, to Jewish lawyers, but in relation to-- to Polish lawyers, who were in Poland, at that time, in my town, who were used to relate to-- to a nice, democratic relationship with everybody, with-- with Jews and non-Jews. It didn't matter. They were quite different people. 

And I remember that he used to-- I remember, asked us, with this new-- with this new idea of the fascists, to punish people, to-- for-- for the smallest-- for-- for-- for the smallest felony with high, with-- with-- with many months of prison and so on. And he behaved very rashly, very-- very-- very vulgar in relation with-- vis-a-vis all lawyers. 

And I remember a case. I describe this case. I remember a case when I had a Polish-- a Polish client, defending a Polish client. Normally, I had civil cases because Dr. Salpeter had-- he was known. He was a brilliant lawyer. He was known as a civilist, the so-called civil cases. But from time to time, there were criminal cases. 

So when there was a petty fellow, a petty felony, and I remember, he-- the-- the procedure was in Poland not like here. A procedure was that in criminal cases, the judge first tried, first of all, to interrogate the man who sent-- who was in dock, on the dock. And after the judge finished, it is the prosecutor, it was the policeman. The prosecutor was a magistrate. He was a prosecutor. He had this-- had this right to re-interrogate. And this ended with the-- the defendant-- the-- the lawyer of the-- of the defendant. 

And I could see, when he was interrogating this fellow, he is-- was dictating. That was the procedure. He was dictating to the minute what the-- what the-- the defendant said. Ask a question, and the question was minuted. The answer was minuted. But the answer was like the judge dictated to the clerk. And I could see that he is falsifying the testimony of the defendant. 

So I stood up and I said to him, I'm sorry, Your Honor. But he didn't say that. He said that. And why did you say-- well, why did you-- why did you say to the minutes, that he-- what you said to the minutes was not correct. He didn't say it. And the judge just ignored me. 

Question after question, he falsified the record. And then I stood up, and I said to the judge, Your Honor, I'm going to disappoint you. I'm not going to do like all-- like many of my elderly colleagues did, to leave the client and walk out because of your antics. You are falsifying the records. And I'm going to make an application that you should be removed from this case, because you are falsifying this. 

And at this moment, the prisoner-- the-- the defendant was not imprisoned, you see? But he could have-- he-- he could have imprisoned him. At that moment, I stood up. I pushed out the client and I said, go home. You'll be a-- there will be another judge who will be presiding, who-- who will be conducting your case. And I pushed him out. And I walked out. 

The same day, the afternoon, I brought my case. I-- I made an-- I made an application that the judge would be removed because-- and disciplined, because he was falsifying the record. Brought it in. You know, a fact was that he sent two-- two well-known solicitors, senior solicitors, to me, begged me to have a meeting, that he will do everything now. He will exclude himself. But that I shouldn't ruin his career. 

But that point that-- that is pointed out, that the-- that in the last years, in 1938, '39, the public service was already infiltrated from above with Falangists and fascists. 

INT: What were the names of some of these new judges? 

MV: Some of these? 

INT: Judges, these new judges. 

MV: I don't remember the name of the new judges. But I remember the judges. I remember the judges who were really models for democracy. I remember-- I mentioned in my book Kosowski. I remembered in my little book, [NON-ENGLISH]. These were judges of high, very high principles, who-- who-- who were ready to risk their lives in order to uphold the law. 

INT: Did they experience a lot of difficulty in those last years? 

MV: I don't know last year. But after-- after the-- after the war, they were honored. What happened to them later, I don't know. I tried to make contact with them, but somehow I could not. I knew that, let's say, Kosowski became later a high-- not a high court, but an-- a judge of the appeal, in the-- in the Court of Appeal. 

INT: What about in the years just before the war, '38, '39, did they have problems? 

MV: No, no, no, no no. The-- the good judges were-- were really-- no, as I mentioned to you, there was antisemitism that came only from above. It settled down soon because of the-- it was the years of-- of-- of Hitler. Hitler brutalized the masses. It settled down. But up till that time, the masses were really indifferent, not only indifferent, they were friendly with the Jews. 

INT: Did these particular judges have problems with their superiors in government? 

MV: No. I-- no, no, they didn't have problems with their superiors. Yeah, because besides this, the judges, by and large, in Poland, they were-- they were like-- like here, were independent. The Poles, the judges were independent. So they didn't have anything, not at what I know of, because they were, during the war, they were still working during the war. They were not-- they were not mistreated, whatever. 

But what they were doing is, when a position was vacant, they sent in-- the government nominated-- nominated judges from the Falangists and from the antisemites and from the national-- from-- from the index and so on. 

INT: Thank you. Let's continue next tape.

INT: Let us now turn to the outbreak of the war itself. Where were you when you first found out that the war started? 

MV: 

A day before-- a day before, I had a case in a town called Lancut. I was taken there by my client, Count [POLISH], who had an estate there. He had a car. He was very interesting man, large, tall fellow, who spoke perfect German, French. But he was dumb and deaf. 

He talked through the throat. You should have watched him, but he could express himself all right. He didn't need a-- he didn't need an interpreter for dumb and deaf people. Now, he had a case in Lancut, and he took me his sports car to Lancut. On the way back, it was night, and suddenly we could see-- that was the night of the 31st of August and the 1st of September. 

And we could see soldiers moving in darkness like ants. The road was-- the road was really congested. And suddenly we found ourselves in a ditch, in this narrow sports car. And the soldiers were helping, and they lifted the car out. And then he told me-- he told me that there's something wrong with the engine. But not far, he said, is the palace of Potockis, not far from Lancut. We will go there, and I will repair the car. It was dark, pitch dark night. 

We went over there. The-- the palace of Potocki. There was no Potocki anymore. They left a couple of days earlier to Paris. There were plenty of soldiers around. Anyhow, we went around. We found-- we found this kerosene lamp. A kerosene lamp. And we came back. He told me to hand-- to-- to keep the handle, to keep this light. And he took out his tools. He was an excellent mechanic. He worked for about three hours and fixed the engine. And we traveled to Mielec. 

Came to Mielec. In the morning when I got up, it was Friday morning. I woke up a little late. I could hear already the bombs on the-- how do you call this? Shop. There was this central [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a-- a factory making airplanes. There were the bombs over there. And that is how the war-- how I-- how I met the war, during the night, and next day in the morning. 

Immediately I went to the Stanislaw, where I ask to be nominated as the manager of Sadkowa Gora-- my father was an [NON-ENGLISH] man of Sadkowa Gora-- because we have to-- we have to do our work in order to feed the army. It was 1st of September. 

The harvest came in, and there's still not everything ready, were the-- the potatoes are still inside and so on, and others are-- we have to do a lot of work, and we have to prepare the-- yes, that I should be nominated the manager. And they told me yes. Immediately they nominated me the manager. And they told me that, look, you remember thing. Not only the army will have to be fed, we here have to be fed, too. 

And I-- I told them there that I'll keep an eye on them that they should be fed well. And I went to Sadkowa Gora. And that was how the wars-- how I met the war on Friday. A day or two days later, we could see already people start-- people moving along the roads in horse, in-- in carts, horse-driven carts, all walking. And they used to come to, then, to our estate. And we gave them food. And we gave them food for the horse-- for the horses too. But they moved and there. 

Well, they couldn't understand. But that-- the roads were now full of people moving, running, refugees running. After about four days, my brother and his family-- no, five days. My brother and his family from Cracow arrived the same way. On-- in a cart-- or in a horse-driven cart. They arrived in Sadkowa Gora. And a day later, we decided it's-- well, we asked where is-- where is the front? Says, nobody knows. 

The front is here. The front is there. The front is behind. The front is from the left side. Maybe in front of us. We don't know. People just walk and run. So we decided it's our turn. So we loaded two large-- two large vehicles, similar type as you could see in the United States. You see-- when they-- when they traveled to conquer the west. And instead of seats, you had this stool. 

And we put everything possible-- everything what we could, we put on these two. One-- one was driven by my brother with his family. And the other was driven by us. And I went over there. My-- only one of my sisters left-- was left behind. She was left behind and she took over that she will be-- she was not well-- that she will be in charge of Sadkowa Gora. Nothing will happen to her, and so on. 

This is how we arrived. And we were running in the direction of Przemysl. The reason was that in Przemysl I had a sister, and I have another sister in Mostyska, which is outside Przemysl, a little bit north-- a little bit east of Przemysl. On the way-- on the way we could see the Stukas. The Stukas coming down. We could see the youngsters just laughing their head off. And we always ran as much as we could into the forest, or we hid ourself under-- under the vehicle-- under the vehicle. 

And I will never forget a scene, which I saw at that time, and which really shocked me. I-- also we could see soldiers, soldiers marching individually like the refugees, saying that they are looking for their units. That their units were disbanded and were defeated. They are looking for their units. 

And the irresponsibility of these soldiers was something unbelievable. When a Stuka came, you could see the-- you could see there-- that there-- the youngsters, the pilots, were laughing, were laugh-- looking down so low. They could take, let's say, a rifle and shoot at this-- at-- at-- at this airplane. I don't remember how-- how these German airplanes-- the name of these German airplanes. Look, a rifle bullet couldn't do any harm to a-- to-- to-- to a fighter in the air. But they risk the life of other people. 

And then I could see one more thing, one thing which-- which shocked me. We were standing, there ready to go, after a raid, when two soldiers approached a couple of-- two young-- two middle-aged people. I could see they were Poles. I would say [NON-ENGLISH] Poles. Must have been public servants. 

They were walking, and they were looking at a map. We were traveling without a map. A map in Poland at that time was not a ready-made-- was not a-- a thing which everybody, you used to have a map. Not like today, everybody's traveling and everybody's got a map. Well, I could see a couple of people, Poles, with a map in their hands. The one said to other, I will-- we were on-- we were on already be on-- on another cart. 

Well, I could hear them. He said, look, they are spies. They've got a map. We were standing still there, and there were Poles. Somebody says, papiere, your papers. So they showed them the papers. And I could see that the people-- that one of them looked at the-- at this piece of paper upside down. He was illiterate-- upside down. And he said, yes, spies, all right. You'll go with us into the forest. 

And I said to a man, who was a brother-in-law of mine, who was the driver of this, said go. A few minutes later, we heard-- we-- we heard two shots. They shot two Poles because they were-- had-- they were handling maps. It shows you only the danger of people, illiterate people, having a gun in their hands, trigger happy. This is how it happened. 

And so we were traveling to Przemysl, but instead of Przemysl we went to-- we had to change it because people told us that the Germans are coming through the mountains from the south, Carpathia, through the south. And they will be earlier in Przemysl than we will be. So we decided to travel east north, instead of just east to Przemysl, east north. 

And now we traveled, and the same was everywhere. At one stage, you-- we met German tanks. But they let-- they didn't say anything. There was-- was a crossing. They were on that side, and we just moved, and they didn't say anything. We moved further. 

After quite a time-- after quite a time, we could see that it is quiet. As a matter of fact, in the meantime, I tried to-- I tried to cash some money, because before we left, Polish officers came to buy horses. And they had a-- I-- they had suitcases with money. And they offered-- they offered astronomic amounts for-- for the horses. I was very lucky that we will be-- pay-- taking such money. 

But my father says, I don't want the cash. I want from you only a receipt that I should cash it when I needed it. I have experience, he said. In the first World War, it was the same. I went to Vienna. I went to Budapest. I went to Bratislava. And wherever I wanted, I cashed the money. And they gave me another receipt for a lesser amount of money. He don't want cash. 

So I went to-- to wherever I came to cash money, there was no more a sign of Polish authority. At one stage, I went to-- I went to-- to cash some money, and there was bombs all around. And I came in-- I came in through the yard, which was full of ditches, full of ditches. And there were bombs all always. 

And there were two people there that said, look, you want money? They laugh. We haven't got money here. The people with the money already left this morning. You have to-- you have to find it a little bit further east. You will find them. And don't go around these places because they are bombing all around. And when I went back, there were bombs all around. I thought maybe all my people are killed. Nothing kept to them. I thought it is too late. We are going further. 

Now, it's-- this is how I describe to you the war, how it-- how it looks like. And then suddenly, we came-- and my father said, don't worry. In a few days time, the English and the-- and the French will settle the matter with the Germans. They will know that they are losing, so they will not be fighting. Everything will be fine. 

And then we could see no more Stukas. No more seeing people were-- were really traveling east. No-- we traveled northeast, but it was quiet everywhere. Very quiet. And my father says, you see? They don't have any more grease to grease their tanks. This is what the papers said it will happen to them. Their tanks haven't got anymore-- they-- they can't move further. We can't see a soldier anymore. We can't hear anymore Stukas. We can't hear the airplanes. We can't hear the bombs. 

And I remember it was Saturday, and my father said there is no more dangers. It's quiet. On Saturday, a Jew is not allowed to travel. Let's have a rest. And we had a rest. Next day in the morning, at dawn, my father was still standing next to the cart with his tallit and tefillin, saying the morning prayers. 

And I-- I said-- I looked around, and there are soldiers everywhere, Russian soldiers with Kalashnikovs. Well, I don't know where they came from. They was in a forest. From all sides within minutes, the-- the forest was full of soldiers. They said, you shouldn't worry. We came to liberate you. Very nice. 

And that was the 17th-- Sunday the 17th of-- of September, when the Russians crossed the border. And then we went back to Przemysl. You want to listen what's happened in Przemysl? We had to-- went-- went back to Przemysl. 

And in Przemysl, we had, as I mentioned to you, my sister was-- was there, and my brother-in-law. And another sister was in Mostyska. My mother went to Mostyska-- to Mostyska. We sent her, and we stayed-- we stayed in Przemysl with my-- with my sister. They had a-- a-- a wholesale grocery store, a large store. 

And were quite a few people employed there. Outside were the porters. Porters, as I mentioned to you, were Jewish-- was a Jewish profession. People came. They bought sets of whole different things. Then the porters were took away. Money came in. Money came in. 

And this is where I lived, where I lived there. And my brother-in-law, from time to time-- once-- once I remember he gave me-- he gave me a parcel with money, with-- with money. It was Russian money. And he told me to put it into the grand piano. When I came there, the grand piano was already full with money. But somehow, he had stocks and stocks. Who knows where? 

And one day, I didn't-- I didn't like it. By the way, it reminds me-- it reminds me why I was very sad and I was very depressed. It was a case of a man from Mielec. A man from Mielec, a certain Mr. [POLISH]. Herschel [POLISH], came to me and told me, look, I am in trouble. They arrested my wife and her two children. They were living in Israel, and they came to visit their mother on British passports. So the Russians arrested them here. 

And they didn't arrest-- he wasn't home, so they didn't arrest him. If I could help him. I told him, look, I'm not a registered lawyer here. But if you are in trouble, I will try to do my best. Anyhow, I went up to the prosecutor, prosecuting officer, Officer [NON-ENGLISH], prosecuting officer, and I told him, in Polish. I told them. I said, look, I am a lawyer, but I'm still not a member of the bar here because I don't know Ukrainian. 

But I will learn a bit of Ukrainian, and then I will register myself as a lawyer here. But I have got two clients. I've got a client here. And that is Mrs. So-and-So with her two children. You arrested them. You didn't have the right to arrest them. 

They arrived here on the way back to Palestine before you came here. They didn't need an entry visa to Przemysl because you weren't here. What they need now is an exit visa to Romania, to go on their way to Israel. What do you want from this? We are not at war with Britain. What do you want from them? 

He listened. He listened. And I was-- I spoke Polish and he listened. He understood what I have told him. He went with me to the window, and pointed suddenly out at the-- it's an officer outside. He said, look, [NON-ENGLISH] pal'to The officer wore a-- a leather-- a leather pal'to-- what do you-- 

INT: Coat. 

MV: A leather coat. A long, leather coat. Point. I understood what he wants. I told him that I will bring him a coat like that. So he pointed out to me, tomorrow at 9 o'clock I should bring him the pal'to. Or maybe-- I don't remember now exactly. I told him I will bring him the pal'to this afternoon. 

And-- and he told me, if you bring me the pal'to tomorrow-- this afternoon, something like that-- I don't remember exactly-- tomorrow at 9 o'clock or 10 o'clock-- he pointed-- he showed me this-- the-- the lady and the two children will walk out. I brought him the pal'to. And next day in the morning, the woman walked out with her two children. 

The same night, the whole prison was emptied. Everybody in the prison-- everybody knew about it-- was sent to Siberia-- everybody. At that time, I became very, very depressed. I thought myself, I was not anymore a lawyer. I was a fixer. I gave a man a-- a-- I made a man, a so-called prosecutor, who is no more than an agent of a bloody regime without anything, yet I-- I dirtied my hand bribing such a fellow. I never-- I never had to bribe anybody. 

That I would never do such a thing, I was very much depressed and walking. And I-- and I was walking around depressed for a few weeks. And suddenly one day-- one night I went home. I went home along the streets of Przemysl. I suddenly could hear a lot of people, too many people. 

I mean, it was congested like a-- I mean, something unbelievable. People were choking-- choking the street. So I tried to go into a side street, but the people from the side street were moving into the main street. And I couldn't go anywhere. I had to go where the people go. 

And suddenly I found myself at the sports stadium, with lights all over-- all over the place, and somebody talking-- I didn't understand it-- Ukrainian or Russian. And somebody was talking and talking to masses-- to the-- to the people all around, and it was Finlandia, Finlandia, Finlandia. I didn't know what-- whether-- whether it was an earthquake in Finlandia or not. I didn't know what happened. 

Anyhow, I went home. I opened the radio. And I could hear that there-- that the Russians are invading Finlandia-- are invading Finlandia. And next day in the morning, I opened the paper, and the paper said that spontaneously the masses of Przemysl came to protest against the fascists of Finlandia who provoked Soviet Russia, and so on and so on. I was among them. I was amongst them. This was how it looked when I was in-- when I was in Przemysl. 

INT: How long were you in Przemysl for? 

MV: I think I was in Przemysl until-- until April 1940. Until April, that's when I decided to go back. In the meantime, I went to visit my brother was-- was left on the way back. He didn't go to Przemysl. They stayed in Kovel. They stayed in Kovel, and they came from-- they came on business to Lwow, and I visited sometimes. I visited them sometimes. 

They came on business. They had-- they-- their father-in-law had a business of silver cutlery. Now they bought in Kovel silver, and brought it to Przemysl-- to-- to Lwow, where they had contacts, to make silver cutlery, which they sold to Russian-- all the Russian officers. That was their business. 

And it's interesting to know how it-- how-- how the travel looked like. We came-- we came to the railway station. The railway station was full. People were lying on the floor. And there were thousands of people. Everybody wanted to travel, because whole business, people made a living of traveling. They-- they-- they bought in one place a few kilos of sugar and sold it in another place. And they made enough money to live for a week or so. 

And the travel expense, I mean, the-- the railway tickets, were next to nothing, were very cheap. Or you-- or they bought yeast, a-- a few packets of yeast from one place to another place, and they could make a living out of it. So were thousands of people lying on the floor, and until the-- until the-- the little window selling the-- selling the tickets opened to sell the tickets. And after a while, they closed it, and people were lying again. 

Our people from Kovel knew the way how to travel. They came along 15 minutes before the departure of the train, or 20 minutes. They-- they were always-- there was always an officer in gray, they-- you call it. He's an officer-- an officer-- a railway officer in the-- in the hole. 

They went to him. One went to him, sold him something. After a while, the-- he came back and told him to follow him. And we-- we always-- we-- when I went there, we followed him and we went with the lift, down a corridor. We came out on the other side of the-- the railway lines. And there was a train standing on a dark-- on a blind-- on a blind line, a railway. And they called in the fellow there. 

They opened the door. And we went in. And we got our-- we got our compartment. We got our compartment. And so we could see it was following. Before the plane-- before the-- before the train moved to the right place, to the platform, half-- half of the-- of-- of the train was already full, was already full. That was how we traveled. It gives you a picture how it looks like, how-- how Russia looks like. 

And I decided that there-- that there's no use to stay with-- with-- with a regime like that. And I didn't go to register myself as a lawyer after this experiment. I didn't go anymore to register myself. I decided that I'm rather go-- I would rather go back. And I went back to Germany. 

INT: To-- you went back from Przemysl to-- 

MV: From Przemysl to Mielec. Yes. I-- the-- you want to know how I did it? 

INT: Well, we have to finish this tape. We'll continue later. 

MV: Yeah, OK. 

INT: Thank you.

INT: --Verstandig. Yes, we were talking about your departure from Przemysl in April around about that Pesach time, 1940. 

MV: Yeah. I-- Przemysl was a border town between the Soviet Russia and the Germans. I observed that not far from the station-- outside there was, there was an officer working, an officer working outside, was outside. 

There were a couple of Russian officers and three German officers. And people were registering there to-- people from the army, from the-- these invalids and people who were before members of the Polish army who had demobilized. 

I mean, their units would disappear. They were, that is like they were registering themselves to go back to Poland. Some were wearing uniforms and-- and remnants of uniforms. Others were in civil clothes, and so on. 

So Poles-- going back. I found among the papers, which we took with them a whole-- a whole suitcase of different papers, which we-- and then we emptied a desk before leaving [NON-ENGLISH] there were a lot of papers which belonged to the people employed in [NON-ENGLISH]. 

Among them, I found, I found an identity card without a picture. Most of identity cards in Poland were without pictures-- only a description-- how high you are, and so on. 

So I found, I had it-- I think it was some-- [NON-ENGLISH] or something like that, a name like that. The description was similar. The age was similar. So I took this, I decided that I'm going home that way. 

At the same time, the Germans, the Russians advise Poles, and Jews-- whoever were-- people who came as refugees-- that they should either register themselves to accept Soviet citizenship, or they could register themselves to go back. 

Now, Jews did not want to accept Soviet citizenship, because they were afraid they will never be able to go back. And the war may be finished in a few weeks' time, and they live in France, and England. The English, will start the offensive. 

The people didn't want to accept their citizenship. And I thought, why should I wait so long here, along here. The best way to go will be I will go as a Polish soldier or officer back to Mielec. 

So I took my, I took, I knew that I will have two suitcases, so I went over there, I registered myself to go back that time from a Polish unit, so and so. I felt if I used the name of-- I made sure the types of units was there from a division like that. 

They didn't make any, they didn't make any fuss. They just put the name on, and they asked me how many pieces of luggage we have. I told them, four. They gave me four tickets, because I had to plan how to do it. 

So I, in the afternoon, I was allowed on the train. I was on the train back to Mielec. But suddenly I realize that we are going-- instead of going west, we are going northwest, north. 

And I think it was in [NON-ENGLISH]. Wherever it was, I think it was a place like that-- I think [NON-ENGLISH]-- that they started-- Poles, on the station, when the station stops, they started to shout-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- People who are healthy will go to Germany for work, will go to Germany. 

And then I realized that the doors are locked. And I realize that in the last, in the last carriage were German soldiers. And the moment the train stopped, they all were down on both sides, watching the train. 

So I decided that I will have to jump the train. I took three, four tickets-- because I decided not to go to Mielec, with, with two, with two. I said, I decided that before I go to Mielec, I will have to give away my two suitcases in somebody else's trust and change the tickets and go down a station before Mielec or after Mielec and then go back. 

Because if I come back with two suitcases, everybody-- I mean, it will draw attention that somebody came from that side without, somebody came from that side on a false name. 

So I befriended a man next to me-- he was an invalid, a sergeant, an invalid. And I told him, look, I will have to jump train. Here are your two blank tickets. Write out your name, take-- take off mine. 

I, he was from Cracow. He gave me his address. I will come to pick them up. And I've got a dinner jacket, a smoking-- a dinner jacket in here. The dinner jacket will be yours. He was very, very pleased with me. 

But now the question of how-- how to do it, because people-- the-- the-- the-- the doors were locked. But I spotted that the south-- when I was-- but I could move the-- the window down. 

So I spotted that when the train makes-- slows down and goes around, it goes not straight, but it has to go, it has to make the curve, pieces of coal were being thrown out from the engine to be collected by somebody. 

So when the train goes like that, they threw out, the trains from the outside. So that people, that the, the soldiers from the last, from the last carriage couldn't see it. When it came the other way, they threw out the coals from the other way. 

Now, I decided that when the train, train will slow down, it will be evening, I will jump out then. This is how I did. I jumped out, I walked in the forest. Oh, there was a forest not far from [NON-ENGLISH] or something like that, not far from [NON-ENGLISH]. 

I walked out, and I didn't know whether it was [NON-ENGLISH] or not. I think it was [NON-ENGLISH]. I walked out, and I walked and walked for a couple of hours in the dark. I knew that the lights were there. 

The Germans-- although it was war, but it was far away. You see, the city lights were on. So I walked in the opposite direction. I walked for about an hour, an hour and a half or two hours until I found, I found a little cottage. 

I knocked the door. I told him-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- It was the common greeting in Poland. I told him the truth-- that I'm an officer, that I jumped the train, because they are taking the people to Germany, and I want to-- I want to, I want to sleep here for the night, and I'll go away tomorrow morning. 

And this is what I did. He-- he-- he-- he-- he accepted me. He accepted me. We were eating breakfast in the morning-- I told him, and I-- he-- I could see that he has got a daughter. 

She was 17 years old. And she looked-- my God, she looked like a typical Pole-- blond and blue eyes, you know, with this face. And I said to him, look, I'll tell you something-- why don't we take, you have a horse, the cart. Why don't we take this cart and you give me a lift to town. 

And for money-- I had money prepared-- and for money, you will have a nice meal and a good time, and a good time, and later, I will go. You'll go back. He says, it's a very good idea. 

That daughter dressed herself, you see, like for a Sunday outing. And I thought that-- how she looks like, it will be the best possible cover for me, that I am in their company. 

INT: How were you dressed? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: How were you dressed? 

MV: Just a-- a bit ragged, because of the-- I jumped the train, and I was a little bit-- I was-- 

INT: But what did you look like? Did you look like an officer? 

MV: Oh, yes, oh, yes. I looked like an officer. I was-- but I was dressed very-- very well, because I tore my jacket. You know, when I fell down-- when I fell down at the-- it was not-- it was not nice. I-- I looked like a peasant. I looked like a peasant boy. 

I was not an old man at that time. And that-- you know, I thought-- and I will avoid asking people the way to the station. They took me to the station. And I bought a ticket. I went-- I went-- in order to get, to be able to-- a line from the train, the station before Mielec, I had to change the trains in Przeclaw. 

I went down in Przeclaw, and I bought a ticket. It was for another train which moved through Mielec. And I could see this train arrived-- my train arrived with-- but instead of, let us say, 20 or 30 carriages, there were maybe four or five carriages, there. 

I could see this. My-- my sergeant-- my sergeant, who took my-- I could see him in the window-- I turned my head that he shouldn't see me, that he shouldn't see me. So that was how I came to Mielec. 

Then I came, and I came a station before-- about-- about seven kilometers before Mielec, I came down, and I walked. I knew that I didn't want to take a cart or something like that. I walked around seven kilometers, was how I came to town. 

And there we decided that the best way will be that I go to Cracow. There are a lot of Jews already coming from smaller towns, concentrating in Cracow. And there I will take a ticket. I'll take-- I will take over there a document that's registered in Cracow to go back to Mielec. 

And this is how it happened. I went to Cracow later, went to Mielec. From Mielec, I went by train to Cracow. From Cracow, I came back to Mielec, and this is how I started. 

INT: Why was the registration important in Crakow? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Why was your registration important in Cracow? 

MV: Because people-- I had to be registered. And people should not know-- the people-- the-- the German people should not know that I came back from Soviet Russia. So I had to bring a document that I came back from Cracow, because you needed a so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. 

In order to be accepted, you had to get a [NON-ENGLISH], a document from Mielec that they accept you after you came from Cracow. So I had to have such a document, and I received such a document in Cracow-- that I lived in Cracow and I'm going back, and I'm going back to Mielec, and I came back to Mielec. 

And this is how-- how I start. I mean, it was a small town, and people lived there quite, quite nicely. They were very, very, very good conditions. People-- it was not a closed ghetto. It was open, and people were engaged in-- in trade. 

And they had their shops, like, you see, nothing has changed. The only thing which-- which-- which was changed-- it was you had to wear this white band with a yellow-- with-- with this Star of David, showing that you are a Jew. 

INT: Did you wear that band? 

MV: Yes. I wore one. I had to. I wore one. It, it didn't bother me. In the beginning, it didn't bother me. It didn't bother me, and I started working. 

INT: And where was the rest of your family? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: Where was the rest of your family? 

MV: The rest of my family, at that time, was in Przemysl, Wyszatyce, and in Kowel. 

INT: And your father was in? 

MV: My father was in Przemysl. My mother was-- my mother, with the two sisters, were in-- my mother and two sisters were in Wyszatyce. And later, and at a later stage, my father went to Kowel. 

The reason was my father changed place with my mother. My mother went from Wyszatyce-- my mother went from Wyszatyce to Przemysl. And my father went from Przemysl to Wyszatyce. 

And there he was with my-- with my sister, [NON-ENGLISH] Regina. And at that time, my sister in Wyszatyce and my brother-in-law were considered as Zionists and as capitalists. 

Of course, they, they had a factory making roof tiles. And the moment you had a factory, you were a capitalist, so they took away your, your-- they took away your factory. 

And they took away your house and place you in a slum, because you are a capitalist. And so they lived there in a slum. And there later, and my brother-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH] started to work as, as a roof tiler. 

He had to replace roof tiles on-- on the roofs. And later, they decided that they would-- the moment they got their passports, as workers, they decided to leave the town where they knew, they knew them as capitalists and as Zionists. 

So they went to Kowel, to live in Kowel. And they took my father with them and-- and my-- my-- my sister. They took them to Kowel with them. 

And it's interesting that my brother, who lives in Kowel didn't know about it-- that they are there for a time. But later they-- he didn't know that they are there. 

But later on, it so happened when they were-- my-- my-- my brother lives in Kowel, and he didn't know that my father moved to Kowel, because the father used to be in Wyszatyce. 

In the meantime, my brother was arrested with his family and with his father-in-law-- his whole family, because they registered themselves to go back to Poland, so they sent them over to Siberia. 

The train was sending there, and I learned later that my-- my sister came to the train, because it was not like they say, that the Poles say, that they were transported like the Jews to the gas-- to the gas houses. 

It's not true. They were quite comfortable in this-- in-- in-- in these carriages. They-- each carriage had a stove and had water. There was no-- was no problem. They, they traveled the same way as the Russian army traveled. 

But my sister came along and told them, look, you are going to Siberia. Everybody knows about that. Why don't you leave your children with me? There where Louise, and the boy, Wilhelm. 

They said, leave the children with me. She-- and the girl was 4 years old, and the boy was 8, 9 years old. She said leave them with me. I'll watch them, and I will-- I'll take care of them like my own children. 

But my sister-in-law and my brother said that where we go, our children go with me. And they didn't want to leave the children. That's a dangerous-- they go to Siberia. It gets cold. They said, look, they are going with us. 

And the fact was that my sister perished with her children, and my brother survived in Soviet Russia with, with his children, with his whole family. 

INT: And your father was still dressed in the traditional Jewish way? 

MV: Yes. There was no reason in Russia not to. There were Russians going with beards. It's how they looked, and so on, the traditional way. And they-- they stayed when the Germans came. 

And later, we decided-- they wrote us that they would like to come back to Mielec. Now, we-- through a middleman, we found a German officer-- a German, not officer, employee-- a German with his own car. And we paid him. 

He didn't want the money in advance, even. He said, when I bring your father and your sister, you will pay me. He was a very nice man, and he had to go by car to Kowel to bring two Jews. 

And then my father shaved his beard, But he still had a little beard. Shaved him, and he brought him. And he got him, and he said, he behaved in a-- in a-- in a-- in a way which showed that he had nothing to do with the Hitler regime. 

INT: Do you remember his name? 

MV: Beg pardon? I don't remember his name. I don't remember his name. He behaves in a very-- in what-- what-- was-- what-- what hit me to start with, he said, you don't need to pay me in advance. When I bring your father, you'll pay me. 

INT: What time of the year was this? 

MV: I don't remember. Couldn't-- 

INT: It's 1940. 

MV: That was definitely in 1940. At the end, could have been the end of 19-- it could have been the end of 1940. 

INT: So this was already a year after the war started? 

MV: No, no no, no. Just a second. It was not the end of '40. It was in '41. It was in '41, because at that time, the Germans were already in Kowel. And this is how he could have traveled to Kowel, because there were no more Russians in Kowel. 

Was-- must have been by the end of 1941. Not by the end, must have been maybe September, 1941. 

INT: So at this stage really, the war had been running for two years? 

MV: Beg pardon. 

INT: The war was going on for two years at this stage. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: Did your father still have the same optimistic views about the end of the war? 

MV: No, I never discussed with him the optimistic views. I never discussed, I never discussed with him the optimistic views. But I don't know what happened to him. 

But when I ran away, before when even there was this-- when there was this talk about something's going to happen in Mielec, and I run away to Polaniec a day before-- was on Saturday. And on Sunday-- on Sunday morning, the [INAUDIBLE] let it be known through the remnants of the-- of-- through the acting president of the Judenrat, because the president of Judenrat went with me to Polaniec. 

He ran away with me to Polaniec. He let it be known that they were right-- there were something to be happened. But if he-- if-- if we collect so much and so much money, everything will be OK, like it was before. 

Well, the Jews collected some money. And everybody was-- was-- was full of joy that everything will be fine. And then it happened that my father came to my wife and told him, look, send somebody quick to Polaniec that Mark should be back as soon as possible, immediately. 

Because if not-- if it-- if it will-- if it will be known that he ran away, he may not be able to carry on as a solicitor. I can't understand it. He's tried to convince her that I should come back, because I will-- I broke the law running away. 

I was aware that something happened to his way of thinking-- that I will not be able to, to carry on as a solicitor-- but because I broke the law running away, I should come immediately back. 

No, it was-- I'd never had a discussion with him about his optimism, even when he could see what's going on. 

INT: And going back to the beginning of the war, was the outbreak of war a surprise for you and your family? 

MV: The outbreak of the war was not a surprise. People knew that something will happen. But people knew at the same time that something could be, that something may happen which will avert the war. 

[NON-ENGLISH] said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- we will not give an inch, we will not give a button from a-- from-- from-- from-- from-- from a coat. We will not give anything. But maybe there is something there will come. 

Something will happen to avoid the war, because we didn't believe that Hitler is-- at that time, is ready for-- is ready for a war. So we-- we were optimistic that something may-- may still happen to avert the war. 

INT: In that period when you came back to Mielec, summer, 1940, what did you do? 

MV: I was working as a solicitor. And by the way, I must have been the only solicitor in Poland working openly as a solicitor, because the Justice Department, which means the Department of Justice-- the Justice Department of the Generale Governement issued an order that Jews should not be allowed to practice. 

And a Polish solicitor is not allowed to employ a Jew, not even as a cleaner. Under no circumstances, should be employed, because they were afraid that under the guise of a cleaner-- of a cleaner, of a servant-- the Jewish solicitor, the Jewish lawyer will be working together with Poles. 

So that is when that was known. Therefore, when I came to Mielec, not a single Jewish solicitor was working-- was working. And I-- I contacted Antoni Droba, who was a partner of Leon Jacob Selpeter, my brother-in-law. 

And he was working in his premises. I contacted him if he could give some work to do outside, to-- to do illegally, but privately, from my room. 

And he answered to me, look, Freda Edda, that was, she was a secretary. There was a typist and secretary, she is working in the office, although she is not allowed to, working quite openly. 

And you come and work quite openly, and not a cock will [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: No rooster will crow. 

MV: Huh? 

INT: No rooster will crow. 

MV: No rooster will crow. People will think that you are allowed to, that you have got a permit. People don't know. Only solicitors. Only solicitors know that a Jew is not-- is not allowed to work in a solicitor's office. So you come along. 

When I came along next day-- the same day, when I came along, Droba took me to the court. We went to the-- we-- when-- when we entered the court, he said, take off this white band. 

And I took off the white band, and we went in the court. And I was greeted by-- this shows you there's antisemitism. I was greeted by the people all around-- by the solicitors, by the clerks, by the cleaners, by everybody like a friend. 

And as a matter of fact, I was introduced to one fellow, a tall, young fellow-- Dr. Frank. But he's not, not Frank, the-- it was another Frank. He's not, he had nothing to do with the Governor Generale, the, the Generale Governement. 

A doctor, he was a Reich worker working as a solicitor and barrister in Poland, at that time, in Mielec. But it so happened that he was the son of Dr. [INAUDIBLE] Frank, a surgeon, residing in Mielec, who came to Mielec before the First World War. 

He was respected by all the Jews in town, and he was in the best-- the best relationship with the Jews-- the Jews in town. His son went to school with Antoni Droba. They were school friends. 

But later he went back to Vienna to his relatives, where he practiced over there, and he married over there and became a Reich worker. Now, he came to Mielec, because you know, it's much easier for food and for all of those things to be together, to be in a small town like Mielec, where his father was the mayor. His father was the mayor of the town. 

So he introduced me to him. Ah, yes, I remember-- I went over there, and I had my armband still on. And it was this Dr. Frank that said, take it off. Here, in this court, between us, you are our colleague and our friend. And I don't want to see you with that here. 

INT: Thank you very much. We'll continue next tape.
