INT: Part three, take one. Running tape 13. 19th of May, 1997. Interview with Mark Verstandig. Last tape we were talking about the beginning of your period working in [NON-ENGLISH] as-- officially as a lawyer. 

MV: Yes, it was-- it was almost officially. As a matter of fact, at that time, as I mentioned before, employing a Jewish lawyer was penalized with death. Even as a-- even as a cleaner, a Jewish lawyer couldn't have been employed by a-- by a-- by a Polish lawyer. 

And interestingly, may I tell you that in Warsaw, in Warsaw, the head of the bar was a well-known antisemite, a lawyer who was known from before the war, that he tried to exclude Jewish lawyers from practicing law in Poland. 

But when the Germans issued this decree, that a-- a Polish lawyer should not employ a Jewish lawyer, the Jewish lawyer has no right to-- to practice law, he-- the same-- the same fellow-- I don't remember his name-- he said, no. This is not on our agenda now. We will not going to accept such a discrimination against our Jewish colleagues. 

And he employed a Jew, or-- or some Jews, and so did others. And soon the Germans cracked down. And he and a number of-- of Polish lawyers-- I think about 80 or 90-- were sent to Auschwitz. At that time, Auschwitz was not as dangerous as a year later because some of them came back. 

There was still no gas chambers at that time at Auschwitz, but it was a very dangerous concentration camp. And many times within a few weeks, the ashes came back. At that time, the administration of Auschwitz were-- played-- played a game. They were sending back-- even the Jews-- sending back the ashes that-- they regret very much that the prisoner such and such died of pneumonia, natural causes. 

Well, I'm pointing it out that at that time, there were-- the Polish antisemites in Warsaw, the lawyers, were defying the German-- the German order not to employ Jews. But later, they stopped, after-- after about 80 or 90 lawyers were sent to Auschwitz, that was the end of it. 

In Mielec, it was different. It was a small town. And I must have been the only lawyer in Poland practicing law. I used to go every day to court. When I came to court and I entered the courthouse, I used to take off my-- this white band. It was this-- star of David. 

And the judge, if I was representing, would say the-- the-- the defendant or the plaintiff. And the judge [INAUDIBLE] would say to-- to the minutes, on behalf of the plaintiff of [NON-ENGLISH] Droba. That was the Pole, my senior partner. So as far as the minutes are concerned, it was Droba and not me. 

And that went on for almost to the time of-- to the time of the expulsion of Jews, of the liquidation of the-- of the Jewish community [INAUDIBLE]. At that time, may I tell you, that I was, otherwise, very friendly with the judges. And there were a few judges who were excellent lawyers and they enjoyed just discussions on law, like, in-- in the yeshiva [INAUDIBLE]. 

Just kind of a [INAUDIBLE] and so because Poland had two or three different-- three different laws. There was the old law, the Russian law, the German law, the [INAUDIBLE] law, and later, the Polish law, which-- the-- the new Polish law. But some still existed before, and we used to discuss the differences in the wording and so on. 

I was very friendly with them. And a very interesting-- a very interesting incident developed, which had some bearings later, very important ones. The number of judges in Mielec were the same as before the war, but the cases were very few. Very, very, very, very-- there were fewer cases than-- than-- than before the war. 

And there was a [INAUDIBLE] that the [INAUDIBLE] department-- the-- the-- the department-- the Justice Department [INAUDIBLE], of Dr. [INAUDIBLE], will dismiss some judges, and they sent them in to the concentration camp. Of course, if [INAUDIBLE], you wouldn't be nice. The best-- the best way was to-- to send them to Auschwitz. 

They sent them to a concentration camp. And we discussed this matter, the danger to the judges. And then I came up, privately [INAUDIBLE] in the chambers to discuss it with-- with the Judge Kosowski. I came up with an idea. 

I told them, look. [INAUDIBLE] the-- the Justice Department, [INAUDIBLE] Justice Department is looking at the numbers of cases, but not at the cases itself. What about if I supplied, every day, a number of fictitious cases? 

The procedure is such. You supply a case, and then the court asked you to-- ask you to pay the fee within seven days. If you don't pay the fee, the case-- you-- the whole case goes [NON-ENGLISH], to the archive. And there it lies for three years. And after three years, it's out. It's off. 

So I told them, look. We will supply, every day a number of cases. They will get numbers, current numbers in the index of cases [INAUDIBLE] were issued. And you will ask-- you will ask us to supply the money to supply the fee. We will not supply it within seven days. 

So it will go as [NON-ENGLISH], but the number will be there. And they said-- it was a conference of the judges-- it's an excellent idea, and-- but you shouldn't spread it all over. I-- we will be-- our-- our office will be the only ones supplying those cases. And we used to supply 10, 20 cases a-- a day. Didn't cost us nothing. 

Didn't cost us-- couldn't cost us anything. They ask for money. And as far as the Jews are concerned, there was still another-- another point, namely, if a Jew issued a writ or a Jew was sued, a translation of that writ should be submitted in German and sent to the government, to the-- to-- to-- to-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- to the Justice Department. 

The reason for that was that the Jews had to submit a list of all their-- of all-- of-- of all their assets, including-- including claims to the Justice Department, so that they should know the-- all the jewelry, everything what they had, so they should know how much the Jew is worth. 

Now, there was a-- there was a kind of a fear-- the Germans feared that the Jew may cover his assets by submit-- by asking a Pole to claim his assets. And then, he will not defend it, and the assets will be transferred to the Pole and-- a trusted Pole-- and they will keep it for him. 

Well, the Germans wanted to know about it. On the other hand, if a Jew choose somebody else, another Jew or a Pole, the government wanted to know-- the German government wanted to know whether this claim was submitted beforehand. 

If it was not submitted, they will immediately seize it. And they will enter as a plaintiff against the Pole. Well, that was a kind of-- of controlling whether the Jew-- whether the Jew submitted all his-- a list of all his assets. 

Now, what they wanted is-- whether a Jew was a defendant or-- or-- or a plaintiff, a translation of the writ should have gone-- should have been submitted to the German government. So many times, when I-- when I ran out of names, I used to submit-- I used to submit Jewish names, and I used to be myself sometimes as a plaintiff. 

For instance, I-- I issued a writ against Mr. so and so that before the war, I lent him 100 zloty, and he didn't' return it to me. Therefore, I ask the court to issue a writ that he should return me the 100 zloty with interest. I should have submitted the-- a translation, but I did not. I should have paid the fee. I did not. 

And the judge, the court ask-- the court ask-- the office of-- of Droba to submit a list, submit a translation, and to pay the fees. We did not, and it went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And so it went on. 

The court of Melbourne-- the court of Melbourne was working on a-- on-- on-- on a number of nonexistent-- of nonexistent cases. And then something happened. Namely, there was a-- a colleague of mine from the same high school, [INAUDIBLE] Greenberg-- was the family Greenberg. He finished the high school. He didn't study, wasn't a scholar. But he somehow learned the trade of making artificial wine. 

You could make wine, artificial wine, made out of yeast, sugar, and essence of wine, from the same-- you-- you-- you pour it into a-- into a large barrel, and poured in water, a certain amount of yeast. That was the secret, a certain amount of yeast. And a certain amount of sugar. 

The yeast brought about the-- the fermentation. The-- the sugar brought about the fermentation-- the fermentation of the yeast, and the yeast, the fermentation of the sugar. And the sugar changed into alcohol. And later, after a certain-- after a certain amount of days, the alcohol was-- there was enough alcohol already. I mean, the whole sugar was turned into alcohol with the-- with the-- with the yeast. 

And then was a question of-- of coloring and the question of a certain essence to make it Bordeaux, or to make it a Mosel, whatever you wanted. From the same barrel, you could have a red wine and you could have white wine. That used to be a Jewish trade for many, many centuries. 

Somehow this-- the-- the brother of this fellow, Greenberg, marries into a such a family, and thus he didn't go to university. He went to his brother, he works in his factory, and he learns a trade. And then he came back to [NON-ENGLISH] and there he engaged-- he made the-- he-- he made a company-- he makes company with a former teacher, a professor of gymnasium, [INAUDIBLE] a certain Mr. Zbreschavski, Professor Zbreschavski. And he opened his factory making wine. 

And they didn't have [INAUDIBLE] capital, so they found another Jew, a certain Mr. Leipzig. And he sold his jewelry and [INAUDIBLE], and he put in the money. And so, it was a company made by Leipzig, Greenberg, and Zbreschavski, and they started-- they started to make artificial wine. 

And after a while, after a good few months, they were producing thousands of liters of wine daily. And German lawyers used to take away the wine and pay the money. It was very difficult to recognize, but when a Mosel from this barrel then a Mosel from-- from-- from a German-- from-- from a German winery. 

Anyhow, and that went on for a good few months until one day this Mr. Leipzig came to the-- came to work. He worked [INAUDIBLE]. And Mr. Greenberg, that is the friend of my-- my-- my-- my colleague from the gymnasium, told him, look. I think we don't need you anymore. You can go. 

Said, what do you mean you don't need me anymore? I'm a partner. He said, you are not a partner. You are a worker. Out you go. And he called in another couple of Poles, and they threw him out. In [NON-ENGLISH], he knew that the [NON-ENGLISH] could hear the conversation because he was in the other room. 

When he went, [INAUDIBLE] Zbreschavski, and Zbreschavski said to him, look. I'm sorry. I don't know anything about you. The manager of this factory is coming back. He's-- and he said-- and he said to me this morning that he doesn't need you anymore. 

He said, but I'm a partner. I don't anything about that. He just pushed him away. But this company, of the-- the partnership, was [INAUDIBLE] in a contract. They came because they couldn't go to a-- to a non-Jewish lawyer, but they went to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], who didn't work as a lawyer. I-- as a matter of fact, I-- I-- it was before the war, and after the war, the moment I got some money to pay for it, I was living in their flat, [INAUDIBLE]. 

And he prepared this document that they are partners. And if there is a dispute between them, it will be resolved in an arbitration. So this fellow came to me and asked me if I could help. He has got the document that he's a partner. I told him, yes. I think I could. We'll ask Droba if he's willing to accept a case like this. 

I talked to Droba. Droba says, why not? So we wrote a letter to this Greenberg and to Zbreschavski that our client submitted a case to us. And there's a-- should be an arbitration. So he's nominating [NON-ENGLISH] Droba as his arbitrator. And we ask him-- we ask them to submit another arbitrator on their behalf. 

And if they can't resolve, they will choose [INAUDIBLE]. And if they can't resolve this [INAUDIBLE], they will go to court, as if this-- as-- as-- according with the law. And we want an answer within seven days. Surely, they didn't answer. 

But on the seventh day, when I went down from work, Mr. Greenberg waited for me, and he [INAUDIBLE]. He put his arm around me and said, look. We received this letter. And we threw it out, just in the rubbish bin. And we would like to ask you to keep your fingers clean out of this matter. 

You know, you're playing with fire. We are supplying the German army. I didn't say anything. But went to [INAUDIBLE]-- to Droba, and we wrote him another letter. And we asked him that-- which was, by the way, hand delivered. And we asked him that within 24 hours, we wanted a reply whether or not they are going to. 

Now, they didn't-- they didn't answer, so we issued a writ to the courts. It said, issued a writ that we-- that this is in accordance with the law, that the-- that the court should nominate an arbitrator on their behalf. That is the law. So the writ was not for anything more, but that the court should-- should-- that the court should nominate an arbitrator. 

Now, the question was, at that time, of a-- of a translation, if we put a translation in. Within seven days, the German government ruled-- immediately put-- [INAUDIBLE] confiscates the whole business and put Greenberg and Zbreschavski into jail. 

So we asked-- I asked Kosowski that he should give us time to submit a translation, not within seven days, but in 30 days. And the reason being that that is not a writ for a claim, that the writ is from an arbiter, whether there is a claim or not, we still don't know. After the [INAUDIBLE], after the-- after the arbitration court will issue-- will issue the judgment, then before we-- before we give it to-- before the court will acknowledge it and send it to the sheriff, then we will issue. 

They-- they agreed with me that it is [INAUDIBLE] times or 30 days, and we issued the writ against them. Now after a couple of weeks, they came along, made an appointment, came along with Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. He was a very nice man, very nice man. They came along to negotiate, and we started negotiations. 

In the discussion somehow this-- this Professor Zbreschavski, who used to be a very nice and-- a very nice and decent-- decent fellow before the-- he somehow said to Droba, [INAUDIBLE]. That's what you have done with this writ. It's blackmail. And before the war, I will make you accountable for that. 

Droba jumped up, opened the door. He said, you [INAUDIBLE]. You made an agreement with another Jewish fellow thinking that because he is Jewish, he will cover you up. But you are both highway robbers. And under the-- and-- and under the protection of the German law, you wanted to rob this fellow. You robbed this fellow and threw him out. And I've got a document for it. You are telling me this, and he said, now, get out of my-- of my office before I kick you out, or before I just give you a kick out of it. And he threw him out. 

To make a long story short, within another 10, 12 days, before the-- before the-- the matter was finished, they came along, they paid some money. They paid a hell of a lot of money to this fellow. They paid him back, and they paid him-- they-- they paid him a part of-- of the profits. 

And I knew that the only thing they will ask will be to return them the letter, the contract, which [NON-ENGLISH] produced, that he's a partner. Because after the war, they will say that it was blackmail. Now, before-- I knew that he will go-- that he ask him. But when we went-- we went to a-- to a-- to a-- photographer, trusted photographer, [NON-ENGLISH] and he made-- there was no-- there was no photostat, copy machine. And he made a photo out of that. And they came, and he sent in Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], and they want it back. 

And so we gave them back-- we gave them back the document, and they paid some money. But that was not the end of the case. A week later, I was in court during the morning. A Gestapo fellow came in. He wanted to discuss some of this with Dr. Droba. 

Dr. Droba invited him to his office. And he immediately said to the secretary-- a Jewish secretary was there. He said, Frieda, and sent her to-- just to disappear, because he's not allowed to employ-- but she didn't look Jewish. 

So she disappeared. She disappeared, and they were sitting in his office [INAUDIBLE] in-- in-- in-- in his office with the Gestapo. The Gestapo told him, look. I've got a report that a certain Dr. Mark Verstandig is working with you. And that is against the law. 

He said, no. He doesn't work here. He comes here from time to time. We have a conversation. We are good friends. We have a conversation, but he doesn't work here. Now, while he was talking, somebody opens the door. There was nobody in there-- in-- in the first floor where the receptionist was sitting-- of course, she disappeared. 

So he went through, opens the door, opens the door, looked in, and saw Droba. He didn't see the Gestapo man. And he said, can I speak to Dr. Verstandig? And he said to him, Dr. Verstandig doesn't-- doesn't work here. What do you want Dr.-- what do you mean he don't work here? I was yesterday here. I gave him a case.

MV: Now the Gestapo officer understood Polish. He must have been a Volksdeutsche. So he started to smile. He said, look, Dr. Droba, I didn't come here to make trouble. Think about it. We'll see each other. He-- he gave to undersatnd that he want a bribe. 

The same day, we sent in somebody, a certain Mendel Bloch. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was really making trousers. But somehow he-- he had some connections with the Gestapo. He was a little-- he was-- he was-- he was a fixer. He went to this fellow and he came back, said he wanted curtains for his house. We-- he want-- and he gave him the measurements, and the curtains were made. 

And he's let it be known to Droba that Dr. Verstandig should not work otherwise as he did up till now. Should-- he-- he should not-- he-- he shouldn't be employed in any other-- in any other job as-- I mean, other than he's employed here. This way he gave to understand that I should continue the work. 

And that was almost the end of 1941, almost the end of 1941. I think December. 

Maybe it was-- it-- it was November. Anyhow, a few weeks later it was already December. It was a cold day. In the morning it was already dark. Dr. Droba's brother, Brigadier Joseph Droba, came, knocked at the door where I lived. He's-- opened the door. He said there is trouble. Run for your life. 

The Gestapo is here. Representative of the Justice Department here. They came to investigate that you are going-- coming to court. You're attending court cases every day, and you are conferring and sitting with the judges in the judges' chambers where other Polish lawyers are waiting in queue for five minutes, and so on and so on. There is trouble. We may all go to Auschwitz, and you must disappear. 

I was very-- I was very confused, and was very scared. He-- but I rushed after him, called him back. And said, look, I've got an idea. 

If the court personnel, the judges, and the secretaries, and the cleaners, will testify that I used to come to court but in my own cases, and not as a lawyer, everything could be saved. And they will find in the index my name many times, either as a plaintiff or as a-- or as a defendant. That-- that's a good idea. I told you about these fictitious cases which went into-- which went into the index. 

He said it's an excellent idea. We'll ask Dr. Frank. Maybe he could help too. And the fact was that they came to court. And after court about Dr. Verstandig coming to court every day, he said, no. Not every day. He used to come to court from time to time, but in his own cases, and not as a lawyer. Here's the index. 

They looked up the-- in the index, and that was not an index in the-- I mean, an artificial thing. It was a real index. Every few months, every few weeks, I had a case, my own case. And Dr. Frank-- I mentioned it last time about Dr. Frank. He was a [GERMAN], and he was a friend of Dr. Droba. And he was very friendly with me. He was there among this Gestapo, and he talked to them in German. 

He said, look, I've seen him many times here, but as-- in his own-- in his own-- in the capacity either of a defendant or in-- in some cases-- and they listened, and they very-- Dr. Frank, a [GERMAN], is-- is-- is-- is confirming what is written in the index. So they said, sorry. Must be a mistake. Somebody must have seen Dr. Verstandig coming to court and thought that he is representing, that he is-- that-- that he is working as a lawyer. And they went away. 

And in that way, the judges were saved. They could have gone-- the whole court could have gone to Auschwitz, including Droba. But the whole court could have been sent to Auschwitz. And that was what this young fellow Greenberg did. He did-- first of all he sent the Gestapo, and when the Gestapo-- when the Gestapo didn't do anything, he made-- he-- he denounced us. He denounced us to the Justice Department. 

To conclude this story, because I am talking about this fellow Greenberg, he got-- he got his punishment later. Belzec wasn't ready yet. Should have been-- Belzec wasn't ready yet.

And after a few days-- we were already in Polaniec. After a few days, they reappeared in Staszow, a town about 20 kilometers or 18 kilometers from Polaniec. A larger-- a larger town, almost as big as Mielec. 

And there, they were walking. There was no ghetto there. The situation was like similar was before-- like-- like before that in Mielec, like before the expulsion. There was not a closed ghetto there. Now they were walking themselves nicely on the main street, and a Gestapo car passed. And in this Gestapo car was a Gestapo man called Jeck. 

This Jeck was a-- a-- he went-- he was a Volksdeutsche. And he was our colleague in the Mielec gymnasium. He was much younger than I am. I didn't even know him. But later he became an executioner, and his-- his hobby was to hunt down his colleagues, his Jewish colleagues. His Jewish colleagues.

INT: '41 or '42?

I had a room-- I had a room not in my house, together with Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] who was the president of the Judenrat. He gave me one-- he had a large-- he had a large flat-- a large flat. He gave me one of his rooms. 

And from that room, I was preparing briefs for other lawyers, not only in Mielec but for other lawyers in [POLISH] and in other small towns. And I made more money working from home than even while I was-- while I was working with Droba where I was a partner. 

But I would like to tell you a very-- a very interesting period-- a very interesting case, which will show you the spirit of a Polish judge. 

A case-- a fellow came up to my place-- to-- to my-- to our-- to our office. I don't remember his name. He was-- he lived in-- not in town, but in a village. And he was a well-known horse trader. An expert in horses. A horse trader. 

And he had a case. He went to buy a horse from a-- a-- from an estate. And as he didn't know whether a Jew is allowed to buy a horse or not, he took a friend of his who was a German, a Volksdeutsche, in a neighboring village in German. He took with him that he is going to buy this horse in his name. 

So they went to this fellow. He bought the horse. And-- and-- and he didn't take him-- he didn't buy a horse like you see this-- also to sign a contract. But he said to the fellow, he is buying the horse. But he paid for the horse. And he took the horse with him. 

And a few days later, this German came to us. So he told him, look, it's such a fine horse. I don't think that you should keep this horse in your stable. It's not-- you know, you haven't-- you haven't got such a nice stable. Besides this, there are fellows around. They-- they will-- they will be jealous that you have got such a nice horse. I will keep this horse for you in my stable. 

So the Jewish fellow was suspicious. Said, look, a horse is a horse, and a stable is a stable. It's you-- I take the responsibility. Nothing will happen to this horse here. A few days later, he came with another few fellows and he removed the horse by force. He said it's my horse. 

And the fellow came to me and told me, look, he grabbed my horse. What should I do? I said, look, that's a hell of a-- you have a hell of a trouble because if you issue a litigation, you have to issue at the same time a German translation-- a German translation, and the government will take you-- the-- the government will take that. So said, doesn't matter. If the German government will take it, the government will take it. But I don't want him to take it. He is a cheat. He was a friend of mine, and he's a cheat. 

If you could make-- if you could make a-- if you could issue a writ against this German. I told him, look, I will have to ask Droba because it's a German involved. And Droba said, why not? A case is a case. Especially when you issue a German translation. So you issue a German translation, you are [INAUDIBLE]. 

So we issued the-- we issued the case against this-- against this German. And before the case, Droba told me, look, I am not going to represent this fellow in court. There is a lot of pressure behind the scenes. I think we should wash our hands of this case. We are not going to represent him. And we should tell him that he should drop it. 

We told him, drop it. We are not going to court. Said, I will go to court myself. I don't need a lawyer. If you don't want it, I'll go to somebody else. Nobody want it. So he went in by himself to court. The judge was Kosowski. Young judge from before the war. I knew him very well. A very nice man. 

And he brought in a-- the German brought in about 20 witnesses that he intended to buy the horse, that he borrowed money from this Jew, and he took the Jew only as an expert on horses. He should be an expert on a horse, so you buy a good horse. To look at the-- the legs and so on. An expert. I brought him as an expert. 

And all they said. We knew that he went to-- everything was-- 20 witnesses. And he had a lawyer. The name of the lawyer was Dr. [POLISH], who was an anti-Semite from before the war. But he never showed up openly. And he never said anything against me representing people in court. Also he knew that I am not allowed to. So he behaved quite all right. But we knew that he was always an antisemite. 

So the judge, Kosowski, called in Dr. [POLISH] to the-- to a-- to a-- to a conference. And he told him, look, this fellow hasn't got a lawyer. Nobody wants to represent him. I have got an idea, said Kosowski. 

Said, if you agree that this case should be stopped for-- this-- this-- kind of an agreement, we stop the case for three years. Now, the position is such. The case is stopped ad-- ad infinitum. If after three years it will not be started again, that's the end of it. This fellow, the German, has got the horse. Well, on three years' time, we will know what's going to happen. 

So this is-- you should agree that we should stop the case, and nothing will-- I mean, your-- your client should be happy because he will have the horse for the time being, for the next three years. But this [POLISH] sees that he had so many witnesses and so on. He said to him-- he said to the judge, I'm sorry, Your Honor, I want judgment. I am not agreeing to stop the case. I want a judgment in this case. 

So the judge put on his hat that was-- that was the procedure. This was the judge hat to-- to pronounce the judgment. And he said to him, [SPEAKING POLISH]. Means the plaintiff won the case. That's his horse. The plaintiff won the case. 

All the people left, and [POLISH] makes an appeal. And in this appeal, he wrote, no more, no less. That it is amazing that the judge issued a judgment like that against the evidence of 20 Christian-- of 20 Christian witnesses. Against the un-sworn evidence of the plaintiff-- un-sworn evidence of the plaintiff-- where it is known that the Jews are perjurering themselves in Polish court. 

And Kosowski had immediately called in [NON-ENGLISH], I think. The [NON-ENGLISH] or somebody else, and they photo-- they made a copy-- photocopy of this-- of this appeal. The appeal went to the higher court in Tarnow. The higher court send it back to Melbourne. And another judge didn't-- didn't need to issue a judgment because in the meantime, all the Jews were expelled or killed. Mielec was no more, and there were no more Jews. 

But that was not the end of it, because after the war the-- Judge Kosowski dug out the document, the appeal, where he said about the Jews and about him. And he submitted this-- this appeal to two instances, one to the bar that he should be struck out as a solicitor, the other to the prosecutor that he endangered the life of a Polish judge who-- who gave judgment in accordance with-- with his conscience by issuing a-- this. 

The end was that he was imprisoned for eight years, but he died in prison. This Dr. [POLISH] died in prison. So it shows you only about different kind of Poles. One was Judge Kosowski, who stuck out his neck in order to-- to issue a judgment in accordance with his conscience where he knew really that a-- he can pay with his life for it. Yet were a few cases-- about the court cases-- about the court cases in Mielec. 

INT: At this time, at the beginning of 1942 when you're working from home, what was the situation in Mielec with regard to the Jewish question? 

MV: The situation in Mielec hasn't changed. From the beginning-- I mean, from the beginning there were-- the beginning was very-- was-- was very cruel because the Germans burned down the synagogues, and together with the Jews-- and with Jews they pushed in over there. 

But later in Mielec, the Jewish life in Mielec was almost-- almost as the Jewish life-- as the Jewish life in Mielec before the war. Situation-- the only thing is you had to wear-- you had to wear a band showing that you are Jewish. But this-- I may just tell you an interesting story. 

We had an estate, a large estate, called [POLISH]. I will show you here the pictures of this manor house over there. 17 kilometers from Mielec. And mentioned to you that my father left-- when we left in [POLISH], he left my sister, [POLISH], Berta, he left her in charge of [POLISH]. 

After a few weeks, she was thrown out by a family called Muller. Very interesting family because the brother of this Mrs. Muller was an appeal judge in Krakow, Jeck, and he was a good friend of my father. In a high-- very high position, appeal court judge. 

And her nephew was a Gestapo man called Jeck who was-- whose hobby was killing Jews after Mielec was-- was-- after the Jews in Mielec were expelled. So they threw out my sister. In the beginning, they sent her some parcels to Mielec, but that was all. But later they stopped it too. 

When I came back from Przemysl, I studied-- I made a study of-- of this German law. On what ground they confiscated the [POLISH] and put in a [GERMAN], which means somebody-- a trustee, a [GERMAN]. And the [GERMAN] threw out my-- my-- my sister. I studied this law, and I came to the conclusion that the German Landrat-- which means the local authority-- broke the German law. He had no right to do it. 

And I decided that I am going to make an appeal to the government, to the general governor in Krakow that they broke the law, and my-- my-- my sister should be reinstated in [POLISH]. But I thought before I am going to send out this appeal, I will consult a solicitor, a certain Dr. Eisenberg. 

I mentioned, I think, Dr. Eisenberg before. He was an old lawyer, a very good lawyer, who originated from Galicia but studied in Germany-- studied in Vienna. Studied in Vienna. I knew that he-- that-- that he-- that he's a good lawyer, and he knew German. And I wrote this appeal in German. 

So I came-- I said I'll go to consult him. As a matter of fact, I went over there and I saw this Dr. Eisenberg, an older man, maybe he was 65 years old. To me it looked an old man. And he was-- I met him outside. He-- he didn't live in the Jewish quarter. He lived outside the Jewish quarter. And-- but there was no closed ghetto. You could live wherever you wanted. 

And I saw him with his arm band in the German-- in the star of David. And I looked at him. He was-- looked to me a very old man. It was the first time that I regretted coming back. In the beginning, I thought, oh, I'll wear-- I'll wear this band. But I could see-- I could see the indignity. I could see this humiliation of this man who was an assimilated-- an-- he was 100% assimilated Jew. 

Yeah, I saw-- and I-- first time-- and I looked at him. I-- I already regretted that I came back before because of that band. I went with him to his room. I told him the story. And I showed him what I have written, two or three pages. 

And I was waiting that he should congratulate me on my writing. And I could see that something is wrong. He looked above me away like you would-- like he was embarrassed-- like he was embarrassed-- like he was embarrassed to look at me. I could see that there is something wrong. 

In the end, he said, look, I must congratulate you. You wrote an excellent appeal. Your German language is perfect. And your argument, he told me, you could have-- you could have done it in-- in Berlin or in German or-- or-- or in Vienna. But the trouble is that the court of-- the old court of Vienna doesn't exist anymore. You are playing with fire. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: Tape 15 for Mark Verstandig. You're talking about Dr. Eisenberg. 

MV: Ah, yeah. Eisenberg. Now, he told me, look, the Germans didn't break the law, he said. I remember when they burned the Jews alive in the synagogues, they used to say in Mielec that there were unruly elements in the German army doing such things. 

Unruly government-- an unruly governments in the German army is a fiction. There are no unruly Germans. They broke the law, but they had orders to do it and they did it in many, many Jewish towns. And they did it because they wanted to tell the Jews, keep your mouth shut. Be quiet. Otherwise you will finish up like them. 

And that was a lesson to the Poles. What we did to the Jews today, we may do it tomorrow. But the Poles still didn't understand this lesson of burning people alive in the synagogue. 

What do you mean, he told me? Breaking the law? The Kristallnacht was breaking the law. They broke the law, but they had orders to do it. And that is how this new Germany is behaving. And with this appeal, you will finish up in a concentration camp, and your sister will finish up in a concentration camp. Tear her to pieces. Don't do it. 

I will make for you an application where I will beg the authorities to help your sister because you are-- you-- you understand that it is good for the German Reich and for the army that it should be in the hand of a [NON-ENGLISH]. But you need something to eat and so on and so on, that they will do it. I will do it for you. 

And he prepared for me such a petition. And he said but between you and me, I wouldn't even send in this petition either. Forget about it, and I did. That was a-- a life-saving lesson that I had from this Dr. Eisenberg. It showed to me how-- how the position of the Jews was. 

They thought that they are living-- that they live a normal life. But that was an illusion. It started with burning Jews alive in the synagogues, and it finished with an expulsion of the Jews on the 9th of December, 1942. They called in the people. 

There-- there was-- I mentioned it before, that-- that-- that Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] went-- there were rumors that something is going to happen, and Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] went to the Landrat, and the Landrat didn't-- they said it's not true. And the deputy Landrat called him in and tendered him over a pack of blank permits to-- to-- blank travel permits and told save-- save your life, and disseminate to the Jews that they should run for their life. 

Something is going to happen. And he showed them that-- with a finger on the calendar-- it's going to happen on Monday. And that was why-- I-- I was living with Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], that I took him to Polaniec and so on. But that was in the-- in December-- in the-- they-- they-- when-- when Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] run away with me. 

And others took over. The Landrat already could see that-- that the people know already that something is going to happen. So they ask them for ransom. If you-- if you-- if you deliver so-- so much and so much in foreign currency, gold, and so on, everything will be fine. Everything was delivered. 

And on Monday the 9th of-- of March, the-- the whole-- the whole-- the whole shtetl was surrounded by police. Jews called out to the main square. And the young people were-- were-- were taken to concentration camps, and to-- and to-- and to labor camps, where the elderly people were marched to the airport, to-- there was-- to the fields of the airport. It was not an airport. There was-- there were factories making airplanes, Hermann Goring worker. 

They called and there were-- they were marched seven kilometers towards [POLISH]. And while they were standing outside in snow in the 19th-- in the 9th of March, one spotted my father with-- he-- who wore high boots. Everybody in Mielec wore high boots in the snow and so on. They were-- were Polish boots. 

He said, look, I like these boots. Take them off. And my father had to march barefoot seven kilometers in snow. And there in [POLISH], people were put into hangers. There was my wife there and my family, my auntie, my cousins, and my sisters, my-- no, there was not my-- my-- and the auntie was-- the aunt-- the aunt and her-- and her daughters were there. And my wife's parents were there. They were-- they were placed in hangers. 

And outside the same day of-- when they arrived, they took out the elderly people and they killed-- they shot about 1,500 people on the spot. Called in the young people from the labor camp a few kilometers further along to dig a-- to to dig a mass grave. And they put them into the mass grave-- into-- into the grave over there. And all other people went-- were-- were left in-- in these hangers, and daily-- were daily selections were still going on. Daily selections through the hangers. You have to come. 

I had some documents where-- under which I-- from which I learned why these daily selections were-- were done. Because they had to-- they had to-- they prepared places to send over-- to travel-- 4,500 people. So they needed to reduce the number in these hangers to 4,500. 

So one day they spotted-- they-- they spotted [POLISH], a judge in Mielec. Was a very interesting personality, this [POLISH]. It's amazing that they didn't spot him while they were marching, because he was an invalid with a wooden leg. 

And he must have got this position as a judge because he was in the-- in Pilsudski's legion. He was a legionnaire. So this is the reason why he-- he was-- he was the only Jewish judge in-- in Mielec. Was a very intelligent man, and a very capable man. A very capable man. 

And was a tragedy over there. They called him out. It's amazing. They called out this judge, and-- to be shot. And his wife and his daughter-- was-- his daughter must have been 12 years old-- were walking behind him begging the SS men to spare his life. But they was outside the door, and somehow this judge broke down. Unbelievable. 

He broke down. He started in a high-pitched voice to beg. He said-- he addressed the German SS's, the German [GERMAN], addressed them. [SPEAKING GERMAN], divine-- something like divine masters. What do you want from me? I lost my leg fighting the Bolsheviks, fighting the communists. I was in the Austrian army. 

And the answer was, you [GERMAN] Jude, [INAUDIBLE] Jew. And they shot him and he fell down. And he fell down. And his wife and daughter asked to be shot, and they obliged. They shot his wife and his daughter. 

Was very-- while-- while-- it-- while-- while writing this-- while writing down this episode in my book, as I mentioned, Primo Levi, who said that there are seldom people who they-- who know how to go to their deaths with dignity. And somehow there are not those people you would suppose-- you-- you would believe they would-- 

On the other hand, there was another episode over there. A certain Jew called Horowitz from a rabbinical family, and he came back from Germany through [NON-ENGLISH]. And throughout the-- the-- 1938, that's a-- a different story. 

So he was there with his daughter. His daughter was about 22, 23 years old, born in Germany. They were thrown out from Germany. And they called this Mr. Horowitz out to be shot in this selection. He went so-- he went very stoically, very-- very quietly went up. And they shot him. 

And behind him was his daughter, and she was very elegantly dressed like it was at that time, the-- the fashion. She wore a white glove on her left hand, and she kept the other glove on-- on-- in the-- in the hand-- in the hand. That was the fashion that the right hand should be without a glove in case you are-- you are shaking hands with somebody. She was walking with him, having-- with these white gloves, and asked the German to do her a favor, to shoot her too. And the German shoot her. 

But somehow he didn't-- he didn't kill her immediately. She put her hand against her breast, which was full of blood, and threw the glove. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Thank you. There is no-- there is no room for two people like you and me to live on this planet. 

It's a very interesting story because it shows you two different [INAUDIBLE], how they are-- how they went to their deaths. 

INT: What happened to-- 

MV: And you would-- and you would not have suspected. 

INT: What happened to the rest of your family there? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: What happened to the rest of your family? 

MV: The rest of my family. 

Yeah. They were-- yeah, I remember now. There were the two sisters over there. 

And there was my wife, her parents. Was my auntie. And her daughters. I'm-- I was-- in the beginning I thought, no, no, that my sisters were not-- they came later-- no, they came earlier back to Mielec. They were not in Mielec. They came earlier. 

I sent them-- I received from the same deputy Landrat who tried to save the Jews, but the Jews didn't believe. He'd given them permits, travel permits, that they should run away and save themselves. Now, through Antony Droba, I got the permits from this on-- Anthony went to him, and he gave him permits. Blank. 

He sent the permits to me. I filled them out and send all the permits to my family in Lubyanka. They went to a place called [RUSSIAN] where-- by train. There, horse carts were waiting for them, and they took them to a small town called Lubyanka. A town like Mielec. 

People lived there quite-- quietly. Like nothing would have happened to them. And they came back. 

I sent them this to-- to-- they came back to Polaniec because I was already in Polaniec. Not that Polaniec was a better place, a safer place, than Lubyanka. But they came back to Polaniec because I was there, and it was closer-- put it closer home, closer to Mielec. 

Now, where we lived in Polaniec, one day-- we lived in a house or-- or in a building which belongs to a-- to a family of [INAUDIBLE]. One day, a-- I was sitting close in the post office. And the post office was not far, on a bench. And a fellow came by on a bike. I looked at him. He looked at me. He didn't say a word. I didn't say a word. I knew who he-- who he was, and he knew who I am. 

That was Vladislav Jasinszki, the famous [NON-ENGLISH], a commander of AK for the whole district, Mielec, Polaniec, Tarnow, and so on. There was a-- an-- an award for his head, dead or alive on-- everywhere in the-- in the area. 

I recognized him. He recognized me. He didn't say-- I knew that he was an antisemite. But somehow we met before in Mielec. While I was in Mielec we met once. And he was quite friendly. But here, we passed. He went to the post office. And I was thinking, what can I do? Should I stay here sitting, or should I disappear? 

If I disappear, he may think that I went to the authorities. And then I laughed. I said, what authorities? There are no authorities in Polaniec. There is nobody in Polaniec. 

No-- no authority in-- with-- within 30, 40 kilometers. So I was sitting. He went out. He came back straight to me. We shook hands. I said, look, I must tell you you're in trouble. 

If you think that Mielec was just an exception, you are mistaken. They are killing Jews on the [GERMAN]. Means on the other side of the Polish-- where-- where the Polish border. They are killing them on the spot. I may be able to help you. 

And he told me that he's going to arrange meeting me. But-- where I live. I told him where I live. He said, look, you will be [INAUDIBLE] We will meet in a valley, on the embankment of the Vistula. 

And so it happened that one day I was sitting over there. Somebody came in. They said, look, could you-- could you offer me a light on a cigarette? He wants a cigarette. Yes. You are Mark? I said yes. Today or tomorrow at 2 o'clock on the embankment of the Vistula. 

And then I came there. He told me, look, bring me photos of your family, of you and your family. What there must be people in your family, your wife who speaks Polish all right. Only for-- for people who speak perfect Polish. And I will make for you-- I'll make for you the Aryan papers. 

I brought him next time my picture and picture of my wife. He looks-- and he said, look, I know this Frieda, Frieda [INAUDIBLE]. I know her. That's her. She will not be able to-- to survive in the open. That's it. She looks too Jewish. Her eyes are black. Brown, dark hair. She looks 100% like-- like-- like her forefathers in-- in-- like her forefathers 2,000 years in-- 2,000 years ago in Palestine. 

As far as you are concerned, he told me, I'll give you papers. But remember only in big cities. Never-- never live in a small city. Only in big cities. In a small city, you'll-- they will immediately know a-- that a stranger came, and they will start looking who he is and so on. 

And for your wife, he told me, she will have to-- how he called it? To-- to-- to dye her hair. And the [INAUDIBLE] was a-- a-- peroxide. She will have to peroxide her hair. She will be a Titian beauty. 

And so I-- and I will prepare a place for her in the forest, a safe-- a safe house, 100% safe. And at the same time, while I was waiting sometimes for him to come-- he never came on time. Never on time. He-- always sometimes wait for him an hour, sometimes two hours. 

I met there two very interesting fellows. Two elderly gentlemen, Poles. I don't remember their names. And they told me who they are. That they are in charge of making [INAUDIBLE] for artillery shells for the army, out of [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], yeah? Which were-- which are grown here along the Vistula. 

They live in Warsaw. And before the war, they were high officials in the Ministry of Agriculture. They are both engineers. One is a chemist and the other is another engineer. I don't remember. 

And we were talking-- we were meeting very often on this embankment. And one day they told me, look, I'll tell you something. I have to-- to repay a debt to Jews. In 1905, after the revolution, we had to run for our life. And we left the Congress Poland to Silesia, which was under the German-- under the German occupation. And there a Jewish family kept us for two years without a penny. Not a socialist, just a Jewish family from Bunde. 

And we live-- we lived there, and they-- they supplied us with lodge and board for two years without a penny. Sooner or later you will have to run away from Polaniec. This is our address. Come to us. You will live with us. You'll look good. You live with us. I don't want anything. I want just to repay my debt. 

And so it happened that the time came for Polaniec. They prepared-- Vladislav Jasinszki prepared a safe house for my wife in the forest-- in the forest, by a family called [POLISH], I think. They-- they were forest rangers. Forest rangers. 

And their daughter-- I knew their daughter. Their daughter were-- worked in the post office. And she was a member of the AK. And that was how he arranged this-- this with the AK. And she went over there. And I went to Warsaw. 

And I also remember how I went to Warsaw. I had a suitcase with all my-- with-- with all my belongings. And I traveled first of all with-- with-- with this long [NON-ENGLISH]. Narrow lane. Narrow-- narrow railway lines. Train-- train-- yeah, to another station in [POLISH] or something like that. And there I boarded the train to Warsaw. 

And I remember I opened the door and there was-- was-- plenty-- plenty of people were there. And I pushed myself with all the chutzpah in the world. I said only with-- with chutzpah you will play the-- you'll-- you-- you will be right. I pushed myself into a seat, and my suitcase close to me, and later I pushed the top because-- before-- after I got my seat. And there I was sitting among a lot of people, all Polish people were-- Polish people, [NON-ENGLISH], were [NON-ENGLISH]. 

There-- they were-- they were carrying sausages and eggs and butter to Warsaw and to the black market and so on. And Polish people around. I said-- next to the window was sitting looks to me like kind of an intellectual, was reading a book. And glasses on, a young-- a young man. 

And the talk was about Jews. Jews and Jews and Jews, and Germans are killing them. We'll be soon-- soon we'll get rid of them. They-- and one-- one-- soon we'll get rid of them. And the fellow sitting next to the window said to them, look, if there-- it has been arranged already that the Jews will survive, will be settled in Russia next-- close to Odessa. There will-- all Polish Jews will be there. 

And that's true. Was such an arrangement. Means-- means they got the minister-- [INAUDIBLE] and a-- and a-- and a-- and a representative of the Jewish, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] with Professor [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't know about it. The first time I heard about it was from this fellow. The Jews will go to Odessa, to the [INAUDIBLE]. 

And one woman jumped out. Not Odessa! No, no, no. Only there they-- if they go to Odessa, they will come back like rats, and they will suck our blood. No Odessa. Palestine! 

And I said it is high time that I should start talking because otherwise I will be-- I-- I-- I will be suspect. And I told them, look? What's going on with us, with us Poles? The schools are closed. The university professors are in concentration camps. Even schoolteachers are in concentration camps. 

Our people growing up without schools. And the only thing you have got in mind are Jews, Jews, Jews. You are playing the part of the Nazis. And that will harm our countries when the peace talks will-- when they start peace talks after the war. 

There was silence all over. And one woman said, you see? A Jewish uncle. That is a Jew uncle. This-- this-- this is a [POLISH], something like-- I said-- I said I passed my exam. I'm not a Jew. I'm only Jew uncle. That is how I arrived. But we never-- we didn't talk any more-- any more about the Jews until we came to Warsaw. That is how I went to Warsaw. 

INT: Could I just ask you, do you think that sort of mentality was common amongst Polish people? 

MV: Oh, yes. There was not a-- there was not a single meeting of Poles without talking about Jews. And I will tell you further when I came to Warsaw, I went-- I went to this-- I took a-- a cab. I went to-- to this house not far from the [POLISH] street. I don't remember where it was, nor the number, but it's not far from [POLISH] street. 

And the concierge, the [POLISH], the gatekeeper was sitting at the gate. And he asked me where to? I told him, it's-- I'm going to my uncle. I know my way. Because I knew where to go. Right, left, and so on. I-- I-- I was here before the war. I am going to my uncle. Now-- 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: --15 with Mark Verstandig. You're talking about your period in Warsaw under false papers now, isn't it? 

MV: Mhm. So I went to these-- to these two brothers. They had a son. They were widowers. 

And they gave me a paper that I am employed by them to organize-- supervise and organize factories making [INAUDIBLE] for the-- making [INAUDIBLE] for the-- for the German army. And everybody, all people are-- are called upon to help me whenever I want to, whenever-- whenever I need them to help. Having such a paper, I came to the conclusion I needed-- it would be nice to have it translated into German. And I went with this paper to the government-- government general-- General Government office of-- headquarters of-- of-- of-- of-- of the-- of the-- of the German government in Warsaw. And I asked to have it translated. 

I asked, first of all, for people who-- who-- who can-- who can speak Polish, because I can't speak German. So they called me in. And I told them, look, I've got a paper like that. I have to organize. In Polish, I told them. I have to organize factories making [INAUDIBLE] artillery and so on. And I wanted to have it translated. So all these people clearly-- German ancestors should help me. 

And where will the [INAUDIBLE] stationed in Sandomierz. So he said to me, people coming from Sandomierz, they come. They always bring some sausage, some good things. 

So I said, so will be some more. I'll-- I'll bring-- I-- I-- I bought some. I-- I'll bring it tomorrow when it is ready. Said, don't forget. 

Next day, I came. I brought a kilo or more sausage and some butter. So I don't-- I'm going to bring a whole parcel. I brought it in. And the translation was ready. I paid them maybe a couple of sausage, or-- next to nothing. 

And I got the translation. I went out. I nearly fell over. 

I could see a document with the Government General-- head of-- headquarters of the-- of-- of-- of the German government in Warsaw. 'To whom it might concern,' and so on, translated. It was written with small letters-- 'translation' or something. Maybe it was not-- 'translated' somewhere. 

It looked like an original German document asking people to help me wherever I need help. The two brothers didn't believe their eyes, looking at this document. And with that, they sent me to Polaniec that I should start working over there. 

They told me where I should go, as I went to-- to this secretary of the amalgamated-- amalgamated villages-- offices. Like here, you have got these amalgamated city councils. And the ones over there-- the [NON-ENGLISH] There was a secretary of [NON-ENGLISH] of this council-- council of-- council of villages. 

I went over there. I-- and I wrote him a letter, another letter received not from these people-- from the two brothers. They knew the fellow-- that said he should help me, and so on and so on. And next day, I went to the-- the-- they-- they-- they arranged for me a host card, and so on. 

And I went and did my work. Did my work. I knew what I have to do, and so on. 

But what I wanted to tell you is-- is the same night that I came, there were a gathering of a few so-called intellectuals. There was a priest. And there were some other youngsters. And maybe you were-- maybe there was one who had-- who made some matriculation. Maybe-- maybe-- maybe, and maybe not. 

They were the intellectuals. And the whole talk was about Jews. They didn't stop talking on about Jews. 

They asked me to play bridge. But unfortunately, I couldn't play. I don't play-- I don't play cards. And I was reading a book. And they were talking and talking. 

And then, I remember, one said that there are quite a number of Jews who are trying to pass-- to pass-- to pass on as Poles. But you will always recognize a Jew. You will always-- when he-- when he lights a cigarette, you will see. A Jew is-- is lighting a cigarette by taking the match off-- from him, off. Whereas a Pole is lighting the cigarette by lighting the match towards him-- 

--covering-- covering the-- covering the light from the wind-- to him. At that stage, I thought-- I thought I will-- a little-- a little earlier, I thought it wouldn't be-- it wouldn't be the right place to make a speech like I did on the train. But at that moment, I told her, look, but I think you are wrong as far as the cigarettes is concerned. It's a difference between urban people and people in open villages. 

And-- in-- in-- urban people will never light the match towards them because sometimes some-- a spark can make a hole in the shirt or-- or-- or on-- or on their coat. They will light it against-- I mean, off. But in small villages, where in open-- the village is open and the wind is blowing, there is covering. This has nothing to do with Jews. That was the only time I took-- I-- they said, he makes a point. That was the only time I objected. 

And we were drinking a lot of Bimba. And I remember it was-- it was Saturday night. We were drinking a lot of Bimba. And I lied down. 

In the morning, they woke me up to go to church. I told them, look, I'm almost sick. I'm too tired. 

I drank too much. Apologize to the priest, the priest that was yesterday here-- apology. I can't come-- not come. 

Anyhow, on Monday, or on Tuesday-- a few days later. A few days later, one of the-- of the two brothers, he came along to Sandomierz, to this-- to this village. And I called him out. I told him, look, I'm sorry. I can't stay here anymore. 

They are talking about the Jews day and night and day and night. It is too stuffy for me. I just don't like it. 

And Jasinszki, I told him, told me, never stay in a small place. Never stay in a small place. And I told him I-- I was not going to-- even to stay overnight here. I'm going back to Warsaw now. 

And the same day, in the evening, I boarded a train. And I can tell you, I boarded the train. It was full of this-- of this smugglers and-- and black marketeers. And it was-- and you-- you could-- you-- you could smell-- you could smell the sausages. 

But I could feel fresh air, in comparison to the nice-- to-- to-- to the nice dwelling of this secretary there of all those combined-- combined villages. That was the answer to your-- to-- to your question whether it was common. The common-- the common conversation everywhere was about Jews. 

INT: Why do you think Poles were preoccupied with this question? Why do you think Poles were preoccupied? 

MV: Why? You know, here, I must tell you my observation. 

In the beginning, the intellectuals, the Polish intellectuals were-- were defending the Jews, because they thought the war will not take long. And as this solicitor, this barrister in-- in Warsaw said, I am for elimination of the Jews from the-- from the bar. But this is not our agenda now. Now there is a German occupation. And they are Polish citizens. 

You see, this were-- this was the approach of the Polish intellectuals when the war started. And that was going on until 1942, let's say March-- March or middle 1942. But when they looked around and they saw that the Jews were being killed, then they changed. 

They changed because they thought, now it is a good time to get rid of the Jews. And the blame will go that the Germans did it. And we will be our own masters. And here, we could see it was quite different. The intellectuals were the people who were feeding the population with the notion, now is the time to get rid of any minority, of-- of the Jewish minority in Poland. 

Now, the city dwellers were forward, because they already saw themselves occupying the Jewish houses and the Jewish dwellings and the Jewish shops. And as far as the villages are concerned, here we can see the people, the illiterate people, they were not keen on playing this game, this game of the intellectuals to get rid of the Jews. 

They were too clever to see that it is not going to happen like that. And if they can make, in the meantime, a few dollars by keeping a Jew and giving him shelter, they will do it. The only things they didn't were on the only-- the only fear they had was not the fear of the Germans, but the fear of the neighbor who was jealous, that vulture who is making money. And he-- he's-- and-- and they-- they thought that he is making big money. And he hasn't got a Jew to hide. 

If there would be a lot-- it would be 10 times as many middlemen to combine a Jew-- to connect a Jew seeking shelter with a peasant willing to give him shelter. 10 times, 20 times more Jews would have been killed. The only people who were against it were either the intellectuals and the city dwellers. 

And that has changed. From the beginning of the-- the beginning of war, they were defending the Jews. Because the Poles, their peasants were came in just to loot. It's a time to-- they-- they-- they can loot, but not to kill. But that changed in the meantime. 

And-- and it's a-- it's a matter of fact that they have were helped by the-- they were helped not only by these agitators from-- from the parties of the-- the well-known antisemitic parties. But they were agitated by the representatives of the-- of-- of the Polish government, by the Delegation, so-called Delegatura. And I've got a lot of documents to prove this point that say-- that-- that's pointed out that-- that the Jews, after the war, will not be able-- will not be-- will not allow-- will not be allowed to come back to their houses, to their dwellings, and to their businesses because they were occupied by the Polish people. 

And if they-- and if they-- and if they will endeavor to come-- if they try to come, they will be resisted by force, because that is not restitution that we will call it an invasion. The Jews coming back to their houses were-- were-- will be called invaders. And-- and these are documents which-- which are-- which-- which are originals of-- of the documents and photos of those documents are on the Yad Vashem. 

INT: This would seem to suggest that a certain proportion of Poles saw the Jews as being aliens in Poland. You said a-- was that a common phenomenon? 

MV: Yes. But you have-- you-- don't forget, they were alien to Poles for 1,000 years. They were living there for 1,000 years. 

The only thing was that they-- that-- that they were-- they were of a Jewish religion. They were Jewish religions. They considered themselves as a minority. And if you have got a minority, it does not necessarily mean you have to kill somebody. They were living here for-- for-- were living here for centuries. 

INT: At this time, at the middle of 1942, when you were in Warsaw, what was the fate of your parents? 

MV: What? 

INT: --was the fate of your parents? What happened to your parents? 

MV: What happened to my parents? Now, my father-- my father was killed in Mielec. My mother was with her daughter in-- in Mosciska. 

And later, they went from Mosciska to Kovel. I think I talked about it. They went to Kovel. Because in Mosciska, after they were thrown out from their house, as capitalists-- they had a little factory making-- a little factory making roof tiles. They went to Kovel having already Soviets passports, as-- as workers. So the only-- and my father was there too. 

No, I'm sorry. My mother was with my daughter in [INAUDIBLE]. My father was with my daughter in Mosciska. And they went from Mosciska to Kovel. And my mother stayed with my family, with her other daughter in [INAUDIBLE], because they changed. In the beginning, it was the-- it was-- it was different, but they changed. 

So the father was there. And later-- and there was my sister [INAUDIBLE]. And my sister [INAUDIBLE], Sarah, she-- she was in Krakow with her husband. And later, she came to Mielec, and she was in Mielec. And while she was in Mielec-- and my sister who was thrown out from [INAUDIBLE]-- Berta-- came to live with her. 

At that time, we received a note from my father, in [INAUDIBLE], that he would like to come back to Mielec. And we arranged, again, through a Jew called Mendel Bloch. He was a fixer. And he found a German taking him back through-- who offered to take him back from Kovel. I-- I think I talked about before-- 

INT: That's right. 

MV: --that-- that he didn't want money in advance. And he brought them safely, my sister [INAUDIBLE] and my father, brought them back to Mielec. And they stayed in Mielec with her family. It was two sisters. Well, there are three sisters, three sisters and a brother-in-law and my father. Now, the brother-in-law was taken to a-- to a labor camp or to a concentration camp when there was this Aktion. 

The two sisters went to Lubianka. And the one sister went to Lubianka too, but she died. She was-- she had tuberculosis. So she died natural deaths over there. The two sisters came back to Polaniec. 

And later, when hiding places were arranged for my family, for me, I had to go to Warsaw. My wife, to a safe house in the forest. And my aunt and her daughters went to a-- to a-- to a-- to a place in-- in Chorzelow. It is a village not far from Mielec. The middleman was a certain called-- a certain man called [INAUDIBLE] Valas. Now-- 

INT: And-- 

MV: --that, more or less-- 

INT: And your father was caught in the Aktion in-- in-- 

MV: My father was-- my father was killed among-- when the-- when there was this expulsion from Mielec and they took the Jews to this [INAUDIBLE], to where the hangars were. Outside the hangars, he was killed, together with 1,500 other Jews. And for my sisters, this merchant Valas found a place for-- for my aunt and his daughters. For my sisters, he couldn't find a place in Chorzelow. But together with a friend of his, they found a place in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a town-- or close to a town called Jaslo. And they went over there. 

Later, in 1943, end of 1943-- Passover 1943. No, it was-- it was Easter time, 1943, when I heard and I got the news that where they were hidden, the Pole took them out-- roped them, took them out to the police, the Polish police. The-- and so-- and the Polish police shot them, killed them. 

INT: Getting back-- killed your mother. 

MV: My mother was with-- with her daughter, the eldest daughter in [INAUDIBLE]-- in [INAUDIBLE]. And what happened to her, I don't know. Must have happened to her the same as with the whole family over there. 

There was a sister, brother-in-law, his daughter. And his daughter had a fiance. And my mother-- they all were killed. I don't know what happened to them. I just don't know. 

INT: Sometime in mid '43? 

MV: Sometime in mid '42. End of-- maybe end of '42. It was already-- it could've been already autumn '42. 

INT: About the same time you were in Warsaw. What impressions did the city make on you as far as the conditions for Jews were concerned? 

MV: I didn't have any contact with Jews over there, while I was there. I lived with these two brothers. We used to go out shopping together, to the black market. 

And we used to go out-- we used to go out to a cafe on the [INAUDIBLE], a cafe called [INAUDIBLE] under the [INAUDIBLE]. And that-- something happened. It was a very nice cafe. There was a composer over there who played piano-- make a living. And one day, I was sitting with one of them in the cafe. 

And what he did, there were some serviettes in a glass, like that, you know. He took out the serviettes and started to draw the [INAUDIBLE] which we-- which we are producing. I have never been in the factory, you see-- which we are producing. Starts to tell me. 

And he said, it's good that you should have it. And he produced, let's say, a [INAUDIBLE], an artillery [INAUDIBLE] out of this [INAUDIBLE], number so and so, the measurements. He told me the measurements on such and such. And so he drew quite a number of-- quite a number of those things. And I put them into my pocket. 

It so happened that a few days later, I was walking the streets of Warsaw, and I saw a cafe. I thought, I have nothing to do. I'll go have a cup of coffee-- a-- a cup of tea. I preferred a cup of tea than a cup of coffee because the tea was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was herb-- herbal tea, a herbal tea. 

I was sitting and-- and drinking this herbal tea. And people were-- a lot of people were-- suddenly, I could see that-- that a few places just disappeared, that people are disappearing. And within-- within a second before, I'm-- I could see what's going on, Gestapo was already at the doors, all around. And ordered all the people to the wall. 

We stood all the people to the wall. And they asked-- there was one speaking Polish. He says, 'papier,' documents. He took the papers. He didn't look at them. He puts them into a bag. 

I thought, my god, it's no good, because he doesn't look at the documents. I was almost in the middle, maybe closer to the end. He came to me. 'Papier.' 

I took out my document, my-- my-- my gray Kennkarte. But the same time, I took out all those serviettes with the [INAUDIBLE] and let them go down. Because I knew that if he takes them, he will throw them into the bag. And they fell down. 

He took the card. He took the identity card, the ID, the gray ID. And then he saw that some papers are coming down. He grabbed it and looked. Military [INAUDIBLE] so and so, measurements. Maybe they got a spy. This is what I wanted. 

He called in immediately the-- look, what's-- what's going? And yet he asked me-- they asked me-- went, and what's that? And then I took out my document written in German, where it says, German-- Headquarters of the German-- of-- of the German Reich in Warsaw. That everybody should assist me and giving-- give-- to give me help, and so on. That I am such an important fellow, and so on. 

He looks at it. So he tells me, look, what are you doing? He says, there's no foreign currency here. 

Says-- I didn't know. I am-- I live in Sandomierz. I just came here because my people haven't been paid for the last six weeks. I came-- I came here to ask for money that I should go back to my-- then I saw a cafe. And I went in to have a cup of tea. And my cup of tea is standing over there and getting cold. 

He said, I'm sorry. And they conduct me-- they-- they took me back to my table and apologized [INAUDIBLE]. I was the only one, the only one-- also, I had a lot of luck. So only one not get-- not to be taken away. All the other people were taken away in a trolley-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a lorry. 

INT: What name were you under in your Kennkarte? 

MV: The Kennkarte wasn't Polish. 

INT: What name were you using for your Kennkarte? 

MV: Ah. The name at that time-- Tomasz Dubula, I think. No, it was not Dubula. 

Yes, Tomasz Dubula. At that time, it was Thomas Dubula. Or Tomasz-- Tomasz-- sorry. Dubula, I came back. It was Tomasz Zajac. 

It was Tomasz Zajac. Dubula was the document I came back from-- from-- from the German part back to the-- from the Soviet part back to Germany. But that was-- the card issued by Jasinszki, [INAUDIBLE], was Tomasz Zajac. 

INT: How long were you in Warsaw for? 

MV: In Warsaw, it was almost till Christmas. 

INT: Christmas '42? 

MV: Christmas '42. 

INT: And during that time, did you see the ghetto at all outside? 

MV: No. I didn't go to the ghetto. I went-- I went close to the ghetto. And I saw this Jewish policeman coming out from the ghetto. And I got a million times more scared than-- than-- than-- than seeing Gestapo. 

INT: Why was that? Why? 

MV: Because I thought they may recognize me. The Gestapo didn't. 

They-- I-- I was scared of them. And I saw them in their boots and their uniforms. They gave me-- they-- they gave me a fright. I never-- I never went close to the Jewish ghetto. 

INT: Again. What-- what-- what sort of uniform did they have? 

MV: I don't remember. I don't remember it now. But it looks like a military uniform, but it was not a military uniform. 

It was a nicely cut uniform, because they had Jewish tailors in the ghetto, and they prefer-- they-- they made some very nice-- very nice uniforms. I wouldn't remember the color. But it was close to a military color, but not exactly like the Germans. A-- it was-- it was-- I don't remember that. 

INT: Do you have any memory-- 

MV: And-- and they had the-- and they had this-- this band, armband with a-- with a Star of David. But besides this, without the stars, they looked like a-- like-- like-- like Gestapo people. 

INT: Do you have any memory of the-- how-- how the walls looked? How the ghetto walls looked? 

MV: How the general walls? 

INT: The ghetto walls, what they looked like? 

MV: No, I don't, because I-- I don't-- I don't remember. I don't remember. Maybe-- surely, I saw them, but I wouldn't-- I wouldn't remember. 

INT: So were there any other important incidents during your stay in Warsaw? 

MV: Yes. It was-- first of all, it was one incident. I came-- later, when I was-- when I-- when I-- when I-- when I decided I'm not going to the villages, to Sandomierz, they made an arrangement for me-- they made-- they made some arrangements for me because they were afraid-- they were afraid of the-- of-- of the-- of the gatekeeper. Now, one of the brothers had a villa in [INAUDIBLE] on the line Warsaw-Otwock. 

So he said to me, look, I've got a villa, I've got a cottage in [INAUDIBLE], in the forest over there, as a-- a number of people of Warsaw used to have. I will go to rent another villa in [INAUDIBLE]. It's in the same line-- in [INAUDIBLE]. 

And you will stay with us for two days-- and in Otwock. You will stay with us for a few days. And then you'll go to [INAUDIBLE], back from work, going home for a day. Next day, you'll go to work, but you'll go to Otwock and stay there a day. And then you'll go to [INAUDIBLE] and stay a day. 

And then, after five, six days, you come back to Warsaw so that the gatekeeper could see that you are employed, that you are working. That was the arrangement. And it worked. 

One day, I went out of-- I-- that was [INAUDIBLE] in December. I went out from-- I came from Otwock. And I could-- I walked along the station. And I could hear heavy-- heavy-- heavy men walking behind me. 

I could hear heavy men walking. I looked around. And before I turned around, I had already-- he had already his-- his hand on my shoulder. And I turned around. And he said, 'You are a Jew.' 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue on next tape. 

MV: So you're--

INT: Part 4, interview with Mark Verstandig, running tape 17. 

Last time, we ended in-- on your account of your meeting with the policemen in Warsaw. 

MV: Yes. I remember the policeman, there was a heavy-- a heavy person behind me. And he put his hand on my shoulder. I turned around. And he told me-- and he told me, you're a Jew. You're coming with me. 

So I told him that, first of all, you are too familiar. I took off his hand from my shoulder. And I told him we didn't feed pigs together on a farm. If you want to address me, be polite. 

And he said to me, look. And besides this, I told him, I am not a Jew, and please do me a favor. Fuck off. 

He said, look, I have met a lot of Jews. I dispatched them to hell. But I have never met a-- a chutzpah-- what do you call it in? Such a-- 

INT: Audacity? 

MV: In such an audacious-- such an audacious Jew. And I told him, look, I'm not a Jew. And-- and I think you should get lost. 

Look at my satchel that I was-- I was carrying a Hessian-- a-- a-- a Hessian briefcase, packed with books, very heavy, of course, from a whole week. I told him, look under the books. There's some hand grenades. And there are people with me, behind me, in front of me. 

And therefore, if you want-- if you want me to go with you to the station, by all means, let's go. But I can assure you, you will never reach the station. You will lose your life before you reach the station. We don't want to carry out executions on the site. But if you force our hands, can't be helped. Now let's go. 

Said-- he was as white as a ghost. He said, what should I do? I said, do you want from me a little advice? I'll tell you what you should do. I'll show you a piece of paper. And you will read the paper, and you will salute and turn around and go away. 

What should I tell the other people, the people who is-- who, who, what shall I tell the men who pointed-- who pointed out to me that you are a Jew and he is observing us? Tell him that I am a Gestapo officer. And take him in to a doorway and bloody his nose that, that he embarrassed you so much. 

He went away. I should say that, but I was shocked as never before, the first time I was recognized as a Jew. And I realized that it was a moment of a bluff which succeeded. But I could have lost my life, and my friends and my wife never-- nobody would have known where my remains are. 

And I decided, at that time, that eventually, eventually I will be recognized, because the war was not going to be over overnight, in a few days. And sooner or later, somebody will recognize me. And not every policeman will be such a fool like this old one. And that will be the end of it. 

So that was one day where I was really very, very shocked. Then I came back to my friends' flat. Of course, at that time, I came back over for two days-- for-- for two days in, in Warsaw. 

I didn't tell them the story. But I had bad news. The news was that my wife is arriving next day in the morning. She would arrive next day in the morning in Warsaw with my friend Kubick, that she was thrown out from her safe place. 

And I went over there, next day in the morning. And there, I found out what happened. She was just thrown out from the safe place given to her, arranged for her by the leader of the AK, by the commander of the-- of the AK for the whole district. 

They just opened the door and told her, look, we don't want to keep you anymore. You go to the Kubicks' with your friend. And they showed even how to go there, this way. 

They didn't rob her. They didn't take anything from her. They, they, they were paid, by the way. But they didn't do any harm to her. But they told her, we don't want to keep you. 

INT: Who were these people? 

MV: These people are-- were forest rangers. And they were members of the AK. Their daughter was working at the post office. And she was a member of the AK. She was the liaison-- the liaison officer of the AK. 

And then I was just wondering what happened. That much I'll talk about some later. But now, first of all, let me concentrate on my wife's arrival in Warsaw. 

It happened that Kubick came with her, my friends from Poland came with her to Warsaw, to where he has family. And the family agreed that they will take her in. 

But the moment they came down from the train, they took a rickshaw. You know what a rickshaw is. It's a bicycle with arrangements for two people and-- and luggage. But the rickshaw-- young rickshaw man, they gave him the address. But the young rickshaw man took them straight to the police station. He immediately recognized my wife, that they are Jews. 

At the police station, my wife denied that she is Jewish. And they ask her all kinds of questions of, of the Catholic liturgy. And she answers the questions very well. And-- but they still insisted that she is a Jew. 

The man in charge at that time, the man in charge, he says that-- that she is Jewish. And he told her, look, you came-- you, you-- and he started first of all to check what she is wearing around to-- a body check. And they found-- he found a lot of dollars, gold dollars, jewelry, and-- and dollars, bank notes, a lot of money, a lot-- lot of money, thousands of dollars. 

Now, she is very-- he had put everything on the table. He said, now, the wife came back to the husband with the money. The husband is already in-- in Warsaw. And then the husband will not even know that she died in the cellar of the Gestapo.
