And my wife told him, look, first of all, I'm not Jewish. And secondly, the Gestapo will not prove that I am Jewish-- that I am Jewish. They may take the money. And they will let me go. 

Again, he said, well, what should I do? He said, look, let's divide it. She made a mistake. She was-- she-- and divide it, so he said, it's all right. 

So he, he divided it honestly, $1 for $1, a-- and a gold coin for a gold coin, jewelry, and so on. His half he put immediately into the drawer. And then from-- he took her-- because she had some local money, probably zlotys. He took about 400 or 500, no, 1,000 zlotys, 1,000 zlotys, which was about $3, $2 or $3, $2 or $3 and left them on the table. 

He called in two policemen and told them, look, [SIGHS] this young lady, she left us $1,000. I give her back what she has got here on the table. And I think we should call a-- [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a cab-- a cab and to let them go. 

It happened also that in the other room, they investigated Kubick. And they thought that he is Jewish. He asked all kinds of questions. And then, when it was a question of a vesper or something like that, he didn't know what they were asking. 

And at that time, they told them to let his-- his trousers down. So they realized that he is not-- he is not Jewish. And he told them, look, gentlemen. I studied to be a priest. What you are talking about, the name of the vesper may be used in another-- may be-- may be-- may be-- may be-- used somewhere else, but not in, in, in, Galicia or not in [NON-ENGLISH] and so on and so on. 

They immediately had the suspicion that he is an AK officer and they were scared of him. And they let-- they called then a cab. And he gave an advice to my wife, never use a rickshaw. They are all informers. Take a-- take a cab with an elderly driver, an elderly coach driver. 

And that was the end of this incident. We came-- we discussed the matter of state in Warsaw. She realized that she will not be able to go out. And the people-- the, the family of my friend Kubick had a very small flat, that was impossible to make a hiding place over there. That means she would have to live open. And with her looks, she wouldn't have survived a single day. 

So they decided that she should go back. And he took her back. And I said to Kubick that I will come in a few days' time, because I was shocked with this incident, what happened before with the policeman. 

And so I came back to my friends. And I told them, look, I'll tell you what happened to my wife. I'll tell you what happened to me with the policeman, because I didn't tell them before. 

The only way of surviving in Warsaw for me would be to join the AK and to go around the city in company of AK. I know how they operate. One goes ahead. They never go two people together. Behind is one and one is in the front. They keep an eye on each other. 

And so they walk around the streets of Warsaw. I speak German like all Germans. I may be of use to go around to a cafe where the Germans are sitting and to listen to their conversations and so on and so on. I'm [INAUDIBLE]. I want to join official the AK. 

They didn't-- they were not so happy about it. But they told me that they are going to bring up the matter at the highest level. And that shocked me. 

Why at the highest level? A young man speaks German, he doesn't look Jewish, speaks German, speaks Polish. Why should it be a question of contacting the highest level to join the AK? 

And how, a day later, they told me that the man they wanted to talk to is-- he's not around. And the other man is not around. They will come tomorrow, come after. 

After a few days, I asked them, look, tell me the truth. They told me that the truth is truth is that AK is not taking in Jews. They have got a few Jews here and there in order to show-- to, to, to create a kind of an alibi for overseas, that there are a few-- there are few Jews here and there. But AK doesn't take Jews in. 

INT: Are we talking about 1943 now? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: 1943 now, is that the year? 

MV: That was-- no, that was the end of 1942. That was end of-- that's December '92. They don't take Jews. 

And it's unfortunate. As a matter of fact, he told me-- he told me some moneys, some money will be coming in for Jews, because there are some Jews connected with the Delegatura, for the supporting Jews. Look, a part of it will go-- a part of it will go to those people to distribute amongst the Jews who are in hiding. But the-- but the most of it will be swallowed by the AK. 

That is the position. I can't change it. But that is the truth and the whole truth. We talked about this. We talked later on with-- we were drinking Bimber for a good few hours, talking about politics, Eisenhower, [NON-ENGLISH] in Africa and so on. 

And I said, look, in such a case, I will go. I'll go back. I'm not-- I'm not-- I can't-- I can't work the city. I lost confidence in myself. I can't work the city force anymore. 

Now, that was that. Few days later, I was on my way. And I was always thinking, how is it possible that my wife was thrown out from a safe place, from a safe house, supplied by-- provided for her by the commander of AK, Jasinszki. 

By the way, one of the AK people in Warsaw told my friends over there, if he is such a pull with Jedrus, why doesn't he give him a job over there in his place? Didn't like it. But I was thinking about Jedrus myself. What's going on? 

I knew that he was an antisemite. I knew that his father was an antisemite. But after all, he approached me. I didn't come-- I didn't come to him begging him for protection and so on. 

He offered me protection. He offered protection for my wife. So without his approval-- I was thinking without his approval, [NON-ENGLISH]-- the family name of those foresters was [NON-ENGLISH]. The family [NON-ENGLISH] wouldn't have dared to open the door. 

At the same time, they didn't do Jan any, any harm. That means it must have been the instruction of Jasinszki. 

INT: How do you explain-- 

MV: And I was-- beg pardon? 

INT: How do you explain this? 

MV: But I didn't-- I thought that he was an antisemite, and his father was antisemite. And after a few months, he regretted that he gave a safe house to a Jewess. He regretted. 

But he didn't want to do any, any harm. He said, look, send her to the Kubicks. The Kubicks are their friends. They will take care of her. And don't do any harm to her. 

That's what I thought. What I didn't know at that time-- I mean, accusing in my mind Jasinszki, Jedrus Jasinszki, of antisemitism what I didn't know is that Jasinszki gave her a-- a safe house and kept her there, disobeying an order of the chief, of the commander in chief of the AK for the whole country. 

That was an order issued by General Bor-Komorowski, was the chief commander of the AK for the whole Poland. And the order was number 116, issued on the 15th of September 1942. And in that order, General Komorowski, Bor-Komorowski, ordered to kill Jews who escaped from ghetto and to kill Russians who escape from the POW camps-- to kill Russian POWs who escaped from the camp-- from, from the German camp, and-- but in the first place, the Jews. 

Consequently, Jasinszki for a few months was disobeying an order of his commander-in-chief in Warsaw. And after a few months, he decided, look, he can't go on like that, being a soldier and disobeying orders. So he-- he certainly issued an order to the [NON-ENGLISH], let her go, but don't do-- don't do any harm. Send her back to the Kubicks. That was my explanation later when I learned about this infamous order of General Bor-Komorowski, the-- from the AK. 

INT: What was the reason for this order? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: What was the reason for this order? 

MV: To get rid of the Jews. As long-- as long as they didn't know that the Jews were-- that the-- about this final-- about this final solution, they didn't do any harm to the Jews. But the moment the Germans started mass killing of the Jews, they didn't want any Jews to survive, just like that. 

No matter what Mr. Feliks says, he aren't there-- or what Tygodnik Polski say. They-- they would be-- they, they would be happy if I would have written in my book at the Poles, that the-- that the AK and the-- the underground, they all were like the Danish. The-- the Jews were evacuated. But when they came back, they opened the doors. Their flats were in, in best-- in good order. Flowers were prepared for them. 

You see, they would-- I mean, Mr. Feliks from the Tygodnik Polski would like me to write about it. But that was not the truth. The truth was that they wanted to get rid of the Jews. And there are documents. 

And I repeated it. I think I mentioned this earlier. There are documents in the Yad Vashem stating, the Delagatura-- the Delagatura policy was that Jews should not come back to their, their homes, to their factories, to their-- to-- to-- to their trade, to their flats. If they come back, they will be treated-- they will be treated like invaders. It will not be a restitution. They-- and the-- and the Poles will, will force them out, will keep them in a-- will keep them in a closed area until they-- until they emigrated. 

Now, that was the position. It was not a question. It was not a question of, as you mentioned, whether they were alien, whether the Poles, they considered the Jews as an alien element. The Jews in Poland were not-- were, were not worse than the Jews in Hungary, than the Jews in Bulgaria, than the Jews in Czechoslovakia, than the Jews anywhere else. But that was the position. 

INT: What, what was behind the thinking in-- what was behind the thinking of the AK, upper echelons in agreeing with the German directive? 

MV: It just an occasion, an occasion to get rid of the Jews and to-- and to grab their-- their houses, their flats, their factories, and they-- and-- and to get-- to get rid of a minority, just like that. 

INT: What-- what-- or actually, where-- where did the AK, as an underground force, where did it stand in the spectrum of the Polish underground forces? Was it left or right? Where, where was it in the spectrum? 

MV: No. That-- and that was the mainstream. The AK was the mainstream. They were Socialist over there. And they were [NON-ENGLISH] people, [NON-ENGLISH] people, and so on. They were-- this was the mainstream. 

The few Jews who were in the AK were in units which was solid-- which were exclusive Socialist units-- PPS unit, like the ALP here, and the-- and the-- the Social Democrats unit. They kept a few Jews. 

The only-- the only underground which kept Jews was Armia Ludowa. That was a Communist underground, which kept Jews. You see, even in Warsaw, in many-- in-- in-- in-- in many memoirs, you see, you will find it that in Warsaw during, during the Uprising, when people came out and they wanted to join the AK, the AK told them, we are not keeping, so you go to the Armia Ludowa. They make the-- they may take you in. We are-- we don't take Jews. 

That was the mainstream under the Delegatura. And the Delegatura was the delegation of the-- of the government in-- of the government in exile. The only-- you asked what was the reason. The reason was just to grab the little house, the flat, the little factory or the larger factory, which belongs to the Jews. That was the only reason. 

INT: Were there any other underground organizations? 

MV: No. There was only the mainstream organization. That is the AK. And on the left side, there was the Armia Ludowa. It's the Communist one. And they accepted Jews. 

And then on the extreme right, was NSZ. This is Narodowe Sily Zbrojne. Yeah. And they were just Hitlerites. Very interesting, because there was-- one of their-- one of their divisions or a few divisions of their who retreated together with the Germans. 

OK. And yes. That was more or less everything. NSZ on this-- but by the way, at a later stage, NSZ joined the AK under Bor-Komorowski. So the NSZ became part of the-- of-- of the-- of the AK. 

INT: So after Warsaw, what did you decide to do? 

MV: After Warsaw, I came back and-- to Polaniec, came back by train, by-- to-- to Polaniec. And my friend Kubick took me to the place where he placed my wife. And that was [NON-ENGLISH], in [NON-ENGLISH], Kolonia, in a forest, just a clearing in a forest. 

There were two houses there. And we went by night. It was cold. It was snow. And we went over there. And there was a cottage, a small cottage. 

The family's name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't remember his, his-- the Christian name of the-- of this [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyhow, family [NON-ENGLISH]. 

They had a small house, a room, a living room, which was at the same time a bedroom, and a kitchen. And my wife was there. They-- they have-- they had a few-- they had two sons. 

The sons were-- were transported to the cow shed. And they kept my wife quite open, but inside. She didn't go out. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: Tape 18, with Mark Verstandig. We were talking about the new hiding place for your wife. 

MV: [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: Yes. 

MV: We arrived there during the night. And next day, in the morning, next day in the morning, Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] came to tell me that, unfortunately, we will have a visitor tonight. That opposite-- there was a house opposite, and he is a-- he's a [NON-ENGLISH], or a deputy [NON-ENGLISH], something like that, an official. 

And he asked me about you, because he saw two people during the night coming to my house, and only one went back. So he's bright and a dangerous man. And he wanted-- he invited himself to Christmas dinner. 

It was Christmas Eve. In Poland, on Christmas Eve was the Christmas dinner, not a day later. He invited himself to Christmas dinner, so you should be careful. Now, and the arrangement was that there was a cupboard, which stood a little bit on a slant. And when visitors came, my wife went in-- behind the cupboard. 

Of course, it was cold. She couldn't come up. She wouldn't go up to the loft, because she would be freezing to death. So she stood behind-- she stood behind this cupboard when people came in. 

So he said, look, your wife will go behind the cupboard. And you will have to sing all the carols, together. He told his-- his neighbor that I am an officer of the Polish army, and I was the officer of this company, where his son, his eldest son, was serving. 

And he accepted that. So he gave the-- the-- the-- they tutored me, the whole afternoon, to sing carols. I knew-- so I knew the tunes, since I knew some carols, but, anyhow, I went through this tuition. And-- and I went through all this, his stories about the army life and about the place where they were serving in the army, and so on, that-- that I should know about it. 

But it started with a-- it started with a surprise, because when-- when-- when-- when people-- when people gathered before the dinner, they all stood up to receive altar bread. I didn't know what to do. But I was, fortunately, in the middle. I could see what the others were doing. I did the same, so I passed the exam. 

Well, what was very-- very unfortunate, they forgot to tell me how-- how the dinner will start. And we were-- we were drinking bimber the whole night. And we're talking about the army life and about the war and about the prospects and what's going on in Africa and the [INAUDIBLE], everything, politics, everything. 

And we are drinking and drinking. I-- from time to time, you-- we-- I-- I stood up, and I went out, like old people drinking a lot. We went out. But when I went out, I put my fingers into my mouth and I threw out-- I vomited-- I threw out all the bimber, which I had before, together with the food. And then I came back. 

At one stage, when I came back, he told me, oh, my friend, he told me, you are white like a ghost. And I-- I entered the room back. And I had my lessons from Jandrush, from my friend, Jasinszki. He told me, when you are in trouble, and you feel that you-- that-- that your blood is going-- is-- is-- is running away from your face, bend down, correct your shoelaces, and the blood will come back to your face. 

So I remembered this immediately. I bent down, I corrected my laces, and I came back saying-- I said, look, my friend, am I white? Oh, shit. Oh, my god. No. He said, look, you drank too much. You piss off, you see? Just like them. He apologized. 

Anyhow, we were drinking till 4 o'clock in the morning. The whole family, the old [NON-ENGLISH], his wife, his daughter, his sons, they all were lying on the floor dead. They all were drunk, dead. The only people who was not drunk, that was me and this man, who was drunk, too. He-- he was drunk, but I was not. 

And then it was-- and my wife was standing, all the time, behind the cupboard. So at that moment, we decided it's time to go home. When we went out, he told me, I tell you a secret. When I came in this afternoon, I thought I will find a Jew, and I will send him to hell, where they all belong, and I sent a good few Jews there. 

And I thought for-- for a while, it would be high time to-- to take this fellow not to his house, but a little bit to the left, where the-- where the Vistula is, where the river is. Where the-- where the river is. And there are holes made in the ice. In the ice, holes are being made, all-- all-- a few-- every few hundred yards for the fish to-- to-- to get air, and-- and to throw him into such a hole. 

But then I decided, I never killed anybody. I never hit a dog. And I don't know that I will be able to go through killing him in. And besides this, I thought-- I thought, too, there will be traces next day in the morning, two people going to the river and one-- and one going back. But besides, I-- I-- I thought, I will not be able to-- I will not be able to do it. 

I took him to his house. And look, he kissed me. He was so friendly. And that was the end of that-- of-- of that beautiful dinner, of the Christmas dinner. I was-- we were there until about-- about-- at least three months, maybe-- no. Almost-- we-- we-- March-- maybe through till March. 

In March, they called in Kubick and told him, look, they are very nice people, but I think people may start thinking that we are hiding somebody, because we are not inviting anybody anymore. 

We have to open our house. And we wouldn't like that something should happen to those people in our house. So find for them another place, and take them away. If you wish, in a few months time, you can bring them back. 

So that was when we called in Kubick. And Kubick called-- contacted a certain man called Marcin Valas. That is the man who found the hiding places for my aunt and my cousins and for my sisters. They-- he went over to Valas and he came-- went together with Valas to this house, and off we went to Chrzastow. 

He prepared the hiding place at his sister's place, a family called Markowski. Very nice people. As a matter of fact, the [NON-ENGLISH] were very nice people, too, were religious people. Religious people. But they were-- were-- were very nice people. And they called the-- he-- he brought us to his sister and this-- and his brother-in-law and their family. And there was a hiding place prepared, a very interesting hiding place, because it was really-- they had the stone, which was-- which had D-- how do you call it? Looks like that. 

INT: L-shaped? 

MV: Hmm? 

INT: L-shaped. 

MV: An L shape. An L-shaped stone. The first-- the first part was-- the-- the-- the entrance was from-- from the hall, and the-- and the other part was behind the kitchen wall. So they cut off-- they cut off this stone, making it just a-- an ordinary stone, no more L shape. 

And the entrance was through the loft. It was all open through the loft. It was dark, and that was the only way they could have us. It was a very-- it was a good hiding place, a good hiding place for people, who didn't know before that there was an L shape. 

And-- and we were there for quite a-- for quite a time. And we were very happy there, because the Markowski's were very nice people. We were eating what they were eating. We never went hungry. And they treated us very, very well. We paid some monthly, or weekly, or monthly, I don't remember. And besides this, I promised them a few acres of land from Sadkowa Góra after the war. 

And one day-- one day, we were sitting. It was-- it was Christmas. No Christmas, it was Easter. It was Easter. And from the morning, we could hear the church bells from [NON-ENGLISH], from Chorzelów. It was a-- it was a church, Chorzelów, two and a half kilometers away. 

We could hear the bells ringing. You see, the Easter-- Easter bells, the resurrection of Christ, the joy, the resurrection of Christ. We were sitting on the-- in-- in the loft, the attic there. 

And suddenly, we heard screams and a cart moving slowly. And a girl crying, I don't want to be killed. What I have-- what I have done to you? I don't-- I'm young. I'm 16 years old. I don't want to be killed. They are going to kill me. They are going to kill us, and so on. 

It was very-- oh, you could see. And then, we could-- on the other hand, we could hear-- we could hear shouting people, kill them, kill them. We ran down to the housing-- hiding place. In the meantime, the cart went away-- went-- the cart went further on, so we couldn't hear already any-- any screams. But we went down. 

And a minute later, Markowski jumped up, came up, and said, Mary, Jesus, Maria. He said, Jesus, they took the [NON-ENGLISH] and her daughter. They chased them. They chased them in the field until they got them. And they-- and they took them-- and they took them to be killed. 

Now, that was Easter. You see? The bells, the church bells were ringing joy and the resurrection of Christ and forgiveness. And here, they took a father and his 16-year-old daughter to be killed. 

INT: Easter '43? 

MV: Hmm? 

INT: Easter '43? 

MV: Easter '43. Easter '43. As a matter of fact, it was, at that time, a few days later, I got the news that my sisters were killed. I told them about-- I talked earlier about how they were killed in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was wondering, what happened? After all, the war-- the-- that the German's were losing the war. The Russians were coming closer and closer. 

And here-- and here, they-- they are-- they are-- the-- the chasing of the Jew, of the Jewish people became more intensive than ever. That puzzled me. I didn't know about the Delegatura. I didn't know about the [NON-ENGLISH], didn't know about the order to kill Jews. I didn't know about it. And I never suspected that such things can happen. I never suspected that such things can happen. 

Even-- they tell you later that they killed Jews after the war, because Berman was in the government or another member. In Romania and in Hungary, there were Jewish people on-- in-- in the-- in the Communist government, in the Communist government of Hungary, in the Communist government of-- of Romania. 

They had a-- they had officially, a stronger grip on the government than in Poland. And nobody killed Jews in Poland-- in-- in-- in-- in Romania after the war. Nobody killed Jews in Hungary after the war. So it's only-- it is-- it is-- it was only in Poland, the only country where they were killing Jews. 

INT: Why were the people in the country so-- 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: In the country-- Why were the people in the countryside so easily influenced, do you think? 

MV: A matter of fact, they weren't. Yeah, I wouldn't say that people were like that. It is enough that the minority was influence. There was some-- there is a-- there is Kulkowski, there is a-- a historian, an AK historian, even, Dr. Kulikowski or Klukowski or something like that. 

And he pointed out that-- that-- that the people were brutalized, and they were killing Jews like vermin. He was, himself, shocked. He was, himself, shocked. And that is a historian. And again, he is quoted in-- he's quoted largely by Martin Gilbert, and he's quoted in the book issued-- in the book, [NON-ENGLISH] book, in the-- in-- in the Yad Vashem, the Yad Vashem edition, he's-- he's quoted. 

The people were brutalized, and people were chasing Jews and killed them, like vermin. That was that. And a few months-- a few-- some weeks later-- a few-- a few weeks, maybe, a few months later-- a few months later, again, Valas came to [NON-ENGLISH]. I think we'll have to leave this place and go somewhere else, because Markowski are afraid that some neighbors may be-- may suspect him of keeping Jews. 

Some people told them, look, what's going on with you? There was-- there-- there is-- there is-- your-- your-- your smoke from your chimneys-- from your chimney in the summer. And they say, smoke from your chimney, you're baking bread more than you used to. So they were-- that means that you are-- you-- you-- you-- you-- you are-- you are baking too-- you are baking bread for-- not only for your family, but for someone else. 

People were-- were checking the smoke from the chimney to assess whether in this house there are some additional mouths to be fed with bread. And Markowski said, it's high time that you should go away for a certain-- for a certain period. Because they didn't want-- they didn't want to have trouble. 

And he took us to another place. It was a place where-- my family was there. My aunt, two cousins, and their family Brenner. They were-- they-- they were-- they were relatives of my-- of my aunt. They were not my relatives. They were relatives of my aunt. Yeah, in-laws, or something like that. There were two women over there. 

[BACKGROUND NOISE] 

There were two woman. 

CREW: I'll just stop the tape for a second. 

INT: OK. We were-- you were talking about a time came where you had to move from the Markowski's because-- 

MV: Yeah. Now, we went over there. There was a certain family called Kolciak. They had a house not on the main road but a side road, where people are not watching the smoke in the chimney. It's far away, so no houses all around there. So it's-- it's just-- just on a side road, deep in a side road. 

INT: Close to which town? 

MV: What's that? 

INT: Close to which town? 

MV: It was the same village. It was the same village. 

INT: And where was that village? What's the closest-- 

MV: That was-- that village was about seven kilometers from Mielec. So we went over there, and there was my family, my aunt, those two-- two cousins, and family Brenner, two-- two-- two middle-age woman and a boy, a 12-year old boy. 

They had an excellent-- an excellent hiding place. A much better one than here, because they had a long shed, brick-- a brick shed, a long-- for horses-- for their horses and for their cows. And at one end, they shortened the-- they shortened the shed with 75 centimeters, erecting a new wall, a new brick wall, and put the cows against this new wall. 

And underneath, there was one stone, which was-- which you could remove. So you crawled in between the cows. You crawled into this hiding place. And there was a hiding place the-- the-- the width of the-- of the shed, with two bank-- with two bunks on both sides, and a little opening or one brick opening for air. 

That was the hiding place, which means that even people who were familiar with this building would not have recognized that the building was shortened with 75 centimeters. And there we were. 

But the difference between the two families was worse. The Markowski's were very friendly, decent people. The Kolciak were-- put it that way-- ugly people. We had to pay them monthly or weekly. I don't remember. I think monthly or weekly. 

They didn't want any promises of any-- of any acres after the war. And that alone was depressing, because it looks that they don't believe that we will survive the war. And what was very important, they didn't give us enough to eat. We were starving. 

They-- they fed-- they-- they were feeding their pigs, and they were feeding their cows. It was a rich-- it was a rich peasant. But as far as we are concerned, we were just starving. Valas used to come to us every few days, during the night. 

But it would have been-- it would have been, first of all, impossible for Valas to bring food, because in case somebody meets him or somebody sees him carrying something during the night, he would follow him. And he would-- he-- he would endanger himself. He would endanger us by carrying something. 

And besides this, it would have-- it would have been in vain, because if he would have known that Valas is supplying us with some food, he would have given us even less food to start with. Well, anyhow, it was very bad. And they were very bad people. 

When good news came along, let's say Valas brought good news from the-- from the press, that the Russians are already close to the border, the Polish border, they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. Not one sour pill. Sour pill, you will swallow before you will be-- before-- before things will get brighter for you. They were very bad people. 

And there we were until-- for a long time. We were there until the 31st of May, 1944. At that time, the Russians were already on the-- crossing the border of Poland. And it was about 10 o'clock at night. And we heard some commotion, some movements on the yard. 

A few minutes later, Kolciak came along. He moved away the stone from the hiding place. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. The comedy is over. Get out. The people from the underground are waiting for you. 

We went out. We went out over there. When I came out, there were some ladies were already there before, my wife. And they took away her wedding ring. No. They didn't take away her wedding ring. They took away my wedding ring. My-- a few people were there before me. 

It was the Brenners The Brenners were there before me, and then I went in. They called me in. They called me in. They put their hands into my pockets, and they took out my-- my gray ID card. Oh, he said. You wanted to masquerade as a Pole. And I told him, look. 

And first of all, when I came in, they mishandled me. They-- they-- I got-- I got a few kicks and so on. But then they put in the hand-- it was the first time that somebody-- that somebody hit me. I'd never-- nobody hit me before. 

Then they put their hand into the pocket. They saw this gray ID. You wanted to masquerade as a Pole. I told them, look. This handwriting is the handwriting of Vladislav Jasinszki Jandrush. He gave it to me. And this is his handwriting. And I am a [NON-ENGLISH], which means an officer of the AK without commission for the time being. And he is no-- he no more alive, I told him. 

When they heard this, they gave it back to me. They went out, had a meeting, and said, you can go back to your place. Nothing will happen to you. You can stay here in peace. And they went away. 

And we were standing next to the shed outside, maybe a quarter of an hour later, and one fellow, a tall fellow, came back. And he said, look. I think we decided that you are important people, that you should come with us. We will take care of you. 

Well we went all out. There were 11 people, but nine of them went-- were in front. We all were in the middle, and two people were behind. One was a 6-foot-5 tall-- tall man with a-- with a rifle, and another one was-- was a shorty fellow with a gun, with a handgun. They looked like Pat and Patachon before the war. They were two comedians known before the war. One was a tall and one was a-- a smaller one. 

And we could see they are leading us to the next forest. The-- the forest was about, let us say, a kilometer away from this house. And we were along this side rode. 

My wife started screaming. They are going to shoot us. Was special, we saw that's not a friendly thing, because the tall man is keeping his gun ready to shoot, and so was-- so was the shorter one. They were behind us. So I told my wife not to panic. We will-- will find something. On both sides of the-- of this road were ditches, irrigation ditches. [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: Thank you. We will continue next tape.

INT: Mark Verstandig. So it's towards the middle of 1944. Now in your time with the Kolciaks has ended. What's-- what happened after you finished with the Kolciaks? 

MV: No. It's not finished on the-- we-- we didn't finish on the Kolciaks, because they took us out to be shot. That is where we finished the last cassette. And then my wife started screaming, they're going to kill us! And we were the last ones. And before us were the other people from the family in [NON-ENGLISH]. And in front were nine people with this unit of the [NON-ENGLISH] unit. 

And the two were behind us. The smaller one and the larger one. I told my wife here, I've got a plan. I have got a purse which they threw away after they emptied it. And I had picked it up because I knew that behind the line we have got 20 US dollars still, over there, a bank note. I took this up. 

I told her, look, I will give him this. He will took away the finger from the trigger, and at that moment you will run a little further, and I will run, and I will push this young fellow into the ditch. Because we were coming-- we were walking close-- very close to the ditch. I will finish off this young man, and in the meantime you will run away and I will jump into the ditch. 

And at a certain stage I took out-- I took out this purse from my pocket, and I told this tall man-- I took him-- I told him, look. Here I've got hundred dollars, and let us go away. He grabbed it. And I-- at that moment when he grabbed it, I pushed the young fellow, the younger one, the-- the shorter one with the handgun, into the ditch with all my strength. And he-- he was just in-- in the ditch. And a few yards away from him, I jumped into the same ditch. 

I didn't want to jump together with him. And I was sure the-- in the meantime, the-- the old fellow, the-- the tall fellow started to shout [NON-ENGLISH]. Stop. I'm-- because I'm going to shoot. I told him shoot at us. I was already in the water. I was jumped into the water. And my wife can't forgive me that I told him shoot. Shoot at us. 

And she jumped. She came back. She was already in the [INAUDIBLE] to-- to jump into the ditch. She just turned back and jumped on this tall fellow, trying to grab his rifle. He couldn't get rid of her. So he-- he was hitting her with the butt of the rifle, and then she was falling. He just put the rifle-- he-- he was-- put the rifle to her-- to her arm and point blank he shot her. 

She fell down. And she-- he was think-- he-- he thought certainly that she's dead. Anyhow, I didn't know anything. I heard a shot, but I was sure that at that time, my-- my wife was-- is already far away in the ditch. I was lying in the ditch for quite a time, maybe a quarter of an hour. Maybe half an hour. Quite a time. 

No, it was-- it was a quarter of an hour, until I heard one, two, three. I heard the shots in the forest. I knew that all the people are dead. 

And eventually my wife and myself, we were the only survivors. I went out. I started to shout for my wife. Nobody answered. I went back to Kolciak's house. And Kolciak, who was in-- in the yard, when he saw me, he thought that he is-- I am a ghost. And he told me, look, your wife came back. She is wounded, and bleeding, and I put her into another hiding place. 

Because he had another hiding place, where he had an officer there. We knew about this hiding place. And it was in-- in the-- in-- in the barn. You have to find out which-- which boards, timber boards, are loose and move them away. And there was underneath the straw in the barn, there was a hiding place. There was a hiding place supported with wood and so on and so on. 

I put her over there. I came back. I came back, and I could hear-- I could hear her-- her crying, whimpering. Were in-- in pain. I went-- I told her that I am back, and I came into-- I came there. And she told me next day-- because she fell asleep later. She fell asleep. But before she fell asleep, I came-- I went out to Kolciak. And he was-- he was on the floor-- he was out-- outside the-- the yard, on-- in the yard. 

And I told him, look, you watch out here. I am going to bring Valas in. I didn't trust him. So he said, no, you stay here. I'm going to-- I am going to bring Valas. I told him, you better do, but I will not be in there. I will not be in here. I will be watching you bringing Valas. 

He could see that I have no confidence in him, that I didn't want to go back into-- into the hiding place. And after a while-- after a while, I could see them both coming. Valas came back. And they brought sheets, and they bandaged the thing. I could see there was a hole-- a hole in-- in-- in the back as big as a fist. 

Because I didn't know it-- I didn't know it, but Valas, when he saw it, he said the bastard used a dumdum bullet. A dumdum bullet is exploding inside the body. It goes in and inside the body it's exploding. That's a dumdum bullet. You-- the bastard used a dumdum bullet. It's-- it's-- this is a-- this is a war crime. To use a dumdum bullet is a war-- is-- is a crime. 

Anyhow, we decided next day that they will bring-- that they will-- that he will send his brother-- Kolciak will send his son to bring a doctor. I wanted to bring him doctor-- doctor-- a friend of mine. I don't remember his name anymore at this-- no. I don't-- his name escape me at this very moment. 

A friend of mine. In the meantime, they brought in another doctor. But that's not important. What I'm trying to tell you is that when my wife came back and Kolciak said that he put her into the other-- into the other hiding place, that was a lie. She came back, and he just bodily threw her out from the house. 

And this was my wife bleeding. She remembered that there is a hiding place. There she found out-- she knocked each-- each board. She found out which board is loose, and she crawled in. She crawled in. He-- he threw her out. But when he saw me, that I am alive, and nothing happened to me, he got bloody scared. And he brought Valas. Next day he sent his son to bring a doctor. 

The doctor came, not-- I wrote a letter to-- I wrote a letter to Dr. Droba, what happened. In the meantime came Valas, and Valas gave me the names of all the people who were a unit. Because Valas just came in when they were there. He couldn't go back. He came in. So-- and he knew them. 

They are from a-- from a-- from a neighboring village. He knew all of them. He gave me a list of all the name. I sent the list to Droba. And I asked for the doctor. The doctor was a friend of mine. 

I was in correspondence with him until-- until last year when he died. And another doctor came, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. I never knew him. It was a-- a man from Silesia, who was thrown out by the Germans anyhow. And he was a new man, and he came back. At the same time, the son brought a bundle of notes, of bank notes, for my wife from-- from Droba, because they took away our money, so I asked him for money. He has-- he has our dollars, our currency, and so on. Anyhow, he sent us the money. 

And my-- he was-- that-- the doctor was examining my wife, and my wife was keeping in her hand this bundle of bank notes. When she asked him how much we owe-- owe for the visit, he said nothing. I didn't come here for money. But anyhow, after-- after she told him here she must pay for it, he told her, look. When I go seven kilometers out of town, it costs so much and so much. You pay me that. That was a few dollars. 

And so she-- she gave him the money. And the man, the son of Kolciak who came back after he-- after he returned-- after return to Mielec with the doctor, when he came back, he brought in bandages and all kind of-- of disinfectant which were available, quite a lot. I was sure that it was Droba who sent it. 

In the meantime, it happened that this doctor who took the money went to the chemist and bought this and certainly paid much more for bandages than she-- than he received it, and he sent it back. It shows you some-- some people-- some good people, some bad people. Matter of fact, he was awarded the-- post-mortem he was awarded by the [NON-ENGLISH]. 

And that was that-- that-- he said that nothing happened seriously. That the bullet didn't touch the lungs. It went from the corner, from the elbow here, slided under the skin, and-- and exploded, made a the hole-- this hole-- this hole. Whenever I look at this hole, I remember that guy. 

And that was that. I knew-- I knew all the people who-- who-- who did it. We-- next day we had to go away from-- we had to go away from-- from-- from this place. And we had to go away. And Valas found for us a place, again back to the Markowskis. 

And that was the 31st of May, the night of 31st of May. Went back to the Markowskis, their old place. A day later, or two days later, a bombshell. It look-- it happened that Valas and Kolciak were arrested by the gestapo. 

The question was who did it? And everybody in the family of the Markowskis knew who did it. Valas and Mrs. Markowski's sister, they had another sister, Mrs. Duskievici. And my auntie placed over there a lot of merchandise from their shop, with the Duskievici, for-- for after-- to-- to-- to-- to take-- to-- to-- to keep it for after the war. And among them there was that Persian lamb coat of my cousin Leah, was in their place. 

Now, Duskievici was going around to find out-- he was spying on Valas, that Valas may-- that Valas may be hiding the family which gave it-- which-- which deposited their goods with his mother. And then Valas came that night, that critical night, to Kolciak. He recognized his nephew on the road in the dark. 

He was-- that means that it was the nephew who tipped off the [NON-ENGLISH] people to come and liquidate-- not me. He was interested in the family Ostrow, in my-- in-- in my auntie and her-- and her two daughters, to-- to liquidate them so that they could inherit the goods and especially this-- this Persian lamb coat. 

Well, Valas gave me the list of all the names. And the-- and the suspicion of the family was that they came out and decided to let us live because I am a-- I was a friend of Vladislav Jasinszki of Jedrusie. And they believed it, that we went together to university, together to gymnasium, and-- and that-- that he nominated me [NON-ENGLISH], which means an officer, a no-commissioned officer [NON-ENGLISH]. So they-- they-- they decided that they are not going to shoot us. 

But it was on the instance of this Duskievici that they came back. And besides this, they-- he argued certainly that all-- after all they took away some jewelry. They took away my wedding ring. And I had another ring here. And on the-- that they-- they-- that in case we-- we survive, they will not be-- they-- they-- they will-- they will in-- in any case be responsible for-- for-- for-- for such an attack on us. 

Anyhow, they came back. That was-- the answer is that it was Duskievici who wanted Valas and Kolciak be liquidated so that nobody could point the finger that it was him, Duskievici, who brought-- who brought the [NON-ENGLISH] for the killing. 

INT: If I could just ask you to go back to the time the [NON-ENGLISH] came for you. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: And your cousins. What was the state of mind of the other people there? 

MV: Of which people? 

INT: The other people that were with you in hiding when the [NON-ENGLISH] came for them? 

MV: What was the other people afraid? 

INT: Were they very afraid? Or were they optimistic that they were going to the forest, somewhere else? When they came then? 

MV: No, no. They-- they didn't know. Maybe they were optimistic. You see, everybody-- everybody thinks that maybe-- as a matter of fact, before-- the moment I-- I-- the moment I saw that he took away his finger from the trigger, I shouted to them, run for your life! Run for your life! But they didn't. They felt somehow they were-- they felt somehow paralyzed. 

There were nine people in front of them with guns. I had only-- I was far away in the-- in the back because I slowed down. I was far away from the back, and there were-- I-- we-- we had two people in the-- and so when I shoved the other one into the ditch, there was only one. That's our different story. 

So they were such-- they were-- it's-- they-- they-- they were resigned. I think they were resigned. 

That was that. That was-- that way we came back, and that was the 31st of May, 1944, two months before we were liberated. 

INT: Then you were back at the Markowskis. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: And can you tell us, please, about that period? Tell us about the period at the Markowskis in the second half of 1944. 

MV: What was then? 

INT: Your period with the Markowskis in the second half of 1944. 

MV: The appearance? 

INT: Your period. Your time. 

MV: Ah, the-- the period. The time over there. They were very disturbed because Valas was arrested and Kolciak was arrested. So I was afraid that they may throw us out because-- but they said that Valas will never-- will never tell the gestapo that they are keeping us. And Kolciak didn't know where we went. 

So they were safe, and they-- they-- they tried their best to keep us-- to keep us alive, and to control us. They were very, very nice people. And they were very nice people. We can't complain. 

And that was until-- we were there until the Russians came very close. One day, they came along. Markowski came along and said, look, the Russians are in the village. There are few people, a few tanks. There were a few tanks. There was-- no, one-- one reconnaissance-- reconnaissance. The have one reconnaissance truck with Russians here. 

If we want, we can go out to them. I told him, look. I'm not going out now because they may go back. They're coming and going. I'm not sure about it. And then it-- as it happens that two days later, they-- they packed. They were in the middle of a barbecue. And one of the-- the truck, the reconnaissance truck, came back from-- from the-- the end of the village, and they said that the Germans are preparing a counter-offense, offensive. 

And they moved away very quick. They said to the people, don't worry. In two, three days we will be back. We can't move there today because we haven't got the bridges ready, and the-- the tanks can't go through. But we have got enough artillery to mince them. And the same-- that was midday when they went back. 

We started to think that maybe we should have gone out and go back with them. But it was too late. They went already. They left already. During the night we could see the German army moving, and too, we could hear them. We could hear them moving along the two roads [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] in the direction of-- to Sandomierz. To-- to the Vistula and the San coming together. 

And there-- a few hours there south of this-- south of the-- the battle. And we could hear-- we could hear the battle of tanks and artillery going on day after day. I mean, night-- day and night. On the third day, in the morning-- on the third day, in the morning, we could hear already the-- the rattle-- the rattle of machine guns. That means the Russians were very close. We could hear the-- the-- the battle was already with machine guns. 

And then something happened again. Something happened again. We were-- it was a beautiful day. There was never-- no more-- no more gestapo. No more Polish police. We were sitting on the loft, and suddenly-- suddenly I could hear a wiz-- a shell, an artillery shell coming close, coming close, one shell. 

My wife said, I'm going back to my-- to-- I'm going back to-- to the hiding place. The hiding place, you have to go back from the loft. There was a ladder, step-ladder. There was a ladder down. I'm going back. I said to her, look. You're not going back. And I said I'm going back. 

So she went back. She was halfway. The-- only the head was out. And I heard another whiff, another shell coming. And I felt it is coming like to-- to the south. I jumped and brought my wife out with all my strength from this ladder, back to the loft, because in case of a fire, it will be too difficult to go up from the hiding place on the ladder to the loft and then with the ladder down to the hole. So I pulled her back with all my strength. 

And when she was there, we could hear a-- we could hear the-- a-- a shell-- a shell exploding. The house was shaking. I looked over that way. I looked the direction of the-- of the hiding place, and I could see light. The hiding place was dark. I could feel as I came near there, that there's no more hiding place. The whole corner of the house is exploded. 

I looked down. I said there is no more hiding place. What happened was, that the neighbor-- that the neighbor-- our neighbor, the neighbor of Markowski, was a curious fellow. He wanted to watch the exchange of shells, of fire between the Germans and the Russians. The front was there. 

And the observer-- the German observer from the tower of the church of [NON-ENGLISH] spotted him on the tree. He was spotting-- he was sitting on a tree watching the shells. And he was sure that this must be a Russian directing the fire-- the Russian fire against them. So he pointed a couple of shells in this direction. The first shell fell short, but the second shell came just where the hiding place was. And everything in the hiding place was completely destroyed. 

Now we were without a hiding place anymore. Markowski, at that time, dug a new hiding place for a day or two, with-- with the Russians are already-- there-- you could hear the machine guns. The-- the-- he dug a grave in-- in the barn for the night, and covered it with-- with-- with timber, with boards, and straw and so on. They went with-- there we went for the night. 

And next day in the morning-- next day in the morning we went-- we were in the barn because there was no more anywhere else. There was no more hiding place, but we're in the barn. We-- we were out of the grave in the barn. And I could see the Russian soldiers coming from the field, from behind. From behind, they went out of the barn, the Russians there. We-- the only question they ask, are there Germans around here? Don't they know they went-- anyhow, within moments there were hundreds, thousands of them everywhere. 

I approached an officer. I told him who I am. And I told him if I can come home, Mielec. He said Mielec was liberated 12 hours ago. That another unit came from an another-- from the south and liberated Mielec before they came here. And then the afternoon, he said, you can go. When we look around, and-- and if there aren't Germans around here, you can go home. 

INT: What was the date? 

MV: Huh? 

INT: What was the date? 

MV: The 4th of August. Was the 4th of August in the afternoon. We went home. And we were not used anymore to walk. It was seven kilometers. And the walk was not so easy. Because when-- the walk was along the road. And next to the road there were, let's say, a couple of hundred-- a hundred yards, was the river embankment of the Wisloka. And there was the river. 

On the other side of the river were the Germans. And while we were walking, when-- while we were walking, shells started to come from the other side of Wisloka to this place, closer and closer to the road. And so we went down from the road farther away, but then the shells came over there to this place. So then we went back. We said, look, we-- certainly the other place, the-- the Germans are trying to destroy the road-- the-- the road, and they are trying to find the road. So we can't go back there. 

And so we were walking for quite a time. And some houses were burnt out on the way, and so on. And until my wife couldn't walk anymore. I had to help her. I had to. Because we were not used to walking after two-- two kilometers or so. And then suddenly a small vehicle, like a cart, like a box-- a box-- a cart filled with-- with hay-- with hay. 

And was a one-- a one-horse cart. And a-- a soldier, a [NON-ENGLISH] soldier, was sitting-- an Asian-- was sitting on top over there. And I just-- riding this-- this cart slowly to the-- in our direction to the town. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: Mark Verstandig. So, you saw this cart approaching-- 

MV: This cart approaching, so they stopped. They said, look what's happened to you-- you are-- you are sick. He said to-- to Frieda, you can't walk. And I told him who we are, and he jumped down, lifted my wife to this place where he was sitting. And he was walking with me. 

And so we were walking until we came to the city, to Mielec, about another 3, or 4, maybe 4 kilometers, or 5 kilometers. We were in the city. When we were already on [NON-ENGLISH] street, which means next to the main square, I thanked him very much, and my wife came down, and we went to our house. And the man, the first man who recognized me was Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], the clerk of our office-- of our-- of our law office. 

He looked at us, and his first-- and-- his-- his first reaction was, [NON-ENGLISH]. You are still alive? He was disappointed, we are still alive. Now, that was that. 

We went-- we went home to our house, and there was a family over from-- which you-- which we had to-- they were our tenants, who had this restaurant there. And the first floor where the Judenrat was-- was now occupied by her. Was occupied by the Germans, but when the Germans left, she moved in. When-- the moment we came, she opened the door, well-- well, that's-- --that's yours. 

That's yours. It was-- was our-- our furniture was there. No, we, we came, we came home. A few hours later, a couple of hours later, I went down. I went down to have a look what's going on. In this restaurant where we're at, were dozens and dozens of soldiers-- in the restaurant, in the pub-- dozens of soldiers. 

When I was standing there at the entrance, a man approached me. I knew his face. I didn't know his name, a young man. He said, look, I knew, I heard that you are back. Tell me what is your opinion of the situation. 

I immediately recognized that he is a-- that the AK knows that we are back, and they want to know what's going on-- what, in my opinion. I said, look, why should I tell you my-- my opinion. 

You ask the brother of Dr. Droba, Jozef Droba-- but he got there Jozef Droba. He is a friend of mine. We had discussions before. He used to come to the office, and we had discussions on the situation. 

He knows what-- he, he, he knows my-- he knows what, what I am standing for. They were very indecisive, so I told him that I was a friend of Jasinszki. Jasinszki took-- Jasinszki was the man who gave me his-- his-- his Aryan papers, and he had a safe house for my wife, and so on and so on. He was very pleased. He was pleased with his mission and went away. 

I immediately recognized that the AK sent him out. And I, I thought that I will give him the right address, which will ensure him about my loyalty, because I didn't know at that time what I knew later-- that it was Brigadier Droba, the Jozef Droba, the brother of Dr. Antoni Droba, who was the commander in chief of the NSZ for the District of Cracow. 

But I am sure that he was a figurehead. They took a figurehead, because he was, before the war, a professional officer. And he was a professor, a teacher at the Academy of the-- the Military Academy. 

So he was the right man, but I gave him the right man to, to, to express an opinion about me. And that was that. A couple of days later, I went to Droba, and then we met there. I could see that Droba was embarrassed with our coming to see him a few times, more than, within these two days. 

Somehow with him, I could feel that he is a little bit embarrassed, although we made out all kinds of reconciliation of the accounts, because he told us, he gave us back whatever was left, and so on. 

About two, three, maybe four days later, I suddenly, we suddenly saw that people are winding up the telephone lines. And we were very, very, very worried. 

So I came to an officer. My Russian is very, was very slow, was very limited. I came to an officer, and I asked him-- look, what, what's going on? Are-- are-- are you withdrawing, are you, are you going back? 

He said, we are going back. I was very scared that the Germans may come back. I met another officer, and I told him-- look, we want to know what's going on, because I'm the only Jew here with my wife. In case the Germans go, come in, I have to run away. And he told me they are going back. 

I came to Droba. And there was news of Droba the Colonel and the Brigadier and the other. I told her-- look what's going on? I could see they are winding up the telephones, and the people told me that they are going back. 

He said, yes, I heard it, too, that they are going back. The Germans may come back. On the way, Droba told me, the Jozef Droba, they said, you run for your life to a town, [NON-ENGLISH]. 

I told him, but why? And I asked the other officer, and he told me, they are going back. So I told her, you must have misunderstood him. I, in your place, wouldn't fight. 

And the same afternoon, and a quarter of an hour later, I was already on my way in the direction of town [NON-ENGLISH], another main road. We went over there. We went, we marched 7 kilometers, this time. 

And my wife said, she can't do it anymore. She rubbed off the back of her foot. It's hurting, she can't go anymore. We sat down. When we sat down, and I could see behind-- there was a house deep in-- about 200 yards from the road, and there was a Russian truck over there. 

But it looked that the house is empty. And at that time, two people, two young people rode by on bikes. 100 yards from us, they stopped, and both of them bent down to repair their bikes. 

They made it look that they are repairing their bikes. And I told my wife, look, we are in danger. These are Jedrus' people-- AK. They have got guns behind the belt. 

Suddenly both bikes need repair. They bent down. This is what, what, what Jedrus told me how they are doing it. They are waiting-- that's an ambush. They are waiting that we could come closer. 

And I am going to this road to this house, where there is a Russian truck outside there, although it looks empty. And I jump down to go to this place, and suddenly I saw from afar, an ambulance coming. 

So I jumped back to the middle of the road. I didn't allow it to go. Put my hands up-- the ambulance stopped. I told them my wife is injured. She needs help. 

And one of the officers jumped down and took my wife up. And I went up, and I could see-- I've showed to-- I showed to my wife-- look, the two bikes doesn't, do not need anymore, anymore repairs. They just disappeared. 

In the front of this, in the front of-- but they were going just the opposite direction to Mielec, where we ran away from Mielec. So I told them, look, we are going in the wrong direction. The Germans are coming back to Mielec, and the Russians are leaving Mielec. 

So they came and told what I have told them to the man sitting next to the driver-- without any signs, without any officers. I thought he is a sergeant or something like that-- an elderly fellow, middle-aged fellow. 

A few-- but they didn't say, they-- they-- they went further, went a kilometer or so, and the-- and the-- and the-- two Russians came on a motorbike, on a motorbike from Mielec, that way. They stopped them, and they asked them. 

And then they told us, no-- the telephone unit is going back, and another is coming in. They stay there. Don't worry, we are going, we are going to Mielec. We have to go to Mielec to the-- to the hospital. 

So we went to Mielec back. We ran away to meet a rendezvous with these two. We-- we nearly were killed. And we went back to the, to the hospital, outside, to the hospital outside the town. 

That is then-- then-- the COP, that's the [NON-ENGLISH]. There were some large-- they changed a large building into a hospital. Now, we went over there. They went down. A few minutes later, they came to invite us inside to the-- and they gave us all facilities to wash, and they invited us to have dinner, beautiful dinner. 

And then an officer came, and they brought us blankets and everything. You'll be sleeping in the ambulance, and next day, we will take you to Kolbuszowa, far away from the front. 

About midnight, midnight-- or more past midnight-- we couldn't sleep. We were sitting, we were sitting in, in this ambulance and thinking what, what, what happened. Maybe, we maybe, we are in paradise. Maybe they have, they have shot us, and-- and we are no more alive, that's all a dream. 

And my wife asked me, why didn't they come along to shoot us? I said, look-- because a revolver, a handgun is a nuisance. If you stand, inviting-- two, two, three foot away, two, three feet away, they will shoot you. And this is what they were waiting for. 

But if we were, if they realized that we are suspicious, if the moment they would come closer, we would start running. And when we start running, a handgun is not worth a penny. 

In the meantime, a Russian car can come from that side or from that side. They will be in bigger trouble. They are, they are not such, such big heroes. And a handgun is not good for those things. 

And they aren't good when you are standing against a handgun. Anyhow, this man, who was sitting next to the driver, came out in a white coat, and he introduced himself. 

He said, my name is [NON-ENGLISH]. I am Major General [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm the head of this medical unit for the whole army, thousand army, here. I'm a professor, a surgeon, a professor at the Moscow University. 

And I'm Jewish, we can write in Yiddish. And tell me your story-- what, what was done. I told him-- look, It was the first time I spoke to a Jew during the war. I was-- it was the first time I've spoken to-- I spoke to him in Yiddish. 

He finished. He said that he is performing, they are performing operations. And another fellow came in, a Captain. And so the whole, the whole, the whole ambulance was-- they all came out to hear the story. 

Next day, they took us to Kolbuszowa. And in Kolbuszowa, we were sitting outside where they told us to wait in the ambulance. A minute later, a few minutes later, a captain came along and, and a few minutes-- maybe a few, maybe, maybe a half an hour later, or something-- they came, there must have been a bit of time, they came back. 

They, they invited us inside. And inside, there was this man, [NON-ENGLISH], in this whole uniform, everything. And he introduced us to the man in charge of this hospital, a colonel. 

And he told them, and the colonel told us that we will be the guests of his in his hospital until we are ready to go, until we are, we are ready to go. And that was that. They found us a place, they found us a place. 

And the first night, we were in his, in the surgery inside. And then they found us a place where to, where to live in Kolbuszowa. And there, we met our friends, the Hoenigs. 

INT: And what happened to your wife's injury? 

MV: To what? 

INT: Your wife's bullet wounds. 

MV: Oh to the-- we went to [NON-ENGLISH]. From there, we found our friends from Mielec, who came to, who were in Kolbuszowa. And then we decided to go by truck, by a Russian truck to [NON-ENGLISH] in, in the belief that there will be more Jewish people in [NON-ENGLISH], where we will be able somehow to see what's going on. 

Now, we arrived in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was evening. And we went from house to house, seeking shelter-- knock the door. We are refugees. We want shelter. They recognized us as Jews, because they recognize my wife and the other people were-- a group of people, maybe ten people-- no shelter. 

In the end, in the end, we found a house. We didn't know what's going on. We found a house, they took us in. It was a Mrs. Schreiber from England. She lives now in, in New York. She looked 100% Polish, Aryan. 

I was standing with her in the front. And we looked shelter. We came from the front. So they thought that we are Poles. And they invited us and made us a nice, nice supper. 

And then in the middle of the supper, they realize that we are Jewish, because there were a few other people-- my wife was there. And the lady, the lady came in and said, look, do me a favor, I don't want my neighbors to know that we gave shelter to Jews. 

Before dawn, please leave this place that nobody could see and go to this and that place, that and that street. She told us where. There is a house with, which are occupied already by some of the Jews who came back from the forest. 

The whole house is empty and occupied by them. You go there. And we didn't realize what's going on. But anyhow, before dawn, we went out. We went out, but we realized at that time that the Poles, the Polish family was more scared that the neighboring Poles shouldn't know that they gave shelter to Jewish refugees. 

They were scared for the-- they were, they were scared of their neighbors more than they were scared of the Germans. And we went there, and we went to, we went to a doctor, a surgeon. 

And as a matter of fact, the surgeon made, made an X-ray, which is today an antique, because they say, I would like you to photograph this X-ray. Today, you have got an X-ray on a negative. There, they made an X-ray, a negative, and from the negative, they made the positive. So you could see the X-ray on a positive. 

And the X-ray showed this dumdum bullet inside, and it was bleeding, and it was infected. In fact, it had to be taken out. But he said, look, I can't do it. I can't do it. I am busy every day, the whole day, performing operations in the military hospital. 

The only thing I can do-- I can take your wife one day-- he said, I'll let you know which day. And I will, between one operation and another, I will remove this, I will remove this bullet. 

And this is what happened. As a matter of fact, I wasn't there at that particular day when the messenger came that we should come to the hospital. So a friend of mine and Mrs. Schreiber, they-- and a certain Mr. Schwartz and Mrs. Schreiber took my wife to the hospital. 

And there, she was sitting. And you could see-- she, they told me, they could see people moving in and out-- dead people-- and performing operations while you were sitting outside. 

And then she-- a nurse came in and said, it's your turn, come in. They put her on the table, and there was no anesthetic. The only anesthetic was there-- this-- chlo-- is it called-- 

INT: Chloroform. 

MV: Chloroform. Chloroform anesthetic-- put the chloroform anesthetic. A minute later, they operated, took out the bullet and bandaged it. And out to the porch. 

And they told her, when she was still half asleep-- they said, out to the porch. After a quarter of an hour, he told them-- Mr. Schwartz and Schreiber, take the lady home. 

And if, you know, can't walk, so take a cab and, and take her away from here, because it's not even healthy to sit here. And this is how the bullet was taken out. 

INT: And this was about September, '44. 

MV: No, that was still August. That was still August. It, there is a date, by the way, on this, on this X-ray. You'll see the date on the X-ray, exactly when it was. 

INT: So what happened to the-- to you and your wife the remainder of 1944? 

MV: Yes, the remainder. We came back to Mielec. But at that time-- you see, Valas, in the meantime-- by the way, on the day when we ran-- on the day, on the 4th of August, when we were liberated, Valas was back, because the Germans evacuated the prison in Rzeszow. 

And the people had the possibility of just running away. And Valas went together with Kolciak. And Valas told him-- look, I think we have, we have got all possibilities just to move out, to run away, to disappear. 

There were no, they were, they weren't even, they weren't even watched properly. But Kolciak didn't want. Kolciak was scared. Kolciak was scared that I will take him, I will make him responsible for the deaths of the families, there. 

So Kolciak didn't want to come back, and Kolciak disappeared. Some people say that he died. Some people say that he disappeared among the refugees, amongst the millions of refugees in the western world. 

So Valas came along, Valas came back. Now, when we came back to Mielec from Rzeszow, Valas came along and said-- look, your life is in danger, that what happened-- they're letting people, the unit of AK, who performed, who, who murdered your family and who came over there, they established themselves as the militia of the new communist government. 

And they already know that you are alive, and you are the last witness of their crime. Therefore, you have to run for your life. I immediately took a train to Lublin, to Lublin. And my wife still was there in Mielec. 

And I went to see Dr. Sommerstein. Dr. Sommerstein was a Zionist, and he was one of the top leaders of the, of the Polish Jewry before the war. He was a member of the, he was a member of the Parliament, of the Polish Parliament on behalf of the Jews. 

And when the new government came in, the communist-dominated government came in, he was the Minister for Repatriation. They made him Minister on orders of Stalin. They made a Zionist the Minister of the-- in order to show to the world that it's not so bad, because there is a Zionist in the government. 

So I went to see him. And I can remember coming, him making an appointment with his daughter to come again. And I told him the story-- that they are there, and so on. 

They said-- look, tell me the whole story. But I felt like-- I know that you are a very busy man. He said, I want to listen to you the whole story. Anyhow, I told him the whole story. I was sitting there maybe 1 and 1/2 hours or 2 hours. 

And then he said to me, the only way of saving you is to get you a job here in Lublin in a house which you will be, which you will be guarded day and night. 

He told me to come later in the night, later in the day. And he calculated on the day that he consulted-- what is the name, I don't remember if this is the name-- it disappeared again. 

He was a son-in-law of Dr. Landau, a famous lawyer. And he is now a famous lawyer in the, in the Ministry of Justice. Anyhow, his name was Reisner original name. But in Poland, he used the name-- at that time, he used another name. 

INT: Varshovski. 

MV: Huh? 

INT: Varshovski. 

MV: No, Lublein. 

INT: Varshovski was his name. 

MV: No, not Varshovski, not Varshovski. I'll tell you the name in a minute. Now, anyhow, I came. He said-- look, there is one position free, and that is-- you-- that is a judicial law counselor, a law counselor, a legal advisor in drafting laws. 

Each ministry has got a legal advisor. When a statute is being drafted-- this means it's going from one minister to the other for a legal opinion. And there, there are special-- the lawyer, the specialist is giving his approval or, or he's making correction of, of this draft. 

This is the position you will get, and that this is the Ministry, in the Ministry of Security. And for that, and because of that, you will get a flat, which will be guarded day and night. 

And don't worry, he told me, it is already the local, the local, the Minister, the local security. And Mielec was already informed to guard your wife day and night until she is coming, until she will come to Lublin. 

And that was how it was. You see, she was guarded. I, I came back. I came back to Mielec, and I took my wife to Lublin. And that is how I, that is how I started. I started as a legal advisor in charge of drafting laws. As an advisor-- giving opinion on drafted laws. 

INT: Was this already '45? 

MV: No, it was still-- it was still '44. It was still '44. And in '45, in December, in December of '44, the offensive started to the, the last push, the last push to Berlin. 

The offensive started from Warsaw. And it was Lublin, which, which occupied Warsaw. And from there, the offensive went out to Lublin. To-- to-- the offensive went to Berlin. 

As a matter of fact, I remember that I got a commission. I was called to the Premier's office of Osobka-Morawski. He was, he was the Prime Minister. He was a Socialist-- PPS. 

I was called, they told me-- look, we decided that you are the man who will go with the army to ensure that the administration, that, that the new administration should take over, that the life should become normal when the Red Army-- when the army goes in. 

So I got a car with the chauffeur. And [NON-ENGLISH], the famous actor-- his wife was working in the radio-- Lillian. I don't remember the name anymore. 

Anyhow, [NON-ENGLISH] came to me, that he has got-- he must go with me, because behind him, somewhere in Warsaw or outside Warsaw, his seven-year-old daughter is hidden, is in hiding over there. I had to find her. 

So here, I took-- I gave him my military coat. I myself got a military coat, because I went with the army. I had a military coat, and we went, we went to Warsaw. The next day, he said, look, you will be behind the front. 

[INAUDIBLE], you shouldn't be worried, you will not be in the front. But next day, when after the bridge was created over the Vistula, in December, we saw that the Vistula-- we went, we went, we went along, and I could see, really, I could see trucks coming up on mines, exploding on mines. 

And that they called you will be behind, you will be behind the front. Eight hours, it took eight hours until we come to the center of Ostrow from the bridge, from the bridge. 

Anyhow, from there we went to Bydgoszcz. We went in the-- the-- that was the first town was Bydgoszcz, where I came along, and people told me that the, that an Endek, an antisemite, an Endek became, established himself as the mayor of the city. 

When I had-- this, this is what I was told by a PPS man, by a Socialist, a Social Democrat-- came to me. Because my, my job was that the life should be normal. So I, I came, I went immediately to the office of the mayor, I told him-- look, pack your things now and go. 

This man is going to be the mayor. And I put a Socialist, a Social Democrat in his place. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.

INT: Tape 21 of Mark Verstandig. We are talking now about the period, end of '44, beginning 1945, where you were in the army, and the places that you were stationed in. Were the Germans completely-- were these areas free of Germans by now? 

MV: When? 

INT: Were these areas in Poland free of Germans? 

MV: Yeah. The areas were free of Germans. We went to Bydgoszcz. And the Germans were outside Bydgoszcz. But we were in Bydgoszcz already, and Bydgoszcz was free. And I threw out this mayor, who was a well-known antisemite and an ND, belongs to the-- to this national party, to the antisemitic party, and then installed a socialist-- a social Democrat one, PPS. 

And next day-- ah, that was the same day, I think. The same night at dinner, there were-- that-- we had dinner in a hall. In the school hall was a restaurant-- was a canteen for-- for the officers of-- for officers of the army, of the Polish army. And I was sitting there by myself, and I saw next to me-- I saw it was a table. And Major [POLISH], he was a political commissar. He was a major. He was later Minister for Interior in Poland and of-- and-- before he defected. 

He was a well-known communist. And I could see that he is sitting there with some youngsters, and the discussion was with them to organize the-- a kind of a Komsomol, a kind of a communist-- of-- of-- of a communist youth organization. And the youngsters told him that before the war they were scouts. And they were connected with the church, and so on and so on and so on. 

In the end, I don't think that they went-- I could hear because-- what they were talking about, it was next table. And they went away and this Mr.-- the Major [POLISH] took them to-- showed them to the door and came back with a bottle of vodka, with two glasses, and sat next to me. And told me-- and now he sat next to me. 

I told him, look, what are you talking about with those youngsters? You could see they are NDs. They were connected-- they are scouts connected with the church, and you wanted to make out of them-- to make out the communist, young communists, a Komsomol. You are wasting your time. 

So he gave me a-- he gave me a political lesson. Look, we will be communists. We'll come to an agreement, and to come close, rather with the NDs than with the Szalasia. The Pilsudski Szalasia were close to the Germans, but [POLISH] was always pro-Russian. I told him, what are you talking nonsense? [POLISH] was pro-Russian when there was a-- a reactionary Russian regime of the Czar. But not now. Now they are communists in Russia. 

I said, look, in political language, it doesn't matter. If one is poor Russian, the change of regime doesn't matter. You-- you will see that we will make an agreement. We'll come to close rather with the NDs than with the Pilsudski people. All right. 

And by the way, he told me. I wanted to tell you something. You threw out the major-- the-- the mayor, the ND mayor, and installed a PPS man, a Social Democrat one as mayor. May I tell you? I threw out your mayor, your socialist mayor, and reinstalled the ND. 

And I said, why did you do it? He said, look, I told you why before. And if you want to know, I will tell you something. He says your duty here to see that the bakeries should bake bread. He says your duty here to see that the schools reopen. And all other things, which are very important, that the people should accept in the meantime, the Polish money, the old money. That is your duty. And don't mix in politics. Don't-- don't do anything. 

For politics, that's me. And your duty-- your duty is to see that bread should be baked and people should accept the money, and schools. I got the lesson. I got the lesson, and-- and that was that. 

By the way, I remember the name I mentioned to you-- I mentioned a name before of this man in-- in the Justice Department, Dr. Sommerstein tried to-- through him to get me a job. That was Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], a famous-- a famous lawyer. He was later the-- the representative of Poland in the Nuremberg-- Nuremberg trials. His name, real name, was [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and he was Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. 

And the name of the doctor, which I wanted to come to help my wife, a friend of mine in gymnasium, was [NON-ENGLISH]. As a matter of fact, I read later that he himself was going around helping Jewish people in-- in-- in hiding. But they sent me a-- a stranger to my wife, and the stranger was such a one that I would have never believed that he will stretch out his hand for help, and-- though that's a mixture of people. 

INT: At that time, the beginning of 1945-- 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: --in your job of looking after the administration of the various places, what sort of picture of life did you see? 

MV: [SIGHS] The same was everywhere. As far as the Jews are concerned, there were not-- there were not many Jews over there. But there were Jews in Warsaw. So I traveled through Warsaw when I went back from Bydgoszcz. 

So the situation was such that there were many people who helped Jews. 

Jews paid them. They were paid. There were many of them. There was a minority-- a minority, which was spying around-- spying around and-- and delivering Jews to the gestapo. I saw it myself while walking the streets of Warsaw. I mentioned, I think, maybe in the-- my-- before in one of the tapes. I mentioned it, that I saw this [NON-ENGLISH], where there were professional-- there are swarms of them, professional people whose-- whose business, whose industry, was to just smell out, to recognize a Jew, and take from the Jew whatever he possesses. 

If he has a ring, if he has gold, if he has dollars, and-- they were-- they-- they were taking away. If you-- if-- and they were letting you go for the time being.
